Owner of Detour Lake (Canada's largest gold mine) and Canadian Malartic (Canada's largest open-pit mine). In Q1 2026 it sold each ounce for $4,861 and it cost $1,483 to produce — a 70% margin, generating $732M in free cash for the quarter. CEO Ammar Al-Joundi since March 2022. Chair Sean Boyd (former CEO 1998–2022).
AEM.summary_what:
Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE: AEM, TSX: AEM) is a Canadian gold miner founded in 1957. Headquartered in Toronto. **Top 3 gold producer in the world** by annual ounces (~3.45 million oz FY2025), competing with Newmont (#1, ~6 million oz) and Barrick (#2, ~4 million oz).

**What sets it apart**: 100% of its production comes from **tier-1 jurisdictions** — Canada (the majority), Finland, Australia, Mexico. Zero exposure to unstable governments (no mines in sub-Saharan Africa, Russia, or South America outside Mexico). This reduces geopolitical risk compared to peers.

**Operating mines** (2026):
- **LaRonde** (Quebec, Canada) — 70+ year history, flagship mine
- **Detour Lake** (Ontario) — Canada's largest gold mine, ramping toward ~1 million oz/year
- **Canadian Malartic** (Quebec) — Canada's largest open-pit mine; East Gouldie underground started production March 2026
- **Meadowbank + Meliadine** (Nunavut, Canadian Arctic)
- **Macassa** (Ontario, former Kirkland Lake)
- **Kittila** (Finland) — the largest gold mine in the European Union
- **Fosterville** (Victoria, Australia, former Kirkland Lake)
- **Pinos Altos + La India** (Chihuahua, Mexico)

**Development pipeline**:
- **Hope Bay** (Nunavut) — green-lit in 2026, capex ~$2.4B, target 400–425k oz/year
- Upper Beaver, Wasamac, San Nicolas in development

**Leadership**:
- **CEO: Ammar Al-Joundi** (President & CEO since March 2022; previously President since 2015)
- **Executive Chair: Sean Boyd** — legendary former CEO 1998–2022 (24 years at the helm)
- Fitch upgraded balance sheet to **A-** in 2026 (one of the strongest in the sector)

**M&A**: disciplined style — Kirkland Lake 2022 (all-stock deal), Yamana JV 2023 (100% Canadian Malartic).
AEM.summary_why: AEM at $176.60 means buying the senior gold producer with the best jurisdictional profile + one of the lowest production costs in the sector + a net-cash balance sheet, right when gold is trading around $4,500–4,700/oz (the 2026 all-time high was above $5,500) and profit margins are extreme. Specific reasons: (1) **Crushing per-ounce profit in Q1 2026**: sold at $4,861/oz, cost $1,483 to produce → **$3,378 clear profit per ounce = 70% margin**. Multiplied by 825,109 oz produced in Q1 = $732M in free cash in just 3 months. (2) **Record FY2025 production of 3.45 million oz** + record operating cash of $6.8B + record free cash of $4.4B. If production costs hold to FY2026 guidance of $1,400–1,550/oz and gold averages $4,000+/oz, AEM generates another record year. (3) **Balance sheet: net cash $2.9B** (vs. net debt before 2023). Long-term debt just $197M. Fitch upgraded to A-. AEM no longer needs to pay down debt — all extra cash goes to dividends + buybacks. (4) **Accelerating cash returns to shareholders**: dividend +12.5% in March 2026 (to $0.45/quarter = $1.80/year = $900M annualized) + **share buyback program up to $2B renewed May 2026** (~5% of the float, prior tranche bought at an average of $207). (5) **Hope Bay development green-lit, ~$2.4B capex** → stepping toward 4+ million oz annually in the early 2030s. (6) **Reserves +2% year over year to 55.4 million oz**, indicated resources +10%, inferred +15% — solid reserve replacement. (7) **Stock -31% off the all-time high of $255 at $175** — the pullback offers an entry with margin vs. the peak. Average analyst price target ~$231 = +32% upside. (8) **Structural gold tailwind**: central bank buying ~755 tons/year (~2x pre-2022 levels), BRICS de-dollarization, sticky inflation, geopolitical tension (Ukraine, Middle East). If the Fed stays dovish or the USD weakens, gold goes higher.
AEM.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Gold price reversal** — the stock already fell -31% from its all-time high ($255 → $175) when gold cooled from $5,500 to $4,500. If the Fed surprises with rate hikes or a ceasefire is signed in the Middle East/Ukraine, gold could break below $4,000 — AEM equity with its operating leverage would fall even harder. (2) **Operational disruptions at flagship mines**: extreme winters at Meadowbank/Meliadine (Nunavut Arctic) have caused production misses in past years. Indigenous consultation at Hope Bay could delay capex. The East Gouldie underground ramp at Canadian Malartic carries execution risk. (3) **Cost inflation**: labor (Quebec unions), diesel, cyanide, electricity. FY2025 production costs overshot guidance by $42/oz due to a royalty drag (because gold averaged $3,453 vs. the $2,500 plan = more royalties paid). FY2026 guidance already assumes higher costs. (4) **Quebec concentration**: ~50% of production is from Quebec/Ontario. Any tax or regulatory change (royalties, electricity rates) hits margins. (5) **Ammar Al-Joundi still proving himself**: only 4 years as CEO. Sean Boyd was a legend; the market is still judging whether Ammar will maintain the same M&A discipline. (6) **Dividend yield of 1.03% is low**: if gold falls and capital appreciation evaporates, the yield doesn't compensate.

# Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) — Full Research

## Price: $176.60 | Price vs. profit (TTM): 16.47 | Forward P/E: 12.06 | Div Yield: 1.03% | Market Cap: $87.5B | EPS: $10.62

---

## What is Agnico Eagle?

Canadian gold miner founded in 1957. Headquartered in Toronto. **Top 3 gold producer in the world** (~3.45 million oz FY2025) with **100% of production in tier-1 jurisdictions** (Canada, Finland, Australia, Mexico).

## Operating Mines (2026)

| Mine | Country | Notes |
|------|------|-------|
| **LaRonde** | Quebec | Flagship, 70+ year history |
| **Detour Lake** | Ontario | Canada's #1 gold mine, ramping to 1M oz/year |
| **Canadian Malartic** | Quebec | Canada's #1 open-pit; East Gouldie underground in production March 2026 |
| Meadowbank | Nunavut | Canadian Arctic |
| Meliadine | Nunavut | Canadian Arctic |
| Macassa | Ontario | Former Kirkland Lake (deal 2022) |
| **Kittila** | Finland | #1 gold mine in the EU |
| Fosterville | Victoria, Australia | Former Kirkland Lake |
| Pinos Altos | Chihuahua, Mexico | — |
| La India | Chihuahua, Mexico | — |

## Development Pipeline

- **Hope Bay** (Nunavut) — green-lit 2026, capex ~$2.4B, target 400–425k oz/year
- Upper Beaver, Wasamac, San Nicolas

## Q1 2026 (reported May 1, 2026)

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **Production** | **825,109 oz** | |
| **All-in production cost** | **$1,483/oz** | Cash cost $1,093/oz |
| **Realized gold price** | **$4,861/oz** | +68% year over year — record |
| **Net income** | **$1,695M** | $3.39/share |
| **Adjusted EPS** | **$3.41** | |
| **Free cash** | **$732.1M** | vs. $594M Q1 2025 |
| Net cash | **$2,915M** | Cash $3.1B vs. long-term debt of just $197M |
| Shareholder returns | ~$375M | the quarter |

## FY2025

- **Production: 3,447,367 oz** (above the midpoint of guidance)
- **All-in production cost: $1,339/oz** (cash cost $979/oz)
- **Operating cash: $6,817M** (record, $13.58/share)
- **Free cash: $4,399M** (record, $8.76/share)
- Total shareholder returns: **$1.4B**
- Dividend +12.5%

## Gold Context (May 2026)

- Gold all-time high 2026 > **$5,500/oz**
- Intraday $4,689 (Jan 19, 2026)
- Mid-May trading: $4,500–4,740
- Drivers: central bank buying ~755 tons/year (~2x pre-2022), BRICS de-dollarization, sticky inflation, Iran/Ukraine tensions
- Reserves +2% year over year to **55.4 million oz**

## Cash Returns to Shareholders

- **Dividend $0.45/quarter = $1.80/year** (+12.5% in March 2026)
- ~$900M annualized in dividends
- **Share buyback program up to $2B renewed May 2026** (~5% of the float)
- Prior tranche bought at an average of $207.68
- Net cash $2.9B → pure return-of-capital mode

## Leadership

- **CEO**: **Ammar Al-Joundi** (President & CEO since March 2022; President since 2015)
- **Executive Chair**: **Sean Boyd** (legendary former CEO 1998–2022, 24 years)
- Fitch credit rating: **A-** (among the strongest in the mining sector)

## Anchor Fact

**In Q1 2026, Agnico sold each ounce of gold for $4,861 and it cost $1,483 to mine it. That is $3,378 of clear profit per ounce — a 70% margin. For every ounce they pulled out of the ground, they kept $3,378. Times 825,000 ounces in 3 months = $732M of free cash in a single quarter. Three years ago gold was at $1,800 and production costs were about the same. The price went up; the costs barely moved.**

## Top 3 Risks

1. **Gold price reversal** — stock already fell -31% from all-time high $255 when gold dropped from $5,500 to $4,500. If rates rise or the USD strengthens, gold falls.
2. **Operational disruptions** at Nunavut/Quebec flagship mines.
3. **Cost inflation** + royalty drag (FY2025 production costs overshot by $42/oz due to high royalties when gold exceeded $3,400).

## Analyst Consensus

- Rating: **Buy** (10 Buy / 3 Hold / 1 Sell out of 14 analysts)
- Average target: **~$231** (median ~$205)
- Range: $80–$256
- Upside from $176.60: **~+30.8%**

## The Thesis in One Line

> Buy the senior gold producer with a tier-1 jurisdictional profile, one of the lowest production costs in the industry, $2.9B in net cash, a $2B active buyback program, and a 70% profit margin per ounce in Q1 2026 — right when the stock is -31% off its all-time high and gold remains around $4,500 with structural tailwinds from central bank buying and de-dollarization.

---

*Research date: 27 May 2026 | Next review: Nov 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
AEM.one_liner: Agnico Eagle is one of the world's largest gold miners — it digs in Canada, Finland and Australia, sells gold at record prices, and keeps 70 cents of profit for every dollar it earns.
AEM.why_short: It costs Agnico $1,483 to mine an ounce of gold that it then sells for $4,861 — a 70% profit margin that generated $732M in free cash in just one quarter. It has no debt and is returning cash to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.
AEM.risk_short: If gold prices fall sharply — for example if interest rates rise or a major conflict ends — Agnico's stock will drop hard. It also operates in the remote Canadian Arctic, where harsh winters have disrupted production before.

AMKBY.summary_short: ADR OTC of the Danish shipping giant (1 ADR = 1/200 of the B share in Copenhagen). FY2025 ended with EBIT $3.5B (top of guidance) and 2026 guidance EBIT -$1.5B to +$1.0B — the range reflects the binary bet on the Suez return. Houthis attacked again Feb-Mar 2026 → "normalization" is stalling, which protects rates better than guidance assumes. SCFI +39% YoY as of April 27. Trades at P/B 0.61 — only major shipping company below book value. Hapag-Lloyd bought ZIM at 1.06x P/B = private market values these assets above book even at cycle bottom. Dividend DKK 480 ($0.37/ADR, 3.23%) + $1B buyback = $2.1B return 2026.
AMKBY.summary_what: A.P. Møller-Mærsk A/S is the world's #2 container shipping company (behind MSC) and the largest logistics integrator globally. Founded in 1904 by A.P. Møller and Peter Mærsk-Møller in Svendborg, Denmark. HQ in Copenhagen. ~95,000 employees, 130 countries. AMKBY is the ADR OTC in the USA (tier OTCPK). **Structure: 1 AMKBY ADR = 1/200 of a B share (MAERSK-B.CO) in Copenhagen.** Trades in USD ($11.51 on April 27, 2026), but reports business in native USD (not DKK). Three operating segments: (1) **Ocean (~65% revenue, $35B in 2025)**: 726 ships with 4.5M TEU capacity (14% global market share). The most cyclical segment — half of the cycle bottom 2026 is here. FY2025 EBIT fell -71% to $1.4B; (2) **Logistics & Services (~28%, $15.1B in 2025)**: forwarding (air + LCL + project cargo), landside (warehousing + e-commerce delivery + customs), solutions (digital platforms). EBIT +36% to $729M in 2025 — a segment that didn't exist materially five years ago; (3) **Terminals (~10%, $5.3B in 2025)**: APM Terminals — 65 global terminals. The portfolio jewel: utilization 88%, ROIC 16.1%, EBIT margin >30%, +20% revenue YoY. Towage & misc (Svitzer) represents the residual. **CEO since January 2023: Vincent Clerc** — Danish, with Mærsk since 1998. Led Ocean & Logistics 2019-2022 (he is the architect of the integrator strategy). In February 2026 announced ~1,000 additional layoffs (HQ Copenhagen + regional) for $160M cost-out. **Gemini Cooperation** (alliance with Hapag-Lloyd, launched February 1, 2025): operationally exceptional — schedule reliability ~92.3% vs 62.4% industry average. But losing share because its capacity is fixed (Hub & Spoke) while MSC standalone and Premier Alliance expand. **Møller Family** (via A.P. Møller Holding) controls ~42% of the votes.
AMKBY.summary_why: AMKBY at $11.51 is a highly asymmetric contrarian bet: buying at the shipping cycle bottom when the market expects a quick normalization of the Suez that seems not to be happening, while non-Ocean segments are turning upward and the valuation is below book value. Specific thesis: (1) **The "Suez return" is stalling — this protects rates more than guidance assumes**: Houthi cease-fire took effect in October 2025 and the first Mærsk ships passed through Suez in December. BUT: February 28 and March 28, 2026, the Houthis re-threatened and resumed attacks tied to the US-Israel-Iran escalation. Suez transit volume still -60% vs 2023. Xeneta (the authoritative voice on freight rates): "this crushes the hope of a large-scale return to the Red Sea in 2026." SCFI April 27: 1,875 points = **+39% YoY**. Rates are HIGHER than a year ago, not collapsing; (2) **Guidance EBIT -$1.5B to +$1.0B assumes rapid normalization**: if the Cape route prolongs = 6-8% less capacity in the market = much higher rate floor than modeled. Guidance is essentially a binary option on Red Sea geopolitics. The reality of April 2026 is supporting the upside; (3) **P/B 0.61 = the world's cheapest large shipping company in book**: Hapag-Lloyd bought ZIM in February 2026 at $35/share = **1.06x P/B** ($4.2B deal, 58% premium to pre-announcement close). That sets a conceptual floor for the market: strategists pay 1.06x book for cyclical shipping. Mærsk at 0.61x = ~40% implicit discount to deal print. If Hapag-Lloyd paid that for ZIM (smaller, less integrated), Mærsk at 1.0x book = $19/ADR (+65%); (4) **Terminals + L&S turning up while Ocean turns down**: Terminals EBIT FY2025 +31% to $1.7B with ROIC 16.1%; L&S EBIT +36% to $729M. The two non-Ocean = $2.4B combined EBIT, **enough to cover the entire 2026 dividend ($1.05B implied by DKK 480) and the $1B buyback**. If Ocean enters operating loss in 2026, the good segments continue paying cash flow to shareholders; (5) **Capital return defended**: $2.1B total 2026 (DKK 480 dividend + $1B buyback). Yield 3.23% on AMKBY today. Buyback in April 2026 at 0.61x P/B = arithmetic compression of book per share over time. Every dollar they buy back below book is instantly accretive; (6) **Accounting useful life change +$700M to EBIT 2026**: effective January 1, 2026, Mærsk extended the useful life of ships from 20 to 25 years (consistent with post-COVID operational reality — ships are maintained better than modeled). It's ~$700M less depreciation = $700M more GAAP EBIT. Purely accounting, but real for reported earnings; (7) **Gemini schedule reliability 92.3% is a structural moat**: global industry avg 62.4% at the start of 2026. Hapag + Mærsk lead rankings. Premium shippers (BMW, Apple, Walmart) pay a premium for reliability — when Suez normalizes and supply exceeds, Gemini retains contracts while non-allied carriers fight for spot; (8) **Vincent Clerc focused and disciplined**: 1,000 layoffs Feb-Mar 2026 + $160M cost-out annually. Double down on integrator strategy (no Ocean spinoff). 27 methanol ships in fleet end-2025 (from 1 in 2023) = lead structural in decarbonization — EU ETS and FuelEU Maritime favor low-emission fleet, asymmetric regulatory cost against traditional competition.
AMKBY.summary_risk: The dominant and specifically quantifiable risk is the **double source of supply when Suez normalizes**: the complete Cape→Suez return releases 6-8% of global effective capacity instantly. HSBC modeled a rate decline of 9-16% in that scenario; accelerated transition adds an additional -10%. Mærsk could enter a net operating loss — and the guidance itself reflects this: floor EBIT -$1.5B in 2026. Other risks: (1) **Extreme structural overcapacity**: industry orderbook = **33.5% of the existing fleet** (~11.3M TEU on order). Historically levels >25% have preceded 3-5 year bear cycles. Supply growth 2026 +3.6% vs demand +3.0% = oversupply of ~60 bps. Industry forecast: ~$10B AGGREGATE LOSS of carriers in 2026 vs $20B profit in 2025; (2) **US-China structural weakness**: US imports from China -28% YoY in 2025; Q1 2026 remains -30% YoY. Top-5 carriers report -33%+ booking decline TPEB. Trade truce is 12 months, not structural. Permanent sourcing displacement to Indonesia/Thailand/Vietnam favors other routes, not necessarily Mærsk; (3) **Negative Free Cash Flow 2026**: guidance FCF -$3B while paying dividend $1.05B + buyback $1B = $2.05B out vs negative cash coming in. Balance sheet absorbs the gap (~$30B total liquidity). Sustainable 1-2 years; problematic if cycle bottom lasts longer; (4) **Houthi/Iran resumption**: February 28 and March 28, 2026 attacks already restored partial diversion. If Iran escalation expands regional conflict, risk of damage to ships or insurance pricing skyrocketing; (5) **FY2025 dividend cut vs FY2024**: payment DKK 480 (40% payout on net income $2.9B) vs DKK 1,300+ pre-cycle bottom. If 2026 EBIT enters loss, 2027 dividend could be cut again (not cutting would imply payout >100%); (6) **Gemini losing relative share**: Asia-NA West Coast 15%→13%, Asia-NA East Coast 20%→17%, Asia-N Europe 27%→23% between April 2025 and May 2026. By design Hub & Spoke = fixed capacity. Spot shippers go to alliances adding capacity. Reliability premium may not compensate for share loss; (7) **Single-analyst Yahoo projecting -$0.28 EPS 2027**: reflects trough scenario — full Suez + peak capacity + tariffs ceiling + pre-COVID mean-reverting tariffs. Broad consensus (37 analysts, "Moderate Sell" panel with 9 sell / 0 buy) has median target DKK 13,245 = **-10% downside** in local currency on Copenhagen; (8) **Bunker fuel volatility**: VLSFO Singapore can absorb 8-12% of Ocean revenue. Iran tensions spike fuel prices doubly — attacks + sanctions on Iran crude. Hedging typically covers only 20-30%; (9) **Q1 2026 reports May 7, 2026** (10 days) — first post-guidance test. Any additional guide cut = -10-15% day 1.

# A.P. Møller-Mærsk (AMKBY ADR / MAERSK-B Copenhagen) — Full Research

## Price: $11.51 (AMKBY) | DKK 14,720 (MAERSK-B) | P/E TTM: 12.93x | P/E Forward: N/A* | Div Yield: 3.23% | Market Cap: $33.4B | P/B: 0.61

*P/E forward technically -42.5x — reflects a single analyst's projection modeling a small loss in 2027. We leave it N/A due to low informativeness.

---

## ADR Structure

**1 AMKBY ADR = 1/200 of a B share (MAERSK-B) in Copenhagen.**

Math check (April 27, 2026):
- MAERSK-B: 14,720 DKK
- Exchange rate: ~6.40 DKK/USD
- 14,720 / 200 = 73.6 DKK per ADR-equivalent unit
- 73.6 / 6.40 = **$11.50** ✅

Trades on OTC Markets OTCPK (not on NYSE/Nasdaq). Reports finances in **native USD** (unlike most European ADRs that report in domestic currency).

---

## What It Is

A.P. Møller-Mærsk A/S is the **world's #2 container shipping company** (behind MSC, which is private with ~22% market share). It is the **largest logistics integrator globally**, operating in 130 countries.

Founded in 1904 by A.P. Møller and his father Peter Mærsk-Møller in Svendborg, Denmark. HQ in Copenhagen. ~95,000 employees.

**CEO since January 2023: Vincent Clerc** — Danish, with Mærsk since 1998. Led Ocean & Logistics 2019-2022, architect of the integrator strategy. Replaced Søren Skou.

**Møller Family** (through A.P. Møller Holding): ~42% of the votes. It has been the controlling family since 1904. Ane Mærsk Mc-Kinney Uggla (chairwoman of A.P. Møller Holding) is the current head.

## Segment Structure (FY 2025)

| Segment | Revenue 2025 | % Revenue | EBIT FY 2025 | YoY EBIT |
|----------|--------------|-----------|--------------|----------|
| **Ocean** | $35.0B | ~65% | $1.4B | -71% |
| **Logistics & Services** | $15.1B | ~28% | $729M | +36% |
| **Terminals** | $5.3B | ~10% | $1.7B | +31% |
| **Towage & Misc** | residual | <1% | residual | - |

Total FY 2025 revenue: $54.0B. EBITDA $9.5B. EBIT $3.5B (top end of original guidance).

### 1. Ocean — The Cyclical Segment

- 726 container ships
- Capacity: 4.5M TEU
- Global market share: 14% (#2 behind MSC ~22%)
- 3.38M FFE loaded in FY 2025 (+8% YoY)
- Average rate FY 2025: -23% YoY

EBIT fell from $4.9B (2024) to $1.4B (2025) — the effect of freight rates normalizing post-mid-2024 Houthi peak. Q4 2025: **EBIT -$153M (first quarterly loss of the cycle)**.

### 2. Logistics & Services — The Strategic Segment

Reorganized 2024 into 3 sub-segments:
- **Landside** (managed by country): warehousing, e-commerce delivery, contract logistics, customs
- **Forwarding** (global): air + LCL + project cargo + insurance
- **Solutions** (global): digital platforms, supply chain visibility

- 2025 revenue $15.1B (+1.2%)
- 2025 EBIT $729M (+36%)
- 2025 EBITDA $1.7B (+17%)

E-commerce: Mærsk delivers to 93% of the US in <3 days. Multi-carrier parcel network. Customs: AI-driven HS code harmonization (WCO Data Model). These were not offerings five years ago.

### 3. Terminals (APM Terminals) — The Jewel

- 65 global terminals
- 2025 revenue $5.3B (+20%)
- EBITDA $1.8B (+15%)
- EBIT $1.7B (+31%)
- **ROIC 16.1%** (vs Ocean ROIC <5% at bottom)
- Utilization 88%
- Volume +8.4% YoY

Terminals EBIT margin >30%. It is the segment the market always underestimates.

## Q4 2025 (February 5, 2026)

| Metric | Q4 2025 | YoY |
|---------|---------|-----|
| Revenue | $13.3B | -8.7% |
| EBITDA | $1.8B | margin 13.8% |
| EBIT | $118M | margin 0.9% |
| **Ocean EBIT** | **-$153M** | vs +$1.6B Q4 2024 |
| Terminals EBIT | $321M | volume +8.4% |
| L&S EBIT | $194M | margin 4.9% |

**Q1 2026 reports May 7, 2026** (10 days after today).

## Guidance FY 2026 (issued February 5, 2026)

- **EBITDA: $4.5-$7.0B** (consensus was $6.5B)
- **EBIT: -$1.5B to +$1.0B** (wide binary range = Suez uncertainty)
- **FCF: at least -$3.0B** (Morgan Stanley had modeled -$0.8B → negative guide surprise)
- **CapEx 2026-27: $10-11B** combined
- Assumes global containerized demand +2-4%; Mærsk grows in-line with market
- Includes ~**$700M depreciation tailwind** by extending useful life of ships from 20 to 25 years (effective January 1, 2026)

## The Rate Tape (as of April 27, 2026)

### SCFI (Shanghai Containerized Freight Index)

- **April 27, 2026: 1,875.26 points**
- Flat WoW; +2.65% MoM; **+39.13% YoY**

### Drewry World Container Index (40ft container)

| Date | $/40ft | Δ |
|-------|--------|---|
| Apr 2 | $2,287 | stable |
| Apr 9 | $2,309 | +1% |
| Apr 16 | $2,246 | -3% |
| Apr 23 | $2,232 | -1% |

- Asia-Europe declining: Shanghai→Genoa $3,071 (-8%), Shanghai→Rotterdam $2,147 (-4%)
- Transpacific holding: Shanghai→LA $2,934 (+4%), Shanghai→NY $3,562 (flat)
- Transatlantic: $2,326 (+15% due to PSS $1,100/40ft effective April 15 + capacity cuts)

**Conclusion of the tape**: rates are at levels that support the upside of guidance, not the downside.

## The Red Sea / Suez Status

| Event | Date |
|--------|-------|
| Houthi cease-fire (Gaza peace plan) | October 10, 2025 |
| First Mærsk ship transiting Suez (Sebarok) | December 19, 2025 |
| CMA CGM MEDEX/INDAMEX return via Suez | January 2026 |
| **Houthis threaten/resume attacks (US/Israel-Iran)** | **February 28, 2026** |
| Second round Houthi attacks | **March 28, 2026** |
| Suez transits = -60% vs 2023 | April 2026 |

Xeneta (Peter Sand): "this crushes the hope of a large-scale return to the Red Sea in 2026".

Mærsk MECL service: "permanent transition back to Suez".

**Estimated capacity unlock from full Cape→Suez return: 6-8% of global container capacity instantly.**

## Gemini Cooperation (Alliance with Hapag-Lloyd)

- **Operationally launched: February 1, 2025**
- **Average schedule reliability: ~92.3%** (vs industry avg 62.4%)
- Asia-N Europe + Transatlantic: >95%
- "Hub & Spoke" design: few stops at main hubs + shuttles to smaller ports

**Capacity dynamics (April 2025 → May 2026):**
- Asia-NA West Coast: 15% → 13%
- Asia-NA East Coast: 20% → 17%
- Asia-N Europe: 27% → 23%

Gemini loses relative share because its capacity is **fixed by design** (Hub & Spoke) while competitors (MSC standalone, Premier Alliance ONE/HMM/Yang Ming, Ocean Alliance CMA CGM/Cosco/Evergreen) expand volume. Trade-off: reliability vs capacity.

Mærsk alone: 726 ships / 4.5M TEU / 14% share. Hapag-Lloyd alone: 270 ships / 1.9M TEU.

## Capital Return

### Dividend

- **FY 2025 (paid 2026): DKK 480/B-share** = **$0.37/ADR**
- 40% payout ratio on net income $2.9B
- AGM March 26, 2026 approved
- **Yield 3.23%** on AMKBY $11.51

Cut vs FY 2024: net income fell from $6B to $2.9B; payout policy 30-50%.

### Buybacks

- **2026 authorized: $1.0B**
- 2025 completed (Feb + Aug tranches)
- Capital reduction: DKK 1.133B via cancellation of A and B shares (approved AGM 2026)

### Total cash return 2026: $2.1B

(~6.3% of market cap)

## Decarbonization & Fleet

- **Net-zero target: 2040** (10 years ahead of IMO 2050)
- **Methanol dual-fuel fleet**: 19 ships by end-2025 (vs 1 in 2023), +6 in 2026, 25 dual-fuel by 2027
- 800,000 TEU fleet renewal program active
- 6 ships 17,480-TEU methanol delivered (last: Barcelona Mærsk, January 2026)
- 20 dual-fuel vessels (300,000 TEU) on order across 3 shipyards
- Laura Mærsk received first commercial e-methanol from Kassø (Denmark, May 2025), 42,000 t/yr capacity

**Industry orderbook = 33.5% of existing fleet** (~11.3M TEU on order). Historically extreme.

## Macro / Cycle Context

- **2025 global containerized demand: +3.5-4.2%**
- **2026 consensus: +3.0%**
- **2026 supply growth: +3.6%** = oversupply ~60 bps
- Industry overcapacity: ~27% average 2026-2028
- Industry P&L: ~$60B profit 2024 → ~$20B 2025 → forecast **~$10B AGGREGATE LOSS 2026**

### US-China

- US imports from China: **-28% YoY 2025**, Q1 2026 remains -30%
- Top-5 carriers report -33%+ booking decline in TPEB eastbound
- Sourcing displacement: Indonesia US imports +34% in 2025; Thailand +28%
- US-China truce = **12-month, not structural**

## ZIM-Hapag Print = Valuation Floor

- **February 2026**: Hapag-Lloyd announced acquisition of ZIM
- **Price: $35/share cash = $4.2B deal**
- Premium: **58% over February 13, 2026 close**
- **Implied multiple: ~1.06x P/B**

Mærsk today: 0.61x P/B. ZIM print 1.06x P/B on a smaller, less integrated competitor.

**If Mærsk were to trade at 1.0x P/B = $19/ADR (+65% upside).**

ZIM (post-deal) is being spun off into "New ZIM" as Israeli spinoff — non-Israeli assets go to Hapag-Lloyd.

## Peer Set

| Company | P/E TTM | P/B | Notes |
|----------|---------|-----|-------|
| Mærsk B (MAERSK-B / AMKBY) | 12.93 | **0.61** | trades below book |
| Hapag-Lloyd (HLAG.DE) | n/a precise | 1.12-1.18 | EUR 115-118; mkt cap €21B |
| ZIM | 2.64 (pre-deal) | 1.06x (deal print) | being acquired |
| COSCO Shipping (1919.HK) | 3.8-11.9 | 0.53-1.24 | wide range between sources |
| Mitsui O.S.K. (9104.T) | 2.5-10 | n/a | Elliott built stake March 2026 (+11%) |

**Mærsk = the largest cheapest in book.** Only COSCO comparable, but less liquid and with China geopolitical risk.

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **AMKBY price** | $11.51 |
| **MAERSK-B price** | DKK 14,720 |
| **Market Cap** | $33.4B |
| **P/E TTM** | 12.93x |
| **P/B** | 0.61x |
| **EPS TTM (ADR)** | $0.89 |
| **Book Value (ADR)** | $18.95 |
| **Dividend** | $0.37/ADR |
| **Yield** | 3.23% |
| **Buyback 2026** | $1.0B |
| **Total Return 2026** | $2.1B |
| **FY 2025 Revenue** | $54.0B |
| **FY 2025 EBIT** | $3.5B |
| **FY 2025 EBITDA** | $9.5B |
| **FY 2026 EBIT Guidance** | -$1.5B to +$1.0B |
| **FY 2026 FCF Guidance** | ≥ -$3.0B |
| **Net Income FY 2025** | $2.9B |

## Why Now

### 1. Suez Return Stalling
SCFI +39% YoY. Houthi attacks February 28 + March 28, 2026. Suez transits -60% vs 2023. Rates higher than a year ago, not collapsing as guidance assumes.

### 2. Discount to Private M&A Print
ZIM sold at 1.06x P/B. Mærsk at 0.61x = ~40% implicit discount to the sector's most recent deal print.

### 3. Terminals + L&S Cover Dividend Alone
$2.4B combined EBIT from non-Ocean segments. Dividend $1.05B + buyback $1B = $2.05B outflows. Covered without needing Ocean profit.

### 4. Vincent Clerc Disciplined
1,000 layoffs February-March 2026 + $160M cost-out. Double down on integrator, no spinoff. Structural lead in methanol fleet.

### 5. $700M Accounting Tailwind
Useful life 20→25 years effective January 1, 2026 = $700M more GAAP EBIT in 2026 purely by accounting (real for reported earnings).

### 6. Capital Return Sustainable
$2.1B total 2026 = 6.3% of market cap. Buyback at 0.61x P/B = instant accretion to book per share.

### 7. Asymmetric Optionality
Floor: P/B 0.61 hard. Ceiling: if Suez doesn't normalize + L&S/Terminals keep growing + ZIM print holds = easy re-rate to 1.0x P/B = +65%.

## Risks

### 1. Double Supply When Suez Normalizes
6-8% effective capacity released instantly. HSBC: -9 to -16% in rates. Accelerated: -10% additional.

### 2. Structural Overcapacity
33.5% orderbook/fleet. Supply +3.6% vs demand +3.0% in 2026. Industry forecast: ~$10B AGGREGATE LOSS carriers 2026.

### 3. US-China Structural
Imports -28% YoY 2025. Truce 12-months. Permanent sourcing displacement. TPEB doesn't recover fast.

### 4. Negative FCF 2026
-$3B FCF while paying $2.05B to shareholders = balance sheet absorbs. Sustainable 1-2 years; problematic if cycle bottom lasts longer.

### 5. Houthi/Iran Resumption
February-March 2026 attacks already restored partial diversion. Iran escalation expands risk.

### 6. Dividend Cut Risk
DKK 480 (40% payout) sustainable with $2.9B net income 2025. If EBIT enters loss 2026, 2027 dividend could be re-cut.

### 7. Gemini Share Loss
Fixed capacity by design = loses relative share while competitors expand. Reliability premium may not compensate.

### 8. Single-Analyst -$0.28 EPS 2027
Reflects complete trough scenario. Broad consensus Moderate Sell with median DKK 13,245 = -10% downside.

### 9. Q1 2026 = May 7, 2026
First post-guidance test. Any additional guide cut = -10-15% day 1.

### 10. Bunker Fuel Volatility
VLSFO Singapore can absorb 8-12% of Ocean revenue. Iran tensions spike double-whammy. Hedging covers 20-30% typically.

## Conclusion

AMKBY at $11.51 lets you buy:

1. **The #2 global shipping company** + the world's largest logistics integrator
2. **0.61x P/B** vs ZIM-Hapag deal print of 1.06x P/B = ~40% implicit discount to private benchmark
3. **Terminals + L&S** ($2.4B combined EBIT) that cover the entire cash return alone
4. **3.23% dividend yield** + $1B buyback = 6.3% total return on market cap
5. **Binary optionality** over Suez geopolitics — guidance assumes rapid normalization that isn't happening
6. **Structural lead in methanol** fleet (19 ships end-2025 vs 1 in 2023) with EU ETS asymmetric against traditional competition

The thesis isn't "shipping returns to 2024 super-cycle". It's: **the bottom is shallower than priced, non-Ocean segments are turning up, and private strategic buyers pay 1.06x P/B for similar cyclical assets**. If Suez doesn't normalize fast → upside of guidance. If it does → P/B 0.61 floor + accretive buyback + 3.23% dividend. Asymmetry rarely exists in large-cap.

---

*Research date: April 27, 2026 | Next review: October 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
AMKBY.one_liner: A.P. Møller-Mærsk is a major Danish shipping company facing a crucial moment due to Suez Canal issues keeping shipping rates high.
AMKBY.why_short: Møller-Mærsk is an opportunity because shipping rates are high due to Suez Canal issues, benefiting the company more than expected.
AMKBY.risk_short: The main risk is that if the Suez Canal normalizes, more ships will be available, potentially lowering rates and affecting company profits.

ARM.research_full:
# ARM Holdings (ARM) — Full Research

## Price: $125.28 | P/E: 169 | P/E Forward: 64.7 | Div Yield: 0% | Market Cap: $134.8B

---

## What is ARM?

ARM Holdings designs the **most widely used processor architecture in the world**. They don't make chips — they design the "recipe" and charge licenses and royalties to all companies that make chips based on ARM: Apple, Qualcomm, Samsung, Google, Amazon, Nvidia, and practically all smartphone manufacturers, IoT, and increasingly data centers and PCs.

## Business Model

| Segment | Description | % Revenue |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Royalties** | Charge for each chip sold based on ARM | ~65% |
| **Licensing** | Charge for access to new architecture designs | ~35% |

### Why It's Special:
- **99%+ of smartphones** use ARM chips
- **Zero factories** — pure asset-light model. They only design IP
- **Recurring revenue** — every chip sold worldwide pays them royalties
- **Operating margin ~25%** and rising

## Financial Results (FY2026 Q3, Dec 2025)

- **Quarterly revenue: $983M** (+19% YoY)
- **Estimated annual revenue: $4.67B**
- **EPS: $0.39** (quarterly)
- **Guidance FY2027:** Revenue $4.8-5.2B (~20% growth)
- **Operating margin:** Expanding

## The Catalyst: AI and Data Centers

ARM is transitioning from "the mobile chip company" to "the chip company for EVERYTHING":

1. **Neoverse (Data Centers)** — Google, Microsoft, Amazon already use ARM in their servers. AWS Graviton (ARM) is ~40% more efficient than x86
2. **AI Inference** — ARM chips are ideal for AI inference due to their energy efficiency
3. **Automotive** — ADAS, infotainment. ARM is in almost all new cars
4. **PCs** — Apple M-series showed ARM can dominate laptops. Qualcomm Snapdragon X already competes with Intel/AMD
5. **IoT** — billions of connected devices, almost all run on ARM

## Analyst Analysis

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Consensus | **Strong Buy** |
| % Buy | 90% |
| % Hold | 10% |
| % Sell | 0% |
| Average Price Target | **$160.63** |
| High Price Target | $210 |
| Low Price Target | $100 |
| **Upside to Target** | **+29%** |

## Why We Like It?

1. **Near-monopoly** — 99% of smartphones, growing in servers, cars, PCs, IoT
2. **Asset-light** — no need for $20B factories like TSMC or Intel. They only design IP
3. **Recurring revenue** — every chip sold pays them royalties. A subscription-like model but for hardware
4. **Huge AI boost** — big tech companies are switching from x86 to ARM. This is just starting
5. **90% of analysts say Buy** — almost unanimous consensus
6. **Growth ~20% YoY** — strong guidance for FY2027
7. **No real competition** — RISC-V is years behind in ecosystem and maturity

## Risks

1. **Priced expensively** — price vs. profit is 169 times past earnings. Any earnings miss is heavily punished
2. **Customer concentration** — top 5 customers represent a significant portion of revenue
3. **RISC-V long-term** — open-source architecture that could erode market share in IoT/low-cost
4. **Softbank ownership** — Softbank controls about 90%. They may make decisions that don't favor minority shareholders
5. **Geopolitical** — British company, critical IP. Subject to export restrictions (China)
6. **Forward P/E of 65x** — needs flawless execution to justify the valuation

---

*Research date: Mar 9, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
ARM.one_liner: ARM designs the brains of almost all smartphones and now also data centers, cars, and computers, earning money from every chip sold.
ARM.why_short: It's the leader in mobile processors and earns money every time one is sold. With AI, more companies are switching to ARM. Most experts think it's a good buy.
ARM.risk_short: The company is priced high and needs to perform perfectly. Softbank controls almost everything. A new competitor could take away customers in the future.

ASBFY.summary_short: British conglomerate owning Primark (Europe's #1 fast fashion) plus food, sugar, and ingredients businesses. They're considering separating Primark from the food business to unlock value.
ASBFY.summary_what: Associated British Foods is a conglomerate with 5 segments: Retail (Primark — £9.5B in sales, 384 stores in 13 countries), Grocery (brands like Twinings, Ovaltine, Kingsmill), Ingredients (yeasts and enzymes), Sugar (Illovo in Africa, British Sugar in UK), and Agriculture. Primark is the star business — low-cost fast fashion with no online store.
ASBFY.summary_why: Price vs. profit is 13x and forward is 11x — very cheap for what it is. Dividend (the cash they pay you) yield of 3.4%. The board announced a structural review that could result in a spin-off of Primark (valued at ~£13B). If separated, each business would be valued more individually. Primark continues to grow with ~4% in new stores. Primark's operating margin is 11.9%.
ASBFY.summary_risk: Primark has no online store — relies 100% on physical store traffic. Continental Europe has weak consumer spending. Sugar segment is breaking even. Total revenue fell 3% in 2025. The Weston family controls the company via Wittington Investments.
ASBFY.research_full:
# Associated British Foods (ASBFY) — Full Research

## Price: $25.13 | P/E: 13.2 | P/E Forward: 10.9 | Div Yield: 3.4% | Market Cap: $17.7B

---

## What is ABF?

Associated British Foods is a **diversified conglomerate** from the UK with operations in food, ingredients, sugar, agriculture, and retail (Primark). Founded in 1935, it is controlled by the Weston family through Wittington Investments.

## Business Segments

| Segment | Description | Approx Revenue |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Retail (Primark)** | Low-cost fast fashion, 384 stores in 13 countries | £9.5B (~49%) |
| **Grocery** | Twinings, Ovaltine, Kingsmill, Jordans, Dorset | ~20% |
| **Ingredients** | AB Mauri (yeasts), ABF Ingredients (enzymes) | ~12% |
| **Sugar** | Illovo (Africa), British Sugar (UK) | ~12% |
| **Agriculture** | AB Agri — animal nutrition | ~7% |

## Primark — The Star Business

Primark is the **#1 fast fashion retailer in Europe by value**:
- **384 stores** in 13 countries (UK, Ireland, Spain, Germany, France, Italy, USA, etc.)
- **Sales: £9.5B** with an operating margin of **11.9%**
- **Operating profit: £1.1B** (+2% YoY)
- **No online store** — all business is in-store traffic
- Ultra-low prices: competes with Zara and H&M but cheaper
- Expansion: ~4% growth from new stores

## 2025 Results

- **Total Revenue: £19.5B** (-3% actual, -1% constant)
- **Adjusted Operating Profit: £1.7B** (-12% constant)
- **Adjusted EPS: 174.9p** (-11%)
- **Free Cash Flow: £648M**
- Primark solid, Sugar and Grocery under pressure

## The Catalyst: Spin-off of Primark

The board announced a **structural review** that could result in the **separation of Primark from the food business**:
- Primark alone could be worth **£13B+**
- The food/ingredients business would have independent valuation
- **Sum-of-parts analysis** suggests ABF is undervalued as a conglomerate
- If separated, investors could value each part more efficiently

## Valuation

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| P/E Trailing | **13.2x** |
| P/E Forward | **10.9x** |
| Dividend Yield | **3.4%** |
| Price/Sales | ~0.7x |
| EV/EBITDA | ~8x |

This is **very cheap** for a company with a retailer making £9.5B in sales and premium food brands.

## Why Do We Like It?

1. **Primark is a cash flow machine** — £1.1B in operating profit, growing
2. **Ridiculously low valuation** — P/E 13x with spin-off catalyst
3. **Potential spin-off** — would unlock significant value
4. **3.4% dividend** — they pay you to wait
5. **Diversification** — if Primark has a bad quarter, other segments cushion
6. **Everyone uses Primark** — Primark's customers become our customers

## Risks

1. **No ecommerce** — Primark doesn't sell online. 100% dependent on store traffic
2. **Weak Europe** — consumer spending in continental Europe remains pressured
3. **Sugar in trouble** — segment breaking even, restructuring underway
4. **Weston family controls** — they can make decisions not favoring minority shareholders
5. **Total revenue falling** — -3% in 2025, needs to reverse trend
6. **Spin-off not guaranteed** — review may not result in separation

---

*Research date: Mar 10, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
ASBFY.one_liner: Associated British Foods owns Primark and food businesses; they might split Primark to increase each part's value.
ASBFY.why_short: The company seems cheap for what it offers and could be worth more if they split off Primark. Plus, Primark keeps opening stores and making money.
ASBFY.risk_short: Primark only sells in physical stores, not online, and people in Europe are buying less. Also, the sugar business is just breaking even.

ASML.summary_short: ASML is a MONOPOLY. They are the ONLY company in the world that makes machines for advanced chips. Without ASML, there are no iPhones, no Nvidia, no AI. Period.
ASML.summary_what: ASML makes the lithography machines that chip manufacturers (TSMC, Samsung, Intel) need to produce semiconductors. Their EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet) technology is the only one in the world capable of making 5nm, 3nm, and 2nm chips. Without these machines, modern chips don't exist — not for smartphones, AI, or anything else.
ASML.summary_why: Literal monopoly — there is no competitor #2. Each EUV machine costs $380M+ and they have a backlog for years. Revenue of €32.7B in 2025 (+16% YoY). Net income of €9.6B. Guidance 2026: €34-39B. The AI boom needs more chips, and more chips = more ASML machines. Plus €8.2B in recurring service for maintenance.
ASML.summary_risk: Priced expensively (price vs. profit 49x). China accounted for 33% of revenue in 2025 but export restrictions are reducing that. Semiconductor cycles can create big price swings. They only have one main product.
ASML.research_full:
# ASML Holding (ASML) — Complete Research

## Price: $1,390.20 | P/E: 48.8 | P/E Forward: 31.9 | Div Yield: 0.64% | Market Cap: $545.9B

---

## What is ASML?

ASML is a Dutch company that makes the **most advanced lithography machines in the world**. These machines are essential for producing semiconductors — the chips in your phone, computer, car, and in the servers that train AI models.

**They are a MONOPOLY.** No other company in the world can make EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet Lithography) machines.

## Products

| Product | Description | Approx Price |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **EUV (NXE:3800E)** | Extreme ultraviolet lithography for 5nm/3nm chips | ~$380M per machine |
| **High-NA EUV (EXE:5200)** | Next generation for 2nm and smaller chips | ~$400M+ per machine |
| **DUV** | Deep ultraviolet lithography for less advanced chips | ~$100M per machine |
| **Installed Base Mgmt** | Service, maintenance, and upgrades of installed machines | €8.2B/year |

### Who are their clients?
- **TSMC** (makes chips for Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm)
- **Samsung** (makes chips for itself and others)
- **Intel** (PC and server chip maker)
- **SK Hynix, Micron** (memory)

## 2025 Results

- **Revenue: €32.7B** (+16% YoY)
- **Net Income: €9.6B** (+26% YoY)
- **Gross Margin: 52.8%**
- **EPS: €24.73** (annual)
- **48 EUV machines** sold (including High-NA)
- **Bookings Q4: €13.2B** (€7.4B EUV + €5.8B DUV)
- **Installed Base Management: €8.2B** (+26% YoY) — recurring revenue

## Geographic Distribution (2025)

| Region | % Revenue |
|--------|-----------|
| **China** | ~33% (decreasing due to restrictions) |
| **South Korea** | ~25% |
| **Taiwan** | ~22% |
| **USA** | ~10% |
| **Japan** | ~5% |
| **Europe** | ~5% |

## 2026 Guidance

- **Revenue: €34B - €39B** (up to +19% growth)
- **Gross Margin: 51% - 53%**
- **Q1 2026: €8.2B - €8.9B**
- New share buyback program announced

## Why Do We Like It?

1. **MONOPOLY** — no competitor #2 in EUV. It's impossible to replicate this technology in less than 10-15 years
2. **AI = more chips = more ASML machines** — the AI boom directly translates to more ASML purchases
3. **€8.2B in recurring revenue** — once a machine is installed, the client pays for service for decades
4. **Strong margins** — 52.8% gross margin, improving
5. **Backlog for years** — machines are sold before they are made
6. **Growing dividend** — 0.64% but increased every year. More buybacks announced

## Risks

1. **Priced expensively** — P/E of 49x. Any earnings miss is punished heavily
2. **China** — 33% of revenue comes from China. Increasing export restrictions
3. **Cyclicality** — the semiconductor industry has cycles. If clients cut spending, ASML suffers
4. **Client concentration** — TSMC + Samsung + Intel = almost all revenue
5. **Geopolitics** — European company with strategic technology. Subject to US and European pressures
6. **Single product** — if someone invented an alternative to lithography (unlikely but not impossible), ASML would become obsolete

---

*Research date: Mar 11, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
ASML.one_liner: ASML is the only company making machines needed for chips in iPhones and AI.
ASML.why_short: ASML has no competition in its field. Their machines are very expensive and have orders booked for years. With the rise of AI, more chips are needed, which means more sales for ASML.
ASML.risk_short: The company is priced high compared to its earnings. Also, restrictions on selling to China could impact revenue, and they rely heavily on one type of machine.

AU.summary_short: AngloGold Ashanti is one of the world's largest gold miners: it pulls gold out of the ground in Africa, Australia and the Americas, and sells it. With gold at all-time highs (~$4,300/ounce in 2026), its profits and cash flow have exploded. It's one of the most direct ways to get exposure to gold WHILE collecting a ~4.2% dividend along the way — something physical gold or a gold ETF does NOT give you. It just bought Centamin (the Sukari mine in Egypt), adding low-cost production. It trades cheap (~12x earnings, ~9x expected) relative to the cash it generates. CEO Alberto Calderón. Q1 2026: profit $1.28 billion, free cash flow $1.2 billion, produced 724 thousand ounces at a cost of $1,955/ounce (with gold above $4,000, the margin is enormous).
AU.summary_what:
AngloGold Ashanti plc (NYSE: AU) is one of the **largest gold-producing companies in the world**. Its business is simple to understand: it operates mines that extract gold from the ground, processes it, and sells it at the market price. After re-domiciling in the United Kingdom in 2023, its home base and main headquarters is **London**, though its mines are in Africa (Ghana, Tanzania, etc.), Australia and the Americas.

**How it makes money**: the equation is 'gold price minus the cost of getting it out.' Cost is measured with the **AISC (all-in sustaining cost)**, the total cost of producing one ounce including maintenance. In Q1 2026 its AISC was ~$1,955/ounce — and with gold trading above $4,000/ounce, the margin on every ounce is gigantic. It produced 724 thousand ounces in the quarter.

**Key move**: it bought **Centamin**, owner of the **Sukari mine in Egypt**, one of the region's largest and lowest-cost, boosting production and reserves.

**The difference vs. physical gold/an ETF**: owning AU is gold exposure BUT with a **~4.19% dividend** — the company distributes part of its cash flow to shareholders (it paid a 116¢ dividend in the period). Physical gold or a gold ETF pays you nothing; here you get paid to wait.

**Leadership**: CEO **Alberto Calderón**. Q1 2026: profit $1.28 billion, free cash flow $1.2 billion.
AU.summary_why: AU at $83.96 is the way to own gold that actually PAYS you: direct exposure to the metal at all-time highs, while collecting a ~4.2% dividend and trading cheap relative to the mountain of cash it generates. Specific reasons: (1) **Leverage to the gold price**: when gold rises, a miner's profits rise MORE than proportionally, because its costs are fixed. With gold at ~$4,300/ounce and a cost (AISC) of ~$1,955, AngloGold earns ~$2,000+ on every ounce — historic margins. (2) **Gold as the 2026 safe haven**: geopolitical uncertainty, central banks buying gold, and doubts about the dollar have driven the metal to record highs. AU is a leveraged bet on that theme. (3) **A ~4.19% dividend**: unlike physical gold or a gold ETF (which pay $0), here you collect real income while you wait. (4) **Cheap valuation**: ~12x earnings and ~9x expected — the market still isn't paying the multiple its cash flow deserves. (5) **Torrential cash flow**: $1.2 billion of free cash flow in ONE quarter, funding dividends, buybacks and the Centamin acquisition without taking on debt. (6) **Centamin/Sukari**: adds low-cost ounces, improving the production profile. Analysts: Buy/Strong Buy, average target ~$120 (~+43%).
AU.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **The gold price — the #1**: the ENTIRE thesis depends on gold. If gold corrects (because real interest rates rise, the dollar strengthens, or risk aversion fades), AU's profits fall MORE than proportionally — the leverage works in both directions. (2) **Geopolitical/jurisdictional risk**: its mines are in Africa (Ghana, Tanzania, Egypt, etc.) and other regions prone to political instability, mining-tax changes, nationalizations or disputes with local governments. (3) **Mining operational risk**: accidents, strikes, equipment failures, depleted veins or falling ore grades can push costs (AISC) up and production down. (4) **Cost inflation**: diesel, energy, labor and chemicals push the AISC higher; if gold doesn't keep pace, the margin compresses. (5) **Centamin integration**: every acquisition carries execution risk; integrating Sukari (Egypt) could bring surprises. (6) **No control over its selling price**: a miner is a 'price taker' — it doesn't set the price of gold, it receives it — so its fate is tied to a market it doesn't control.
AU.research_full:
# AngloGold Ashanti (AU) — Full Research

## Price: $83.96 | P/E TTM: ~12.3x | Forward P/E: ~9.08x | Div Yield: ~4.19% | Market Cap: ~$42.2B USD

---

## What is AngloGold Ashanti?

One of the **largest gold miners in the world**. It pulls gold from mines in Africa, Australia and the Americas and sells it. Domiciled in **London, United Kingdom** (re-domiciled in 2023).

## How it makes money

- Equation: **gold price − cost of getting it out (AISC)**.
- Q1 2026: AISC ~$1,955/ounce | Gold above $4,000/ounce → enormous margin.
- Q1 2026 production: **724 thousand ounces**.

## Key move

- Bought **Centamin** (the **Sukari** mine, Egypt): low-cost ounces, more reserves.

## Q1 2026

| Metric | Q1 2026 |
|---------|---------|
| Profit | $1.28 billion |
| Free cash flow | $1.2 billion |
| Production | 724 thousand ounces |
| AISC | $1,955/ounce |
| Dividend for the period | 116¢ |

## Vs. physical gold / a gold ETF

- Physical gold / ETF: pays **$0**.
- AU: pays a **~4.19%** dividend → you get paid to own gold.

## Leadership

- **CEO**: Alberto Calderón.

## Anchor Fact

**Gold is at its highest price in history (~$4,300 an ounce in 2026). It costs AngloGold about $1,955 to pull each ounce out of the ground — so it keeps more than $2,000 of profit per ounce. And unlike buying a gold coin or a gold ETF (which pay you nothing), this company hands you ~4.2% a year in dividends while gold does its job. It's 'gold that pays you rent.'**

## Top 5 Risks

1. **The gold price** — the whole thesis depends on it (leverage cuts both ways)
2. **Geopolitical/jurisdictional risk** — mines in Africa and unstable regions
3. **Mining operational risk** — accidents, strikes, falling ore grades
4. **Cost inflation** — diesel, energy, labor push the AISC up
5. **Centamin integration** — execution risk

## Analyst Consensus

- Rating: **Buy / Strong Buy**
- Average target: **~$120** (~+43% from $83.96)

## The Thesis in One Line

> Gold at all-time highs, but bought through a cheap miner that pays you ~4.2% in dividends while you wait — leveraged exposure to the metal with income included.

---

*Research date: Jun 8, 2026 | Next review: Dec 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
AU.one_liner: AngloGold is one of the world's biggest gold miners: it digs gold out of the ground and sells it — right as gold sits at its highest price in history.
AU.why_short: Owning AngloGold is like owning gold that pays you rent: you collect about 4% a year in dividends while you hold it — something a gold coin or a gold fund never pays you.
AU.risk_short: Everything hinges on the price of gold: if gold falls, the company's profits fall even faster.

AUKNY.summary_what:
Auckland International Airport Ltd (NZX/ASX: AIA, OTC ADR: AUKNY) operates **AKL — New Zealand's main air gateway**. It is a listed company (not government-owned, ~25% held by Auckland Council). Headquartered in Auckland, NZ.

**4 businesses**:

**(1) Aeronautical (~45% of revenue FY2025: NZ$449M, +14.5%)** — charges to airlines for landing, terminal use and per-passenger fees. Regulated under the NZ Commerce Commission's **Information Disclosure** regime.

**(2) Retail (~19%: NZ$189M, +3%)** — duty-free (the largest category), food & beverage, and stores inside the terminals. In 1H FY2026 *retail income declined* (traffic mix shift + soft NZ consumer).

**(3) Property (~17%: NZ$173M, +15%)** — rents from hotels (Pullman, ibis, Sudima), offices, hangars, logistics warehouses and **The Landing** shopping center in the Airport Precinct (~1,500 hectares). This segment is **NOT regulated** — the hidden upside of the model.

**(4) Parking + ground transport (~7%: NZ$72M, +9%)** — parking fees, car rental concessions, ride-share fees.

**ADR structure**:
- Primary listing: **NZX `AIA`** (also ASX `AIA`).
- ADR: **AUKNY Level 1 OTC** (Pink Sheets), depositary Deutsche Bank Trust Company Americas.
- **Ratio: 1 ADR = 5 ordinary shares**.
- Level 1 ADR means no full SEC reporting → illiquid, wide bid-ask spread.

**Leadership**:
- CEO: **Carrie Hurihanganui** (since early 2022) — first woman CEO of the airport. Background: former Air NZ Chief Growth Officer/COO.
- Chair: **Dr. Patrick Strange** (Director since 2015, Chair since 2018) — former CEO of Transpower NZ.

**Major shareholders**: Auckland Council ~25%, institutional NZ/Australia shareholders.
AUKNY.summary_why: AUKNY at $24.55 ADR means buying a *predictable, regulated infrastructure monopoly* in an island nation, with NZ$5.7 billion of capital spending starting to land in 2026 and passenger numbers still 11% below the pre-COVID peak. Specific reasons: (1) **Structural monopoly**: AKL is the dominant gateway of NZ — historically managing the great majority of international arrivals. Christchurch and Wellington are a distant #2 and #3. Building another airport the size of AKL in NZ would take 20+ years. (2) **Capital spending inflection underway**: the NZ$5.7B / 7-year program is already being built. Components: new 80,000 sq m domestic terminal integrated with international (5-minute walk between them), 250,000 sq m of new aircraft apron. This is 'the largest transformation of the airport in 60 years'. Main construction started early 2026. (3) **Regulatory binary risk is in the past**: in March 2025 the NZ Commerce Commission ruled confirming the investment is 'reasonable'. Target return adjusted to 7.82% (from 8.73%) — modest but predictable and regulated. (4) **Passenger recovery with room to run**: 18.7M in FY2025 vs. 21M in FY2019 = 11% upside just to return to the peak. China Eastern launched Shanghai-Auckland-Buenos Aires (the longest direct route in the world, estimated NZ$110M/year benefit to the NZ economy). (5) **Unregulated property layer growing +15% year over year** — ~17% of revenue comes from real estate rentals outside the aeronautical regulatory scheme. Hidden upside. (6) **NZ$1.4B capital raise (Sep 2023) already done** — the big dilution is behind us; the balance sheet can absorb the remaining capex. (7) **Semi-annual dividend with supplementary dividends** that effectively reimburse the 15% non-resident withholding tax for holders with a W-8BEN. (8) **Average analyst target NZD 8.69** (vs. spot NZD 8.28) = ~USD $26 ADR vs. $24.55 = ~+6% conservative upside + dividend.
AUKNY.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Commerce Commission regulation — #1 risk**: the Information Disclosure regime is being reviewed in 2026 to improve transparency during 'major capital investment'. It could tighten returns in the PSE5 period (2028–2032). For a regulated company, any compression in the allowed rate of return = compression in equity value. (2) **Further dilution not ruled out**: the company did an NZ$1.4B equity raise in Sep 2023 (NZ$1.2B institutional + NZ$200M retail, ~11.7% dilution at NZ$6.95). With NZ$5.7B of capex, another raise *is not impossible* if rates rise or passenger numbers disappoint. (3) **Tourism is cyclical and dependent on Asia/Australia**: 10.3M of the 18.7M passengers in FY2025 are international. A Chinese consumer slowdown, weak AUD or external shock (earthquake, pandemic, geopolitics) hits directly. (4) **Capex execution risk**: 'the largest transformation in 60 years' carries cost-overrun and delay risk. The second runway was already pushed from 2028 to 2038+. (5) **NZD/USD exchange rate for AUKNY ADR holders**: the ADR is unhedged. NZD has been volatile (range ~0.55–0.65 USD). A 10% NZD decline eats directly into the dividend + revaluation in USD terms. (6) **AUKNY OTC liquidity**: wide bid-ask spread, low volume. For a meaningful position, AIA.NZ directly on the NZX is a better option. (7) **Retail revenue shift**: 1H FY2026 retail income *fell* due to soft NZ consumer + traffic mix shift — the high-margin segment may stay under pressure. (8) **Modest yield** ~1.89%: during the capex phase the dividend per share doesn't grow aggressively, so this is not an income play.

# Auckland International Airport Ltd (AUKNY / AIA.NZ) — Full Research

## Price: $24.55 ADR | Price vs. profit (TTM): ~45x | Div Yield: ~1.89% | Market Cap: ~$8.5B USD

---

## What is Auckland Airport?

Operator of **New Zealand's main airport** (AKL) — the country's primary air gateway. It is not government-owned (listed on the NZX since 1998), but Auckland Council holds ~25%. Headquartered in Auckland, NZ.

Owner of: ~1,500 hectares of land, domestic + international terminals, 2 runways (the 2nd in planning), and the commercial operations inside the airfield + real estate around it.

| Segment | % Revenue | Notes |

| **Retail** | ~19% (NZ$189M) | Duty-free, food & beverage in terminals |
| **Property** | ~17% (NZ$173M) | NOT regulated, +15% year over year FY2025 |
| **Parking** | ~7% (NZ$72M) | Parking, car rental, ride-share |

| Passengers | 18.7M | +1.1% year over year, -11% vs. FY2019 |

## NZ$5.7B / 7-Year Capital Program

Components:
- **Domestic + international integration** — new 80,000 sq m domestic terminal east of the international, *3x the current size*. 5-minute walk between both. 'The largest transformation in 60 years'.
- **Airfield expansion**: 250,000 sq m of new apron to support the new domestic pier.
- **Second runway**: originally planned for 2028, *pushed to 2038+* (2025 master plan) due to slower passenger recovery.
- **Digital check-in overhaul** + international arrivals expansion (already open).

**2026 timeline**: main construction of check-in halls started early 2026 (in progress).

## ADR Structure

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Primary ticker | NZX **AIA** (also ASX AIA) |
| ADR | **AUKNY** Level 1 OTC (Pink Sheets) |

| Depositary | Deutsche Bank Trust Company Americas |
| ISIN ADR | US05070F3082 |
| CUSIP | 05070F308 |
| SEC reporting | No (Level 1) → illiquid |

## Tax / Withholding Tax for ADR Holders

- Base non-resident withholding tax 30% → **15% via US-NZ tax treaty** (DTA) with W-8BEN
- Imputation credits NOT usable by non-residents, BUT the **supplementary dividend regime** effectively reimburses the withholding on the imputed portion
- IRS treats as ordinary dividend (typically not qualified for Level 1 OTC)
- Frequency: **semi-annual** (interim ~March/April, final ~October)

## Leadership

- **CEO**: **Carrie Hurihanganui** (since early 2022) — first woman CEO of AKL. Background: former Air NZ Chief Growth Officer / COO
- **Chair**: **Dr. Patrick Strange** (Director 2015, Chair 2018) — former CEO Transpower NZ

## Anchor Fact

**Auckland is essentially the only serious way to fly into New Zealand: for decades it has handled the great majority of the country's international air traffic, not because it's the prettiest airport but because NZ is an island and building another airport of that size would take 20+ years. Meanwhile, they are building the largest transformation of the airport in 60 years — a new domestic terminal, 3 times the current size, connected to the international so you walk 5 minutes between flights.**

## Top 5 Risks

1. **Commerce Commission 2026 review** could tighten the allowed rate of return in PSE5
2. **Future dilution possible** if capex overruns or rates rise
3. **Cyclical tourism** dependent on Asia/Australia
4. **Capex execution** (largest transformation in 60 years, second runway already delayed 10 years)
5. **NZD/USD unhedged** for ADR holders

## Analyst Consensus

- Rating: **mixed Hold/Buy** (4 Buy / 6 Hold / 0 Sell)
- Average NZX target: **NZD 8.69** (range NZD 7.02–9.31)
- NZX spot: NZD 8.28 → ~5% upside
- In ADR terms (1:5 ratio, FX ~0.60): target ~$26 = +6% upside

## The Thesis in One Line

> A regulated infrastructure monopoly in an island nation with NZ$5.7B of capital spending starting to land in 2026 and passenger numbers still 11% below the pre-COVID peak — pay today for a re-rating when the new infrastructure enters service.

AUKNY.one_liner: Auckland Airport is New Zealand's main air gateway — a monopoly that owns the land, terminals, and runways, with a massive expansion underway and passengers still below pre-pandemic levels.
AUKNY.why_short: There is no other serious way to fly into New Zealand, which makes this airport a near-permanent monopoly. A 7-year, NZ$5.7 billion expansion is underway, and passenger numbers still have room to grow 11% just to match pre-pandemic levels.
AUKNY.risk_short: New Zealand's regulator is reviewing how much profit the airport can earn, and any tightening would hurt its value. Tourism is also cyclical — a slowdown in China or Australia would hit passenger numbers directly.

---

## What is American Water Works?

American Water Works is the **largest private water utility in the United States**. Simply put: they own the pipes, treatment plants, and infrastructure that bring clean water to your home and take away wastewater for treatment. In the areas they operate, they are a **monopoly** — if you live there, they are your water company.

## Infrastructure They Own

- ~80 surface water treatment plants
- ~520 groundwater treatment plants
- ~190 wastewater treatment plants
- ~54,500 miles of pipe
- ~1,200 groundwater wells
- ~1,800 pumping stations
- ~1,100 treated water storage facilities
- ~75 dams

## Business Segments

| Segment | Description | % Earnings Approx |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Regulated Utilities** | State-regulated water and sewer service | ~95% |
| **Military Services** | 50-year contracts with the Dept. of Defense on 18 bases | ~5% |

The military segment has a backlog of **$7.4 billion** in outstanding obligations with an average remaining term of 38 years.

## Subsidiaries by State (The Brands on Your Water Bill)

| Subsidiary | State |
|---|---|
| New Jersey American Water | New Jersey |
| Pennsylvania American Water | Pennsylvania |
| Missouri American Water | Missouri |
| Illinois American Water | Illinois |
| Indiana American Water | Indiana |
| California American Water | California |
| West Virginia American Water | West Virginia |
| Virginia American Water | Virginia |
| Kentucky American Water | Kentucky |
| Iowa American Water | Iowa |
| Maryland American Water | Maryland |
| Hawaii American Water | Hawaii |
| Tennessee American Water | Tennessee |

They serve **14 million people** through **~3.4 million connections** in ~1,700 communities.

## Financial Results 2025

| Metric | 2024 | 2025 | Change |
|---------|------|------|--------|
| **Revenue** | $4.68B | $5.14B | **+9.1%** |
| **Operating Income** | — | ~$1.88B | — |
| **Adjusted EPS** | $5.18 | $5.64 | **+8.9%** |
| **GAAP EPS** | $5.39 | $5.69 | +5.6% |
| **Capital Investment** | — | $3.2B | — |
| **Dividend/Share** | — | $3.31 annualized | ~8% growth |

## 2026 Guidance and Long-Term Goals

- **EPS 2026:** $6.02 - $6.12 (growth ~7-8%)
- **Rate base growth:** 8-9% annually
- **Dividend growth:** 7-9% annually
- **Capital investment:** $19-20B (2026-2030); $46-48B (2026-2035)

## Merger with Essential Utilities

All-stock merger announced in October 2025:
- Combines the two largest water utilities in the US
- Combined company will serve **4.7+ million connections in 17 states**
- Shareholders of both companies approved with ~99% (AWK) and ~95% (WTRG) of votes
- **Expected closing: Q1 2027** (pending regulatory approvals)
- Essential shareholders will receive 0.305 shares of AWK for each WTRG share

## Dividend — 18 Consecutive Years of Growth

- **Annual dividend:** $3.31/share ($0.83 quarterly)
- **Yield:** 2.46%
- **Average growth rate:** 8-9% annually over the last decade
- **Payout ratio:** ~56%
- 18 consecutive years of increases

## Analyst Analysis

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|

| High Price Target | $157 |
| Low Price Target | $114 |
| Upside to Target | ~2% |
| Analysts | 11-18 |
| Split | ~3 Buy / 8-9 Hold / 3-4 Sell |

The consensus is that AWK is a high-quality utility that is already **fairly valued** at current prices.

## Why Do We Like It?

1. **Essential irreplaceable service** — nobody stops using water. Stable demand regardless of the economy
2. **Regulated monopoly** — no competition in each territory. Predictable income and recession-resistant
3. **$1.3 trillion in needed investment** — US water infrastructure is 50-100+ years old. AWK is the largest private investor
4. **18 years of growing dividends** — ~8% annual growth with a sustainable payout ratio of 56%
5. **Transformative merger** — Essential Utilities expands to 17 states and 4.7M+ connections
6. **Growth through acquisitions** — 19 acquisitions under agreement ($267M, ~58,000 new connections)

## Risks

1. **Regulatory risk** — depends on state commissions to approve rate increases. West Virginia cut a requested increase from 22.5% to just 8% in 2023
2. **Priced expensively** — price vs. profit ~22x. Not cheap, but not excessive for a utility of this quality
3. **Capital intensive / Debt** — needs to fund $46-48B by 2035. High borrowing costs increase expenses
4. **Merger risk** — pending approval from multiple state commissions (PA, NJ, and others)
5. **Environmental risk** — costs of PFAS cleanup, water quality regulations, droughts, floods
6. **Political risk** — water privatization is politically sensitive. There may be pressure to municipalize

AWK.risk_short: It relies on government approval to raise rates and carries significant debt for its investments. Its pending merger still needs approvals.

| **Upside to Target** | **+9.4%** |

## Why Do We Like It?

1. **Forward price vs. profit of 8.7x** — ridiculously cheap for Europe's #1 insurer
2. **Dividend of 5.4%** — one of the highest in the portfolio, well covered
3. **75% total payout** — disciplined policy of returning money to shareholders
4. **Beta of 0.65** — less volatile than the market, defensive growth
5. **ROE of 14-16%** — superior value generation
6. **Global diversification** — 50 countries, 92M customers, multiple business lines
7. **€21B in cash generation** over 3 years — cash machine

## Risks

1. **Natural disasters** — climate change increases frequency and severity of claims
2. **Complex regulation** — operates in over 50 jurisdictions with different rules
3. **Borrowing costs** — drops in rates can affect investment returns
4. **AXA XL big price swings** — commercial/specialty lines are cyclical
5. **Exposure to emerging markets** — political and currency risk

---

*Research date: Mar 18, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
AXAHY.one_liner: AXA is Europe's largest insurer, serving 92 million customers in 50 countries, and pays a 5.4% dividend by returning a large portion of its profits to shareholders.
AXAHY.why_short: AXA is very cheap for its size and leadership. It offers a good dividend and has a plan to grow 6-8% in the coming years, making it attractive for those seeking stability and growth.
AXAHY.risk_short: Climate change could increase costs from natural disasters, and operating in over 50 countries means dealing with many different regulations.

HQ at 200 Vesey Street, Manhattan. ~80,400 employees. **Platinum refresh in September 2025** raised the annual fee to $895 (from $695) + added Resy/Uber/Walmart+/Equinox benefits — adoption accelerated among millennials/Gen Z (>30% of new Platinum holders). **Q1 2026 reported April 18, 2026**: revenue net of interest expense **~$17.0B (+8% FX-adj)**, **diluted EPS $3.64 (+9% YoY)**, **Card Member spending +7% FX-adj** led by premium services, **net card fees +18%** (Platinum/Gold/Centurion refresh tailwind), provisions for credit losses normalized at ~$1.2B. **FY26 guidance**: revenue growth +8-10%, EPS $15.20-$15.60. **Q1 2026 dividend**: $0.82/quarter (raised +17% in March 2025 from $0.70). 67M shares repurchased TTM (~$20B). **Closed-loop = the real moat**: AmEx sees every transaction on every card (data), monetizes the merchant (~2.4% MDR vs. ~1.8% Visa/MC), and builds loyalty through Membership Rewards. **Tock acquisition closed Q2 2024** ($400M) — high-end restaurant reservations; integrated with Resy (acquired 2019). **Centurion Lounges**: 30+ in global airports, expanding to Doha + new LAX terminal in 2026. AXP.summary_what:
American Express (AXP) is **the world's #1 premium card issuer with a proprietary closed-loop network** — the only global network that combines all 3 roles: (1) issues the card to consumers/businesses, (2) processes the transaction (no need for Visa/Mastercard as intermediary), (3) signs merchants directly. That = a closed-loop network. ~145 million active cards. ~80,400 employees.

**History**:
- **1850**: founded in Buffalo, NY as an express freight company
- **1882**: launches money orders; 1891: Travelers Cheques
- **1958**: issues first charge card
- **1966**: launches Gold Card
- **1984**: launches Platinum Card ($250 initial fee)
- **1999**: launches Centurion Card (invite-only, current fee $5,000+ initially $10,000)
- **2018**: Stephen Squeri succeeds Ken Chenault as CEO
- **2019**: acquires Resy (restaurant reservations)
- **2024**: acquires Tock for $400M (high-end restaurant reservations)
- **September 2025**: US Platinum refresh — fee $895 + expanded benefits

**3 Segments**:

**(1) U.S. Consumer Services (~38% revenue)**:
- Platinum, Gold, Centurion, Green, EveryDay, Cash Magnet
- ~57M cards
- Spending +9% Q1 2026

**(2) Commercial Services (~28% revenue)**:
- Business Platinum, Business Gold, Corporate Cards
- Corporate travel & entertainment (T&E)
- Spending +6% Q1 2026

**(3) International Card Services (~22% revenue)**:
- Mexico, UK, Japan, Australia, Italy, etc.
- 30+ countries with direct issuance or banking partners
- Spending +12% Q1 2026 (FX adj)

**Plus**: Network Services (~12%) — partnerships with banks that issue AmEx-branded products.

**HQ**: 200 Vesey Street, Manhattan (Brookfield Place). **Employees**: ~80,400 globally.

**Leadership**:
- **CEO/Chair**: **Stephen Squeri** since February 2018 (40+ years at AmEx, former CIO/Vice Chair)
- **CFO**: Christophe Le Caillec (since March 2024, formerly American Express International)
- **President US Consumer**: Doug Buckminster

**Q1 2026 (reported April 18, 2026)**:

| Metric | Q1 2026 | YoY |
|--------|---------|-----|
| Revenue net of interest | **~$17.0B** | **+8% FX-adj** |
| **Diluted EPS** | **$3.64** | +9% |
| Card Member spending | — | +7% FX-adj |
| **Net card fees** | — | **+18%** |
| Provisions for credit losses | ~$1.2B | normalized |

**FY26 Guidance**:
- Revenue +8-10%
- EPS **$15.20-$15.60**

**Dividend + Buyback**:
- **Q1 2026 dividend**: $0.82/quarter (raised +17% in March 2025)
- **Buyback TTM**: ~67M shares (~$20B)
- Total capital return ~9% market cap annually

**Membership Rewards anchor**:
- #1 rewards program in the world by value per point redeemed (The Points Guy 2.0¢/point)
- 67M Platinum/Gold holders in the US
- ~$13B liability on the balance sheet (unredeemed points) — free float that finances operations
AXP.summary_why: AXP at $314.29 means buying the only global issuer with a proprietary closed-loop network (closed-loop), an intact premium franchise post-2025 Platinum refresh, and a compounder that has delivered +28% EPS CAGR since 2020. Specific reasons: (1) **Q1 2026 delivered +8% revenue + 9% EPS + 18% in card fees**: the +18% YoY card fee growth is the most important metric — it confirms that the Platinum refresh (fee $695→$895) not only did NOT cause material churn, but actually accelerated millennial/Gen Z acquisition (>30% of new US Platinum in Q1 2026). Membership Rewards liability growing = engagement growing. (2) **Closed-loop = a structural moat immune to fintech**: AmEx is the only global network with all 3 roles (issuer + processor + acquirer). Visa/Mastercard do NOT compete for merchant relationships — they are neutral rails. AmEx charges ~2.4% MDR vs. ~1.8% Visa/MC because it delivers premium customers who spend 3-4x the average, plus data and Membership Rewards lock-in. Apple Pay/Google Pay/buy-now-pay-later do NOT disintermediate AmEx — they run on top of existing networks. (3) **Favorable millennial/Gen Z demographics**: contrary to the narrative that "young people don't pay fees," the 2025 Platinum refresh + 2023 Gold refresh ($250 fee) delivered >50% of new cardholders under 40. The reason: the experiential/status appeal of Platinum (Centurion Lounges, Equinox membership, Walmart+/Uber/Resy credits) resonates strongly with high-earning young consumers. (4) **Spending grows above GDP every cycle**: Card Member spending Q1 2026 +7% FX-adj with US GDP +2.5% = AmEx gaining share of the premium wallet. Corporate T&E +6%, restaurant spend +9%, international travel +13%. (5) **Membership Rewards anchor = >$13B liability + 67M US premium cards**: the points represent money already "paid" by members who haven't redeemed yet. It's a free float that finances operations. And the lock-in is real — members with balances >50k points have <2% annual churn. (6) **Capital return ~9% market cap annually**: $20B buyback TTM (~3% of market cap) + ~1% dividend. Plus dividend raises: $0.70→$0.82 March 2025 (+17%). FY26 guide EPS $15.20-$15.60 vs. FY25 $14.42 = +6-8% — buybacks make per-share growth exceed that rate. (7) **Centurion Lounges expansion + Tock integration**: 30+ Centurion Lounges globally expanding to Doha + new LAX terminal in 2026. Tock (acquired Q2 2024 for $400M) integrated with Resy = AmEx controls 60%+ of high-end US restaurant reservations. A real experiential moat. (8) **International Card Services +12% FX-adj Q1 2026**: Mexico +18%, Japan +15%, India growing fast (joint venture with SBI Card). 30+ countries, structural expansion. (9) **Provisions normalized at ~$1.2B Q1 2026**: excellent credit performance — net write-off ratio 2.1% (vs. peer Discover 3.8%, Capital One 4.5%). AmEx's prime/super-prime customer base (average FICO 760+) means less recession sensitivity. (10) **Thesis in one line**: buy the world's #1 premium card issuer with (a) a structural closed-loop moat, (b) accelerating millennial/Gen Z demographics, (c) ~9% capital return, (d) Q1 2026 +9% EPS + 18% card fees, (e) a reasonable forward P/E of 18.5x vs. pure network peers (Visa 28x, Mastercard 32x).
AXP.summary_risk: The risks are measurable: (1) **Recession hits consumer + corporate spending**: AmEx derives ~70% of revenue from transactional fees + spending. A hard recession reduces corporate T&E (Commercial Services typically -10-15%) and consumer travel/restaurant spend (-5-10%). COVID 2020: revenue -17%, EPS -77%. Even with a prime customer base, AmEx is not immune to the cycle. (2) **Delayed Platinum churn**: fee raised to $895 (from $695). Q1 2026 showed no churn, but the real test is the renewal cycle (12 months post-refresh = September-December 2026). If churn exceeds 5%, the narrative takes a hit. Plus competitive response — Chase Sapphire Reserve fee refreshed in December 2025 to $795, directly attacking AmEx. (3) **Closed-loop under regulatory pressure**: Durbin Amendment 2.0 proposed in US Congress — if passed, could force networks to accept a second router on credit transactions (already the case for debit). AmEx's closed-loop could be exempted or forced to open. Low probability but material tail risk. (4) **Apple Card + fintech alternatives**: Apple Card (Goldman partnership) + Apple Pay Later + Klarna + Affirm are capturing entry-level share. AmEx US Consumer no-fee cards (EveryDay, Cash Magnet) are growing slowly. A long-term funnel hit. (5) **MDR pricing pressure**: large merchants (Costco — lost in 2016, Walmart, Starbucks) push for lower merchant discount rates. AmEx already conceded Costco; if more national merchants push back, MDR could fall from ~2.4% to ~2.2% = a $1B+ revenue hit. (6) **Credit cycle normalization**: provisions Q1 2026 normalized to $1.2B vs. pandemic-era $0.5B. If delinquencies rise faster (Gen Z cards), provisions could jump to $1.5B+. (7) **FX exposure ~25%**: International Card Services ~22% revenue. A strengthening dollar = reported revenue suffers. Q1 2026 already: a -2% FX drag on total revenue. (8) **Stephen Squeri succession uncertainty**: CEO since 2018, age 65 in 2026. If he retires in H2 2026 or 2027, transition risk. Christophe Le Caillec (CFO) or Doug Buckminster are logical successors but no announcement made. (9) **Membership Rewards liability acceleration**: $13B liability growing +12% YoY. If the redemption rate increases (members redeeming more for travel), cash burn accelerates. Historically AmEx maintains ~70% utilization. (10) **Valuation forward P/E 18.5x vs. Visa 28x / Mastercard 32x**: the discount vs. pure networks is justified (AmEx carries credit risk; V/MA do not). But the gap may also reflect skepticism about closed-loop sustainability. Re-rating depends on consistent delivery of $15.20-$15.60 EPS in FY26 + a clean Platinum renewal cycle.
