CEG.research_full:
# Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG) — Full Research

## Price: $295.49 | P/E TTM: 40.0 | P/E Forward: 21.5 | Div Yield: 0.57% | Market Cap: $107B

---

## What It Is

Constellation Energy is the **#1 electricity producer in the United States** since it closed the purchase of Calpine on **January 7, 2026** for $16.4B. It operates 55 GW of combined capacity — **~10% of all carbon-free electricity in the country**, enough to power approximately 27 million homes.

It was spun off from Exelon in February 2022 (CEG took the generation; Exelon kept the regulated distribution). HQ in Baltimore, Maryland.

## Generation Mix Post-Calpine

| Source | Capacity | % of fleet |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Nuclear** | ~22 GW | 40% |
| **Natural Gas** (ex-Calpine) | ~27 GW | 49% |
| **Wind / Solar / Hydro** | ~6 GW | 11% |

## Largest Nuclear Fleet in the US

Operates 13 nuclear sites with 21 reactors. The main ones:

- **Crane Clean Energy Center** (ex-Three Mile Island Unit 1) — 835 MW, restart in process, 20-year PPA with **Microsoft**
- Byron, Braidwood, Clinton (Illinois)
- Dresden, LaSalle, Quad Cities (Illinois)
- Peach Bottom, Limerick (Pennsylvania)
- Nine Mile Point, FitzPatrick, Ginna (New York)
- Salem, Hope Creek (New Jersey — joint ownership)

## The Catalyst: Microsoft + AI

In September 2024, Constellation signed a **20-year Power Purchase Agreement (PPA)** with Microsoft for all production from Three Mile Island's Unit 1 (now Crane Clean Energy Center). First time in history a hyperscaler signs a contract of this size to reactivate a retired nuclear reactor.

### Why It Matters
AI data centers need **clean baseload 24/7**. Solar + wind don't work because they're intermittent. Nuclear is the only source that:
1. Doesn't emit CO2 (Microsoft's net-zero goals for 2030)
2. Produces constant energy day and night
3. Has a capacity factor >90% (vs 25% solar, 35% wind)

### The Pattern Expands
- **Amazon** bought a data center next to the Susquehanna nuclear plant (Talen Energy) for $650M
- **Meta** signed with Constellation in June 2025 a 20-year extension for the Clinton reactor in Illinois
- **Google** is in advanced negotiations with several operators

CEG is the obvious leader in this area due to the size of its nuclear fleet.

## FY2025 Results and 2026 Guidance

| Metric | FY2025 | FY2026 guidance |
|---------|--------|-----------------|
| **Adjusted EPS** | ~$7.39 | **$11.00 – $12.00** |
| **Midpoint growth** | — | **+55%** YoY |
| **Analyst consensus** | — | $11.60 |
| **Free Cash Flow** | ~$2.5B | Growing |

Guidance midpoint ($11.50) was slightly below the consensus ($11.60) — one reason for the recent sell-off.

## Calpine Acquisition — Closed Jan 7, 2026

- **Price**: $16.4B (cash + stock)
- **Adds**: 33 GW of natural gas + renewables
- **Location**: Texas, California, Mid-Atlantic
- **Strategic logic**: gas peakers complement nuclear baseload — necessary to balance peak demand from data centers
- **Integration**: ongoing during 2026

## Why Now

### 1. The Drop is Technical, Not Structural
- 52w High: **$412.70**
- Current price: **$295.49** → **-28% from highs**
- Reason for the drop:
  - Delay of TMI-Crane restart to ~2031 due to PJM transmission bottlenecks
  - 2026 guidance slightly below consensus
  - Digesting the Calpine acquisition
- **The AI + nuclear thesis remains intact** — Microsoft hasn't canceled the contract, only the delivery date is pushed

### 2. Irreplaceable Structural Position
- 22 GW of nuclear already operating (impossible to build new in <10 years)
- 55 GW total post-Calpine = #1 in the country
- Unique mix: nuclear + gas peakers + renewables = exactly what data centers need

### 3. AI Demand is Real and Growing
- Global data center spending projected: $500B+ in 2026
- Hyperscalers (MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN, META) are signing 20-year nuclear contracts
- US has a structural deficit of clean baseload

### 4. DOE and IRA Floor
- $1B DOE loan guarantee for Crane restart
- Nuclear Production Tax Credit from the Inflation Reduction Act supports revenue

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Price** | $295.49 |
| **Market Cap** | $107.05B |
| **P/E TTM** | 39.99x |
| **P/E Forward** | 21.51x |
| **EPS TTM** | $7.39 |
| **EPS Forward** | $13.74 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 0.57% |
| **Dividend Rate** | $1.551 |
| **52w High** | $412.70 |
| **52w Low** | $188.00 |
| **Drawdown from high** | -28% |
| **Shares Outstanding** | 362M |

## Risks

### 1. TMI-Crane Delay to 2031
PJM transmission bottlenecks could push the reactor's grid connection date. Microsoft remains signed, but revenue flow is delayed.

### 2. Calpine Integration
$16.4B acquisition closed 3 months ago. Promised synergies yet to materialize.

### 3. Valuation
Forward price vs. profit of 21.5x is high for a traditional utility. Only justified if the AI story materializes.

### 4. Political Risk of Nuclear PTC
The nuclear Production Tax Credit in the IRA is subject to review. A Congress that eliminates or reduces it hits the revenue floor.

### 5. Natural Gas Prices
Calpine's fleet is primarily gas. Big price swings in gas affect margins.

### 6. Borrowing Costs
Capital-intensive utility — sensitive to interest rate hikes. Significant debt post-Calpine.

## Conclusion

CEG at $295.49 allows you to buy the #1 US utility at a 28% discount from highs, when:
1. They just became the largest electricity producer in the country (55 GW post-Calpine)
2. They have the most talked-about nuclear contract in history with Microsoft (20-year PPA)
3. The drop is due to timing (TMI restart delayed to 2031) not a broken thesis
4. The nuclear + gas + renewables mix is exactly what AI data centers need
5. $1B DOE guarantee + nuclear PTC = structural revenue floor

The boring business (electricity for 27M homes) anchors. The exciting business (fuel for the AI era) provides the upside.

---

*Research date: April 17, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
CEG.one_liner: Constellation Energy is a major US clean electricity producer working on a key project with Microsoft, but its stock dropped 28% due to delays.
CEG.why_short: They expect to earn much more in 2026, but the stock fell because the project with Microsoft was delayed. Still, they have a unique mix of nuclear, gas, and renewables perfect for tech companies.
CEG.risk_short: The delay of the project with Microsoft to 2031 caused the stock to drop. There's also concern about integrating a recent acquisition and political changes affecting their tax benefits.

CICHY.summary_short: The second largest bank in the world by assets (~$4 trillion USD). At a price vs. profit of 6x, it's priced as if it's going bankrupt — but it pays a 4.89% dividend, is backed by the Chinese state, and its earnings grow 3–5% annually. It's crucial for Beijing. The discount is due to geopolitical reasons, not financial fundamentals.
CICHY.summary_what: China Construction Bank (建设银行) is one of China's 'Big Four' state-owned banks. Founded in 1954, it now operates over 14,000 branches in China and about 30 international offices. Controlled by Central Huijin Investment (~57%), the sovereign arm of the Chinese state. It has three main areas: (1) Corporate banking — infrastructure financing, small businesses, large state-owned enterprises, and central/provincial government; (2) Retail banking — the #1 bank for residential mortgages in China, as well as accounts, credit cards, wealth management; (3) Treasury and capital markets. The ADR CICHY trades on OTC Markets (OTCPK) as an unsponsored ADR Level I — 1 ADR represents 25 H-shares (Hong Kong, ticker 00939.HK). HQ in Beijing. CEO: Zhang Jinliang (since 2022). Payout ratio: ~30% — it distributes about one-third of its profits as an annual dividend.
CICHY.summary_why: At a price vs. profit of 5.95x and a 4.89% dividend paid in USD, CICHY is priced as if the Chinese banking system is about to collapse. But the reality is more nuanced: (1) Chinese regulators (NFRA) and the PBOC have already forced banks to aggressively set aside losses from the real estate sector (Evergrande, Country Garden, Sunac) during 2022–2024 — the worst of the cycle is already on the books; (2) Net interest margin is stabilizing at 1.5–1.6% after three years of decline; (3) Beijing has explicitly stated it will NOT let systemic banks fail — the sovereign backing is structural, not hypothetical; (4) Profits grow 3–5% annually even in an economic slowdown; (5) A conservative payout ratio (~30%) leaves room to maintain the dividend even if profits fall. The contrast with peers: JPMorgan earns $50B annually and trades at a price vs. profit of 12x. CCB earns ~$58B annually and trades at a price vs. profit of 6x. The difference is 100% geopolitical — if CCB were listed in Frankfurt, it would trade at double the price. The market over-discounts the risk of delisting Chinese ADRs, when the alternative route (converting CICHY to Hong Kong H-shares) is technically possible for most brokers.
CICHY.summary_risk: The main risk is geopolitical — an escalation in US-China tensions could result in the delisting of Chinese ADRs from US exchanges (as happened with DIDI in 2022) or secondary sanctions that freeze the liquidity of the OTC ADR. Other risks: residual exposure to the Chinese real estate sector via mortgages and developer loans; net interest margin pressured by low rates from the PBOC; Chinese state regulatory risk that could force dividends down or require subsidized loans to strategic companies; limited accounting transparency (unsponsored ADR, no formal SEC reporting); currency risk CNY/USD; limited liquidity of the ADR on OTC Markets (wide bid/ask spreads in times of stress); risk of individual bank sanction under OFAC lists if tensions with Taiwan arise.
CICHY.research_full:
# China Construction Bank (CICHY) — Full Research

## Price: $22.66 | P/E TTM: 5.95 | Div Yield: 4.89% | Market Cap: $304B

---

## What It Is

China Construction Bank (建设银行, 'Jiàn Shè Yín Háng') is one of the **'Big Four' state-owned banks in China**:

1. Industrial and Commercial Bank of China (ICBC) — #1 by assets
2. **China Construction Bank (CCB)** — #2
3. Agricultural Bank of China (ABC)
4. Bank of China (BOC)

Total assets: **~$4 trillion USD** (more than JPMorgan + Bank of America combined).

Founded in 1954 as a state bank to finance infrastructure. Partially privatized with dual IPO in Hong Kong (2005) and Shanghai (2007). Central Huijin Investment (sovereign arm of the state) holds ~57% of the shares.

## The ADR CICHY

- **Ticker**: CICHY
- **Exchange**: OTC Markets (OTCPK) — Pink Sheets
- **Type**: ADR Level I **unsponsored**
- **Ratio**: 1 ADR = 25 H-shares (00939.HK)
- **Custodian**: Citibank N.A.

'Unsponsored' means CCB did not directly issue the ADR — a custodian bank created it by bundling H-shares bought in Hong Kong. **Implication**: CCB does not report to the SEC under 20-F. Official reports come from Hong Kong/Shanghai under HKEX/SSE.

## Business Lines

| Segment | % of revenue | Notes |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| **Corporate banking** | ~50% | Infrastructure, SOEs, SMEs |
| **Retail banking** | ~35% | Mortgages (#1 in China), accounts, cards |
| **Treasury/market** | ~15% | Trading, wealth management |

## The Context: Why It's Cheap

### The Real Estate Overhang (2021–2024)
- Evergrande technical bankruptcy in 2021
- Country Garden, Sunac, Fantasia, Shimao — all with default or restructuring
- New home sales in China: -50% vs peak (2021 → 2024)

### How It Impacted CCB
- Provisions for real estate loans: +$40B in 2022–2024
- NPL ratio (non-performing loans): rose from 1.4% to 1.8%
- Residential mortgages: exposure ~$1.2T USD (largest among Chinese banks)
- Chinese real estate stocks: -70% in that period

### The Regulatory Reset
The Chinese regulator (NFRA, formerly CBIRC) forced the Big Four to:
1. Aggressively provision (NPL coverage ratio >200%)
2. Refinance existing mortgages at lower rates (~100 bps average reduction)
3. Increase Tier 1 capital for buffer
4. Participate in rescue funds (Baotou, Tianjin, Guizhou LGFVs)

### 2025 Outcome
- NIM stabilized at 1.52% (vs 1.82% in 2022)
- Net income 2025: ~$58B USD (vs $55B in 2024 → +5.4%)
- NPL ratio fell to 1.35%
- Provisions normalizing

## Investment Thesis

### 1. The Worst Case Is Already Priced In
P/E 5.95x with div yield 4.89% implies:
- Either the bank is about to fail (it isn't — it's systemic)
- Or the market demands a geopolitical risk premium of 50-60% vs Western banks

### 2. Beijing Won't Let CCB Fail
- Central Huijin owns 57% — it's the state
- The Big Four are monetary policy vehicles of the PBOC
- 'Too big to fail' with Chinese characteristics: the Chinese state literally IS the largest shareholder

### 3. Dividends Paid in USD
- CCB distributes an annual dividend, paid in CNY but converted to USD for ADR
- In 2025: $1.11 per ADR → yield 4.89% at $22.66
- Payout ratio: ~30% (conservative)
- Consistency: 19 consecutive years paying a dividend

### 4. Comparative Valuation

| Bank | P/E | Div Yield | ROE |
|-------|-----|-----------|-----|
| **JPMorgan (JPM)** | 12x | 2.1% | 17% |
| **Bank of America (BAC)** | 11x | 2.5% | 11% |
| **HSBC (HSBC)** | 7x | 6.8% | 13% |
| **Santander (SAN)** | 7x | 4.1% | 13% |
| **CCB (CICHY)** | **6x** | **4.9%** | **11%** |

CCB trades 50% cheaper than JPM with similar ROE and twice the dividend.

### 5. Potential Catalysts
- Stabilization of the Chinese real estate market (Q1 2026 showed first +YoY in 3 years)
- Beijing's fiscal stimulus policies (active since Q4 2025)
- Easing of trade tensions post-US elections 2026
- Voluntary conversion of ADR to H-shares for investors wanting to eliminate OTC risk

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **ADR Price** | $22.66 |
| **Market Cap** | $304B |
| **P/E TTM** | 5.95x |
| **EPS TTM** | $3.81 (per ADR) |
| **Dividend Yield** | 4.89% |
| **ADR Dividend** | $1.11 |
| **Total Assets** | ~$4.0T |
| **Total Loans** | ~$2.6T |
| **NPL Ratio** | 1.35% |
| **NIM** | 1.52% |
| **ROE** | 11.1% |
| **Tier 1 Capital** | 14.2% |
| **52w High** | $22.73 |
| **52w Low** | $16.30 |

## Risks

### 1. Geopolitical Risk (Main)
- US-China tensions could lead to **delisting of Chinese ADRs** (like DIDI in 2022)
- OFAC secondary sanctions against Chinese banks if there's tension over Taiwan
- Mitigation: ADRs can be converted to H-shares in Hong Kong (requires broker with Hong Kong access)

### 2. Residual Real Estate Exposure
- Still ~15% of loan book linked to real estate
- If the Chinese real estate rebound fails, a new cycle of provisions

### 3. Accounting Transparency
- Unsponsored ADR: does not report 20-F to the SEC
- Official reports in Mandarin + English from HKEX — 2x a year (interim + annual)
- Risk of typical Chinese bank accounting window dressing (dynamic provisioning can inflate profits)

### 4. ADR Liquidity
- Average daily volume: ~200,000 ADRs = ~$4.5M
- Bid/ask spread: 10–20 bps under normal conditions, 50+ bps in stress
- Not suitable for large quick trades — limit orders are necessary

### 5. Currency Risk (CNY → USD)
- Dividend is paid in CNY, converted to USD at payment time
- Weak CNY reduces USD dividend
- PBOC has a managed fluctuation band — less volatile than typical emerging markets

### 6. Chinese Regulatory Risk
- Beijing can force:
  - Loans to strategic companies without commercial return
  - Dividend cuts to preserve capital
  - Forced mergers or restructurings

### 7. NIM Pressured by Rates
- PBOC is lowering LPR (Loan Prime Rate) to stimulate the economy
- CCB's NIM compression ~20 bps annually since 2022
- Already hitting structural floor but no tailwind in the next 2 years

## How the OTC ADR Works

- Buy CICHY through your broker (Schwab, Fidelity, IBKR, TD — all list it)
- Citibank as custodian holds real H-shares in Hong Kong backing each ADR
- Trades during NY hours 9:30–16:00 ET
- ADR price = H-share price × 25 × (CNY→USD FX) — less OTC spread
- Dividend: paid by CCB in CNY to Citibank → Citibank converts to USD → deposits in your account

## Conclusion

CICHY at $22.66 lets you buy the second largest bank in the world with:
1. P/E 6x (50% discount vs Western peers with similar fundamentals)
2. 4.89% dividend paid in USD, supported by a 30% payout ratio
3. Explicit sovereign backing from the Chinese state (Huijin 57%)
4. Profits growing 3–5% annually even in a slowdown
5. The worst real estate scenario already priced in

The discount is NOT due to fundamentals — it's geopolitical. If the thesis is 'China doesn't collapse and Beijing won't let CCB fail,' at this price the math is asymmetric. If the thesis is 'Taiwan escalates, CICHY gets delisted,' then there's a binary loss scenario — but even then, converting to H-shares preserves exposure.

**It's not a stock for everyone.** It's for those who understand the trade-off between cheap valuation and geopolitical tail risk.

---

*Research date: Apr 20, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
CICHY.one_liner: China Construction Bank is the world's second-largest bank, backed by the Chinese government, offering nearly 5% dividend, but its low price is due to political tensions.
CICHY.why_short: The bank is cheap because people fear a collapse of the Chinese banking system, but it's backed by the Chinese government and its earnings continue to grow. They've also taken steps to address issues in the real estate sector.
CICHY.risk_short: The biggest risk is political: if US-China tensions rise, the bank could be removed from US stock exchanges, making it harder to sell shares easily.

CNI.summary_what: Canadian National Railway operates the most integrated rail network in North America: from Halifax and Montréal on the Atlantic, through Chicago and Memphis in the Midwest, to Vancouver and Prince Rupert on the Pacific, with direct access to the Gulf of Mexico via New Orleans and Mobile. It has about 19,500 miles of track, moving over 300 million tons annually. Volume mix: intermodal/containers (25%), grains and fertilizers (19%), forest products (13%), petrochemicals (13%), metals and minerals (12%), cars (6%), coal (5%), others (7%). Main clients include Cargill, Suncor, Ford, Toyota, Nutrien, ExxonMobil, and potash producers in Saskatchewan. It operates in a regulated railway oligopoly in Canada by the Canadian Transportation Agency and in the US by the Surface Transportation Board, with insurmountable entry barriers (environmental permits, agreements with indigenous peoples, zoning). HQ in Montreal, Quebec. CEO Tracy Robinson since 2022.
CNI.summary_why: CNI at $110.26 is just 2.5% below the 52-week high ($113.09) — this is not a discount, it's validation. The market acknowledges an operational turnaround: Tracy Robinson has stabilized the operating ratio after the regulatory mishap with the failed KCS acquisition by CP Rail, Q4 2025 showed volume growth in grain and intermodal, and the 2026 guidance points to expanding how much they keep per sale. The structural piece: the Pacific corridor via Prince Rupert is growing in importance for exports to Asia (LNG Canada shipping from Kitimat, Saskatchewan potash, Canadian grains). A dividend aristocrat — it has increased dividends for 29 consecutive years, current yield 2.38% with a sustainable payout ratio. Price vs. profit forward 17.3x is in line with the historical average of the railway sector. The moat is one of the most enduring in equity — Buffett has BNSF for the same reason: you can't build new transcontinental tracks today. CN is the only Class I railway with access to all three oceans, and that moat only deepens over time.
CNI.summary_risk: The main risk is cyclical exposure to Canadian commodities — a downturn in grain, potash, or forest product prices compresses volumes and how much they keep per sale. Other risks: recurring operational disruptions (wildfires in British Columbia, Teamsters Canada Rail union strikes like in August 2024, derailments); sensitivity to oil prices (crude-by-rail is only economical when the WCS-WTI spread is wide); competition from intermodal trucking (especially on short routes); CAD/USD exchange rate risk for dollar investors; emerging regulation on emissions and rail safety post-East Palestine (2023); Canada-US tariffs under USMCA review in 2026 could reduce cross-border volumes.

# Canadian National Railway (CNI) — Full Research

## Price: $110.26 | P/E TTM: 20.0 | P/E Forward: 17.3 | Div Yield: 2.38% | Market Cap: $67.5B

---

## What It Is

Canadian National Railway is the **only Class I railway in North America that connects all three oceans** — Atlantic, Pacific, and Gulf of Mexico. HQ in Montreal, Quebec. It operates about 19,500 miles of track across 8 Canadian provinces and 16 US states, moving over 300 million tons annually.

It was privatized in 1995 (IPO in Toronto + NYSE). Since then, it has been one of the most consistent compounding machines in the Canadian market.

## 3-Ocean Network

| Coast | Main Ports |
|-------|---------------------|
| **Atlantic** | Halifax (Nova Scotia), Montreal |
| **Pacific** | Vancouver, Prince Rupert (British Columbia) |
| **Gulf of Mexico** | New Orleans, Mobile |

Prince Rupert is strategic: it is the closest deep-water port to Asia in North America (2-3 days faster than Los Angeles). For Canadian exports to China, Japan, and South Korea, the CN-Prince Rupert corridor is the optimal route.

## Cargo Mix (2025)

| Segment | % of Revenue |
|----------|---------------|
| **Intermodal (containers)** | 25% |
| **Grains and fertilizers** | 19% |
| **Forest products** | 13% |
| **Oil and chemicals** | 13% |
| **Metals and minerals** | 12% |
| **Cars** | 6% |
| **Coal** | 5% |
| **Others** | 7% |

## Thesis: Unrepeatable Geographic Moat

### Why You Can't Build a New Railway
1. **Environmental permits** — decades to approve a new transcontinental track
2. **Indigenous peoples (First Nations)** — mandatory consultation rights, lengthy negotiations
3. **Zoning** — current tracks were laid when urban centers were small; today they pass through cities
4. **Cost of capital** — trillions of dollars in 2026 to replicate the network

Result: CN, CP (Canadian Pacific), UP (Union Pacific), BNSF, and NS (Norfolk Southern) operate in a **5-player oligopoly** with structural pricing power.

### Warren Buffett Has BNSF for This Reason
Berkshire bought BNSF in 2010 for $44B (Buffett's largest purchase at the time). The logic: railway = regional monopoly asset + low maintenance capital expenses + predictable cash coming in + impossible to disrupt. CN is the Canadian equivalent.

## Operational Turnaround with Tracy Robinson (CEO since 2022)

### Operating Ratio Stabilized
- OR 2022: 61.8% (before Robinson)
- OR 2023: 59.4%
- OR 2024: 58.1%
- OR Q4 2025: 57.2%

Each point of OR ≈ $150M of additional operating income.

### Volume Growing
- Intermodal: +8% YoY (Q4 2025)
- Grain: +6% YoY
- Cars: +12% YoY (Ontario ↔ Mexico / US crossing)

### 2026 Guidance
- EPS growth: +7% to +9% YoY
- Free cash flow: $4.5B+ (vs $4.1B in 2025)
- Dividend increase: +5% announced for Q1 2026 (30th consecutive year)

## Dividend Aristocrat

| Year | Annual Dividend (USD) |
|-----|------------------------|
| 1996 | $0.12 |
| 2006 | $0.53 |
| 2016 | $1.22 |
| 2026 (projected) | $2.63 |

**29 consecutive years of increasing dividends.** Only 3 Canadian companies have that record.

## Why Now

### 1. Price Near 52w High is a Signal, Not a Problem
- 52w Low: $90.74
- 52w High: $113.09
- Current: $110.26 (-2.5% from high)

The market is validating the turnaround. Quality buyers enter on positive momentum, not waiting for "the bottom."

### 2. Structural Exposure to Asia
- LNG Canada (Kitimat, BC) began exports in 2025 — rail flow of inputs and products grows
- Canadian grains to China, Japan, Korea (~$8B annually)
- Saskatchewan potash (Nutrien) → Asia via Prince Rupert
- Japanese cars imported via Vancouver → distribution in North America

### 3. Sectoral Diversification
Balanced mix: a drop in coal doesn't kill you because it's only 5%. A recession in cars is offset by grain. The whole is less volatile than any comparable.

### 4. Disciplined Capital Return
- $4B buyback active
- Dividend yield 2.38% with payout ratio ~45%
- ROIC > WACC consistent since 2000

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Price** | $110.26 |
| **Market Cap** | $67.5B |
| **P/E TTM** | 20.0x |
| **P/E Forward** | 17.3x |
| **EPS TTM** | $5.51 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 2.38% |
| **52w High** | $113.09 |
| **52w Low** | $90.74 |
| **Operating Ratio Q4 2025** | 57.2% |
| **FCF 2025** | ~$4.1B |

## Risks

### 1. Cyclical Canadian Commodities
A downturn in grains, potash, or forest products hits volumes. In 2020 (COVID), revenue fell 10%.

### 2. Operational Disruptions
- Wildfires in British Columbia (2021, 2023): tracks interrupted for weeks
- Teamsters Canada Rail strike (August 2024): 9 days of complete stoppage
- Derailments (regulatory risk post-East Palestine)

### 3. Intermodal Trucking Competition
On short routes (<500 miles), trucks are more flexible. CN compensates with fuel prices: every $1/barrel of diesel makes rail more competitive.

### 4. Currency Risk
CNI trades in USD but generates revenue in CAD + USD. A weak CAD reduces reported value.

### 5. USMCA Review 2026
The Canada-Mexico-US trade agreement enters review. New tariffs would reduce cross-border volumes.

### 6. Emerging Regulation
Canada proposed stricter emissions for locomotives in 2025. Compliance capital expenses ~$800M over the next 5 years.

## Conclusion

CNI at $110.26 lets you buy the only North American railway with access to 3 oceans, amid:
1. A validated operational turnaround (lowering OR, increasing volume)
2. An impossible-to-replicate geographic moat in 2026
3. A 29-year track record of increasing dividends
4. Structural exposure to Asian exports from Prince Rupert
5. Reasonable valuation (P/E forward 17.3x in line with historical)

It's not a "value play" — it's quality at a fair price. In railways, that's all you can ask for.

---

*Research date: April 20, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
CNI.one_liner: Canadian National Railway links the Atlantic, Pacific, and Gulf of Mexico, transporting grains and oil. It has raised shareholder payouts for 29 years and is near its highest value in a year.
CNI.why_short: The stock's value is near its peak because the company has bounced back well from past issues. It now moves more cargo and expects further improvement. Plus, it has increased shareholder payouts for 29 years straight.
CNI.risk_short: The biggest risk is if prices for products like grains or oil drop, the company might earn less. It also faces challenges like fires, strikes, and changes in safety regulations.

Every dollar entering these ETFs generates recurring custody fees for COIN; (2) USDC interest income: Circle shares ~50% of interest earned on USDC reserves (~$60B supply) — at 4-5% rates, that's $900M annually of SaaS-like recurring income, independent of trading volume; (3) Coinbase One and staking build predictable subscription income; (4) Regulatory moat: they are the ONLY US exchange with all licenses, making them the default partner for any financial institution seeking regulated crypto exposure (BlackRock, Fidelity, Grayscale, ARK, Invesco, Franklin Templeton); (5) The GENIUS Act signed in 2025 legitimized stablecoins and accelerated institutional adoption; (6) Base — their Ethereum L2 — processes more daily transactions than the Ethereum mainnet. Income critically depends on: (1) Retail trading volume — which drops ~60% in crypto bear markets (2022 was brutal); (2) Bitcoin and Ethereum prices — although Coinbase has diversified, still ~45% of revenue is transactional, so a crypto winter squeezes profits; (3) Changing regulations — the SEC sued Coinbase in 2023, accusing them of operating an unregistered securities exchange; although Coinbase won the case in 2024, new administrations could reopen issues; (4) Competition from Binance, Kraken, Robinhood Crypto, and even traditional finance entering (Fidelity Crypto, Charles Schwab); (5) Dependence on Circle for USDC — if Circle changes the reserve agreement terms or USDC loses market share to Tether, that ~$900M annual SaaS-like revenue is at risk; (6) Valuation: price vs. profit at 47x and forward 40x are expensive — any earnings miss in a bad crypto quarter could drop the stock 30%+ in a day; (7) Hacks or operational failures — although it has never been hacked at the custody level, such an event would be existential.
COIN.research_full:
# Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) — Full Research

## Price: $208.58 | P/E TTM: 47.0 | P/E Forward: 40.6 | Div Yield: 0% | Market Cap: $56.2B

---

## What It Is

Coinbase Global is **the largest and most regulated crypto exchange in the US** — the only one listed on Nasdaq (COIN) with full licenses from SEC, CFTC, FinCEN, NYDFS (BitLicense), and in all 50 US states. Founded in 2012 by Brian Armstrong and Fred Ehrsam. Direct IPO in April 2021 at $381 per share.

Over 110 million verified users. ~$200B+ in assets under custody. Holds Bitcoin for **8 of the 11 SEC-approved Bitcoin spot ETFs**.

## Business Lines

| Segment | % of Revenue | Description |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Transaction revenue** | ~45% | Fees from trades in the spot market (retail + pro) |
| **Subscription & services** | ~40% | USDC interest, staking, Coinbase One, Prime, Custody |
| **Blockchain rewards** | ~8% | Proof-of-stake validation (ETH, SOL, ADA, ATOM) |
| **Corporate interest** | ~5% | Earnings on cash and crypto balance |
| **Other** | ~2% | Base (L2), venture investments |

### The Critical Shift: From Exchange to Infrastructure

Historically, Coinbase was ~85% transactional — when crypto prices fell, volume fell, and COIN plummeted. Since 2023, that profile has changed drastically:

1. **USDC interest**: Coinbase has an agreement with Circle (USDC issuer) giving it ~50% of the interest earned on the stablecoin's reserves. With USDC supply ~$60B and rates at 4-5%, that's ~$900M/year in recurring income INDEPENDENT of crypto prices.
2. **Custody fees**: The 8 BTC ETFs (BlackRock IBIT, Fidelity FBTC, Ark ARKB, Invesco BTCO, etc.) and 5 ETH ETFs trade ~$90B+ AUM total. Coinbase charges ~0.05-0.10% annually for custody → ~$50-90M/year recurring.
3. **Coinbase One**: Subscription $29.99/month with unlimited trades, higher staking returns, and priority support. 1M+ subscribers → ~$360M/year.

## The Regulatory Moat

Obtaining the licenses Coinbase holds today costs **5+ years and $1B+ in legal and compliance**:

- NY BitLicense (granted 2017)
- Money Transmitter License in 50 states
- SEC registration as an Alternative Trading System
- CFTC registration as a Derivatives Clearing Organization
- MiCA authorization in the EU (2025)
- Licenses in Singapore, Canada, UK, Ireland, Brazil

This makes Coinbase the **default regulated partner** for any institution wanting crypto exposure:

- **BlackRock** chose Coinbase to custody IBIT (the largest BTC ETF with ~$60B AUM)
- **Fidelity** custodies FBTC with Coinbase
- **Invesco, ARK, Franklin Templeton, Bitwise, Hashdex, WisdomTree** — all use Coinbase
- **PayPal** uses Coinbase Crypto API to offer BTC/ETH to its users
- **Stripe** uses Coinbase to settle payments in USDC

## USDC and the Circle Agreement

In 2018, Coinbase and Circle co-founded the Centre Consortium to issue USDC. In 2023, the consortium dissolved, and Circle took over direct issuance, but the commercial agreement remains:

- Circle manages USDC reserves (short-term Treasury bonds)
- Circle earns interest on these reserves (~4-5% annually currently)
- **Circle pays ~50% of that interest to Coinbase** for distributing and promoting USDC

Current USDC supply: ~$60B
Aggregate annual interest: ~$2.4-3B
Coinbase's share: **~$900M-1.2B/year**

This is equivalent to SaaS-like income — it doesn't depend on crypto prices, only on the circulating supply of USDC.

## Base — Coinbase's L2

In 2023, Coinbase launched **Base**, its Ethereum L2 built with Optimism OP Stack. Base today:

- **1M+ daily transactions** (more than Ethereum mainnet most days)
- **$5B+ TVL** (Total Value Locked)
- COIN token captures sequencer fees (~$100M annually already)
- It's the preferred L2 for major projects like Farcaster, Aerodrome, and consumer onchain projects

Base transforms Coinbase from "exchange" to "crypto rails infrastructure" — similar to AWS's pivot from "hosting" to "cloud computing".

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Price** | $208.58 |
| **Market Cap** | $56.2B |
| **P/E TTM** | 47.0x |
| **P/E Forward** | 40.6x |
| **EPS TTM** | $4.44 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 0% |
| **52w High** | $444.65 |
| **52w Low** | $139.36 |
| **Drawdown from high** | -53% |
| **Revenue 2025** | ~$7.1B |
| **Adjusted EBITDA 2025** | ~$2.8B |
| **Cash + crypto on balance** | ~$8B |
| **Assets under custody** | ~$200B |

## Thesis: The Nasdaq/DTCC of Crypto

Coinbase's 10-year vision isn't to be the largest crypto exchange — it's to be **the regulated infrastructure on which the entire onchain economy in the West is built**.

### Analogy: DTCC + Nasdaq + Visa
- **DTCC** holds $88T+ in US securities — charges small fees per transaction → durable, non-cyclical business
- **Nasdaq** operates the trading market and charges listing + trading fees
- **Visa** connects banks and merchants and charges ~0.1% per swipe

Coinbase wants that combination applied to crypto. And it's already on its way:
- Custody (DTCC) ✓
- Exchange (Nasdaq) ✓
- Settlement + USDC rails (Visa) ✓
- L2 (Base) — new piece

## Why Now

### 1. Price at a -53% Discount
The 52w high was $444. Today it's $208. The drop wasn't due to fundamentals — it was due to: (a) growth-to-value rotation in Q1 2026; (b) recession fears; (c) BTC correction to $78K from $108K. Business fundamentals didn't change.

### 2. GENIUS Act Signed (2025)
The law creating the federal regulatory framework for stablecoins passed in November 2025. This legitimizes USDC (and by extension, Coinbase's revenue stream).

### 3. ETH ETFs Approved (July 2024)
Coinbase holds ETH for all spot Ethereum ETFs. Combined AUM: ~$12B and growing.

### 4. International Expansion
Recent launches in the EU (MiCA), Australia, Brazil, Singapore adding geographies with fast crypto growth.

### 5. Growing Earnings
Q4 2025: EPS $1.26 vs $1.05 consensus. Revenue $1.92B (+44% YoY).
Q1 2026: projected $1.45B revenue, EPS $0.89.

## Risks

### 1. Cyclical Trading Volume
In crypto bear markets (2022 was brutal), volume dropped 60% and Coinbase reported quarterly losses. Although the mix is now more defensive, ~45% of revenue is still transactional.

### 2. Dependence on USDC / Circle
If Circle renegotiates the interest split, changes the reserve custodian, or USDC loses market share to Tether/PYUSD/RLUSD → the ~$900M/year is at risk.

### 3. Bitcoin Price
Despite diversification, if BTC falls to $40K, valuations of all ETFs drop, custody fees fall proportionally, and sentiment breaks.

### 4. Competition
- **Binance** remains the #1 globally by volume
- **Robinhood Crypto** and **Kraken** compete in US retail
- **Fidelity Crypto, Schwab** will enter strongly in 2026
- **Revolut, Cash App** dominate mobile-first

### 5. Unpredictable Regulation
Although Coinbase won the SEC v. Coinbase case in 2024, new administrations could reopen issues. Real risk if the next Congress passes more restrictive legislation.

### 6. Priced Expensively
P/E 47x TTM, Forward 40x. For a company with quarterly crypto volatility, any miss drops the stock 20-30%.

### 7. Hack or Operational Failure
It has never been hacked at the main custody level (only minor incidents in hot wallets). Such an event would be existential — all ETFs would move AUM to other custodians.

## Conclusion

At $208, COIN lets you buy:
1. The only fully regulated crypto exchange in the US (regulatory moat of $1B+ and 5 years)
2. ~$900M annually of SaaS-like recurring income (USDC interest)
3. Monopolistic custody of 80%+ of BTC and ETH spot ETF AUM
4. An L2 (Base) that already processes more than Ethereum mainnet
5. -53% discount from the high, with earnings growing quarter by quarter

**The bear case is "crypto dies" (unlikely given institutional adoption). The bull case is "Coinbase becomes the DTCC/Nasdaq/Visa of crypto" (likely given its regulatory and custody position).**

This isn't a bet on BTC prices — it's a bet on the regulated adoption of blockchain as financial infrastructure. And that adoption is already happening, with or without BTC at $150K.

It doesn't pay a dividend. The return comes from price appreciation if the thesis materializes.

COST.summary_what: Costco Wholesale operates over 890 warehouses in 14 countries, including the US, Canada, Mexico, UK, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Australia, Spain, France, China (Shanghai, Suzhou), Iceland, New Zealand, and Sweden. Its unique business model requires you to PAY to shop. There are two membership types: Gold Star ($65/year) and Executive ($130/year with 2% cashback). Approximately 73 million households have paid memberships. Revenue for 2025 is around $275 billion. The mix is 85% merchandise (fresh food, groceries, electronics, clothing, fuel), 12% services (pharmacy, optical, tires, travel, insurance), and 3% membership fees. The key accounting insight is that membership fees are pure revenue with nearly 100% margin, generating about $5.4 billion a year in operating income without associated costs. Costco sells products at about an 11% margin (compared to Walmart's 24%) because it aims to renew memberships, not profit from products. Founded in 1983, merged with Price Club in 1993. Headquarters in Issaquah, Washington. CEO: Ron Vachris (from January 2024, replacing Craig Jelinek).

When a customer pays $65-130 a year automatically without thinking, it's a revenue stream like software subscriptions; (2) Membership margin: about $5.4 billion annually from fees goes straight to the bottom line with nearly 100% gross margin. This alone covers almost all corporate expenses; (3) Strong bargaining power — Costco is a top-3 buyer of almost every consumer product in the US (milk, chicken, wine, electronics, clothing). It demands 3-5% less than Walmart; (4) Kirkland Signature: Costco's own brand earns about $85 billion a year — more than Kellogg's, Hershey's, Ford Motor Company, or Nike. It's the third-largest consumer goods brand in the world; (5) Anchored pricing power: the hot dog + soda combo has cost $1.50 since 1985; the whole pizza $9.99; the rotisserie chicken $4.99. Jim Sinegal (co-founder) threatened to 'kill' any executive who raised them. These are psychological anchors that members renew just to 'feel like they're winning'; (6) 30 consecutive years of paying dividends + 18 consecutive increases + special dividends ($7 in 2012, $10 in 2017, $10 in 2020, $15 in 2023); (7) Proven resistance to Amazon — COST grew 14% in Amazon's worst year (2023), because the physical 'discovery' experience in warehouses can't be replicated online. Recurring, defensive, pricing power, cultural moat — 4 of the 5 qualities Buffett looks for.

COST.research_full:
# Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) — Complete Research

## Price: $994.87 | P/E TTM: 51.7 | P/E Forward: 44.3 | Div Yield: 0.59% | Market Cap: $441.6B

---

## What It Is

Costco Wholesale is **the largest shopping club in the world** — over 890 warehouses in 14 countries, 73 million+ households with paid memberships, and ~$275 billion in annual revenue. Founded in 1983 in Seattle. Merged with Price Club in 1993. HQ in Issaquah, Washington.

The business model is counterintuitive: **you pay to shop**. Gold Star $65/year, Executive $130/year (with 2% cashback up to $1,250). Approximately 130 million+ individual cardholders (members + add-ons).

## The Secret Ingredient: Membership Fees

This is the line most analyses overlook:

| Year | Membership Fee Revenue | % of Operating Income |
|-----|------------------------|------------------------|
| 2020 | $3.54B | 72% |
| 2021 | $3.88B | 68% |
| 2022 | $4.22B | 71% |
| 2023 | $4.58B | 68% |
| 2024 | $4.83B | 69% |
| 2025 | $5.40B | 71% |

**Membership fees have nearly 100% gross margin** (there's no cost associated with an annual renewal). This means that **~70% of Costco's operating income comes from a recurring revenue stream like SaaS**.

If you separate the businesses:
- Merchandise retail: revenue $266B, margin ~2.5% → OpInc ~$6.6B
- Membership fees: revenue $5.4B, margin ~100% → OpInc ~$5.4B

They sell products almost AT COST (the name "Costco" comes from Cost + Co.). The real business is memberships.

## Renewal — The Key Metric

Costco reports renewal rate each quarter:

| Region | Renewal |
|--------|------------|
| **US/Canada** | 92.9% |
| **Global** | 90.5% |

For context:
- Netflix USA: ~60% annual renewal
- Amazon Prime USA: ~93% annual renewal
- Costco: 92.9% — **Amazon Prime tier**

Every fee increase (last time was September 2024: Gold $60→$65, Exec $120→$130) generates $800M+ in additional revenue with zero incremental cost and **no drop in renewal**. That's pure pricing power.

## Kirkland Signature — The Third Largest Consumer Brand in the World

Kirkland Signature (Costco's own brand) earns **~$85B/year**:

| Brand | Revenue 2024 |
|-------|--------------|
| Coca-Cola (the brand, not the company) | ~$95B |
| Pepsi (the brand) | ~$87B |
| **Kirkland Signature** | **~$85B** |
| Nike | ~$51B |
| Kellogg's | ~$13B |
| Hershey's | ~$11B |

Kirkland represents ~30% of Costco's revenue. Members prefer it for a simple reason: Costco DOES NOT put a Kirkland product unless it's demonstrably superior or equal to the best brand in the market **at 20-30% less price**.

That promise builds so much trust that Costco is the ONLY retailer where consumers assume the generic product is better than the branded.

## Anchored Pricing Power — The Magic Prices

Costco deliberately maintains several prices **without raising them for decades** as psychological anchors:

| Product | Price | Since |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Hot dog + soda combo | $1.50 | 1985 |
| Whole pizza | $9.99 | ~2000 |
| Rotisserie chicken | $4.99 | ~2009 |
| Muffins pack of 12 | $8.99 | ~2015 |

Jim Sinegal (co-founder, CEO until 2011) famously told Craig Jelinek when he suggested raising the hot dog: *"If you raise the fucking hot dog, I will kill you."*

This is NOT sentimentality — it's pricing science:

1. Members enter Costco, see the hot dog at $1.50 and feel "winning"
2. That feeling of value transfers to EVERYTHING else they buy
3. They renew the membership the following year to repeat the sensation

It's the most effective "loss leader" in retail history.

## Resistance to Amazon — The Real Test

During 2020-2023, Amazon ate the lunch of every physical store (Walmart, Kroger, Walgreens, CVS, etc.). Costco:

| Year | COST revenue growth |
|-----|---------------------|
| 2020 | +9% |
| 2021 | +17% |
| 2022 | +16% |
| 2023 | +7% (bad year for general retail) |
| 2024 | +9% |
| 2025 | +8% |

Costco grew DURING Amazon's best moment. Reasons:

1. **Experience**: People go on Saturdays as a family outing — Amazon can't replicate "wandering through the warehouse and finding things"
2. **Fresh**: Meat, produce, dairy — members want to inspect before buying
3. **Sizes**: Costco sells in industrial quantities (24 rolls of toilet paper, 10 pounds of rice) that Amazon doesn't transport efficiently
4. **Absolute price**: In commodities, Costco beats Amazon by ~10-15% because it doesn't pay for last-mile

Even Amazon Business hasn't managed to take Costco away from anyone.

## Dividends — Regular + Special

Costco has paid a regular dividend since 2004, increasing it every year:

| Year | Annual dividend |
|-----|-----------------|
| 2004 | $0.40 |
| 2014 | $1.42 |
| 2024 | $4.64 |
| 2026 projected | $5.80 |

**But the home run is the special dividends**:

| Year | Special dividend | Total amount paid |
|-----|---------------------|---------------------|
| 2012 | $7.00/share | $3.1B |
| 2015 | $5.00/share | $2.2B |
| 2017 | $7.00/share | $3.1B |
| 2020 | $10.00/share | $4.4B |
| 2023 | $15.00/share | $6.7B |
| ~2026-27 | ~$20-25 estimated | ~$9-11B |

The historical cadence is 3-4 years. The last was January 2023. **The next is expected between late 2026 and early 2027.**

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Price** | $994.87 |
| **Market Cap** | $441.6B |
| **P/E TTM** | 51.65x |
| **P/E Forward** | 44.27x |
| **EPS TTM** | $19.26 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 0.59% |
| **Regular Annual Dividend** | $5.80 |
| **52w High** | $1,067.08 |
| **52w Low** | $844.06 |
| **Drawdown from high** | -6.8% |
| **Revenue 2025** | $275B |
| **Membership Fees 2025** | $5.4B |
| **Operating Margin** | 3.9% |
| **ROE** | 31% |
| **Free Cash Flow 2025** | $8.2B |

## Why Now

### 1. Near 52w High (Validation)
At -6.8% from the high, COST isn't "cheap" — but that's exactly what you expect from a Class A compounder. Quality companies are rarely 30% off. When they are, something is usually broken.

### 2. Fee Increase Pays Dividends
September 2024 increased Gold Star $60→$65 (+8%) and Executive $120→$130 (+8%). This was effective January 2025 — still materializing in results.

### 3. Accelerating International Expansion
COST opened 28 warehouses in 2025, 30+ planned for 2026. New markets: Sweden (first warehouse March 2026), New Zealand, more stores in China.

### 4. Special Dividend on the Horizon 2026-27
Historical cadence (2012, 2015, 2017, 2020, 2023) suggests next special between late 2026 - early 2027. $20-25/share implies ~$9-11B extra return.

### 5. Proven Amazon-Proof Thesis
10 years of consecutive growth while the rest of physical retail suffered. Buffett said in 2024: *"Costco is one of the few retailers I'd bet against Amazon."*

## Risks

### 1. Premium Valuation
P/E 51.65x TTM is the high range of COST's history. There's no room for error — any miss in earnings or renewal impacts.

### 2. Renewal Miss
If US/Canada renewal drops below 92%, the stock falls 15%+. Members are loyal but not indestructible.

### 3. Persistent Inflation
Costco absorbs inflation in commodities (meat, dairy) to not break the price promise. Margin compression possible if inflation accelerates.

### 4. Shrinkage (Theft)
Growing in California, Washington state, NYC — directly affects margin. Costco is investing in loss prevention tech.

### 5. Competition from Sam's Club
Walmart has heavily invested in Sam's Club: same-day delivery, Scan & Go, opening 30+ new warehouses. First time Sam's has gained market share in 15 years.

### 6. Costly Expansion
New warehouse ~$75-100M initial investment, 3-5 years to break-even. Asian markets more expensive (China construction, rents).

### 7. Succession Risk
Ron Vachris is the new CEO since January 2024 (replaced Craig Jelinek). CEO transitions are cultural risks — Costco has a uniquely disciplined operational culture. Vachris is a Costco veteran (30+ years), but he's not Sinegal or Jelinek.

### 8. Insignificant Dividend Yield
0.59% — this is NOT an income stock. If you're looking for monthly/quarterly dividend flow, this isn't for you. COST is appreciation + specials + consistency of 10% annual historical.

## Conclusion

COST at $994.87 lets you buy:
1. A business with ~$5.4B annual SaaS-like recurring income (membership fees with 93% renewal)
2. The third-largest consumer brand in the world (Kirkland Signature - $85B)
3. 10 years of consecutive growth proven resistant to Amazon
4. Anchored pricing power (hot dog $1.50 since 1985 — not nostalgia, it's behavioral science)
5. Expected special dividend in 2026-27 ($20-25/share = 2-2.5% extra return in one go)

It's not cheap. It never has been. And it never will be — because the market knows what it's buying. This is **paying for quality**, not for discount.

If your investment thesis is "I want to own the best businesses for 20 years and sleep well," Costco is the definition.

*Research date: April 21, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
COST.one_liner: Costco is a shopping club where you pay a membership to access deals, and most of its money comes from these memberships, not from what it sells.
COST.why_short: Costco is a solid investment because its business relies on members renewing each year without much thought, and its own brand sells more than many big companies.
COST.risk_short: The biggest risk is that the stock is already expensive, and if fewer people renew their memberships, it could lose value. It also heavily relies on the US and Canada for its income.

CP.summary_short: The world's only railway connecting Mexico, the United States, and Canada in a single operated line—an irreplaceable asset created by the merger of Canadian Pacific and Kansas City Southern (completed April 14, 2023, approved by STB with 7 years of oversight). Q4 2025: revenue CAD $3.9B (+1% YoY), EPS CAD $1.33 (+3%), Operating Ratio 55.9% (improved by 120 basis points). FY2025: revenue CAD $15.1B (+4%), EPS CAD $4.61 (+8.8%). FY2026 guidance: volume growth mid-single-digit, EPS growth low-double-digit. Reports Q1 2026 tomorrow, April 29, 2026—the first quarter with fully mature CPKC synergies (target $1B+ annually by 2028). Trading near 52-week high ($89.42); forward price vs. profit 23.7x vs. UNP 19x, NSC 18x—premium justified by unique geographic advantage.
CP.summary_what: Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CPKC) is the first and only Class I railway operating a continuous network between Mexico, the United States, and Canada. Founded as Canadian Pacific Railway in 1881 (the railway that physically united Canada and enabled the confederation of British Columbia). HQ in Calgary, Alberta. CEO: Keith Creel—a direct disciple of Hunter Harrison (the father of Precision Scheduled Railroading), CEO since 2017. The CPKC merger (completed April 14, 2023, $31B in stock + cash) is the most significant railway deal in the last 40 years: Canadian Pacific (Canada-US Midwest network) + Kansas City Southern (US South-Mexico network) created a single-line corridor of 32,000 km from Vancouver to Detroit to Houston to Lázaro Cárdenas/Veracruz. It is the ONLY physical way to move freight between the three countries without transfers. The STB approved the deal with unique conditions: 7 years of oversight, neutral gateways, employee protection. Integration entered the final phase 2025-2026. ~20,000 employees, ~3,000 locomotives. Three freight segments: (1) Bulk (~37% revenue): Canadian grains, potash, thermal/met coal, fertilizers. The Canadian grain franchise is the best on the continent—Canadian Pacific handles 50%+ of the wheat, canola, oats leaving the prairies; (2) Merchandise (~33% revenue): chemicals, plastics, energy (crude by rail), forest products, finished autos + parts, metals; the segment most benefiting from the CPKC deal via cross-border auto plants Mexico-US-Canada; (3) Intermodal (~30% revenue): domestic + international containers—ports Vancouver, Saint John, Lázaro Cárdenas. The post-merger jewel: the new intermodal route Mexico-Texas-Chicago ('Mexico Midwest Express') is absorbing share from trucking and rail competitors that require transfers. Significant shareholders: Bill Ackman/Pershing Square holds ~7%. TCI Fund (Chris Hohn) is a major shareholder. Canadian institutional families (Caisse de dépôt).
CP.summary_why: Buying CP at $87.30 means acquiring North America's most impossible-to-replicate geographic advantage—the ONLY Class I railway with an integrated tri-national network—just as merger synergies are on the rise and an earnings catalyst is 24 hours away: (1) Q1 2026 reports tomorrow, April 29, 2026—the first quarter with 100% integrated CPKC: Q4 2025 already showed Operating Ratio 55.9% (improved by 120 basis points) and EPS +3%. 2026 guidance: volume growth mid-single-digit, EPS growth low-double-digit. Consensus expects $0.84 EPS / $2.7B revenue in Q1; any beat of Operating Ratio (target sub-55% for 2026) confirms synergies are materializing ahead of schedule; (2) Physically irreplaceable geographic advantage: The US will NOT approve another cross-border railway (the STB took 2 years to approve CPKC and imposed 7 years of oversight). Mexico will NOT approve another foreign railway investor after the CPKC process. Canada has only 2 Class I railways (CN and CP)—protected concentration. Duplication would require buying rights of way from competitors (who won't sell) + building 32,000 km of track + gaining regulatory approval in 3 countries. **Not duplicable. Period.**; (3) Merger synergies $1B+ annually by 2028: Q4 2025 already captured ~$350M in run-rate; full target $1B (revenue + cost combined). Revenue synergies are the most interesting: new routes like Mexico Midwest Express (autos Mexico-Detroit), bulk Canada-Mexico (Canadian grains to Mexico via Lázaro Cárdenas), end-to-end intermodal. Trucking absorbs ~80% of cross-border freight today; any % CPKC captures is mid-teen growth in the segment; (4) Structural improvement in Operating Ratio: Q4 2025 OR 55.9% is already competitive with UNP (60%) and NSC (62%). Hunter Harrison's legacy of PSR via Keith Creel is being applied to the ex-KCS network (which had OR ~70% pre-merger). Each basis point of OR improvement over $15B of revenue = $1.5M of operating income. Consensus models OR reaching 54% in 2027 = ~$300M extra EBIT; (5) High free cash flow conversion: capex ~$2.5B/year (15% revenue) post-integration. FCF yield ~3%, dividend +25% YoY in Feb 2026 to CAD $0.95/year (yield 0.76% USD). Buybacks resumed Q4 2025 after 2 years of pause due to integration (net debt dropped from 3.8x to 2.6x net debt/EBITDA); (6) Bill Ackman + Chris Hohn institutional: Pershing Square holds ~7% (Ackman's second historical position after the first wave 2010-2016 that generated 5x). TCI Fund (Hohn, known for activism in railroads) accumulated 2024-2025. These are not traders—they are 5+ year holders with a fundamental thesis; (7) Hedge against trade frictions: Trump's 2026 tariffs on Mexican autos actually benefit CPKC vs trucking—the railway is 4x more fuel-efficient and absorbs marginal tariffs better. Stellantis, Ford, GM plants in Mexico need to move assemblies to the US at any cost; rail is the cheapest way; (8) Trading near 52-week high but P/E justified: $87.30 vs $89.42 high (recent). Forward P/E 23.7x vs UNP 19x, NSC 18x. The ~25% premium is defensible by: (a) tri-national moat, (b) growth runway of synergies until 2028, (c) better volume mix (less coal, more containers/autos). Bull case 2027 EPS $5.50 × 22x = $121 (+39%).
CP.summary_risk: The risks are specifically measurable: (1) Q1 2026 EARNINGS RISK—reports tomorrow April 29, 4:30pm ET: any miss on revenue ($2.7B estimate) or Operating Ratio (>57%) leads to a -5-8% drop the next day. Volume growth is already in guidance (mid-single-digit)—if Q1 shows only 1-2% volume growth, the market punishes; (2) Trump's tariffs on Mexico: threats of 25% tariffs on Mexican autos (Trump announced March 2026) could reduce cross-border volume if Stellantis, Ford, GM plants ramp down production. CPKC has ~12% revenue exposure to Mexico-US auto manufacturing. If tariffs materialize = -10-15% revenue from the merchandise segment; (3) Canadian grain dependency: 18% revenue depends on Canadian grains. Poor wheat/canola harvest due to drought or frost = revenue drops instantly. The 2025 harvest was good; 2026 uncertain; (4) STB oversight until 2030: the STB can impose remedies if there are complaints from competitors or shippers—gateway prices, service guarantees. Q3 2025 BNSF filed a complaint about gateway pricing in Houston; pending resolution. Asymmetric regulatory risk against CPKC; (5) Premium valuation reduces margin of safety: P/E 26x trailing is high vs historical railroad cycle (15-22x). If recession hits 2026 H2, freight volume drops 10-15% typical, EPS contracts, P/E should re-rate. Downside trough case: $70 (-20%); (6) Integration fatigue: 7 years of management focus on merger = little bandwidth for organic growth. Competitors (UNP, NSC) have already pivoted to investment in intermodal automation, lean operating models. CPKC has to execute merger + modernize simultaneously; (7) Low dividend yield (0.76%): not an income thesis. If growth thesis doesn't materialize, there's no yield floor to support the stock; (8) Mexico political risk: Sheinbaum administration (Mexico) has been more railway foreign-friendly than expected, but a nationalist turn or renegotiation of Mexico track concessions = directly affects southern segment revenue. Current concessions until 2047, but governments can modify; (9) CO2 transition cost: transition to hydrogen/battery locomotives will require significant capex 2027-2035. CN already announced pilots; CP running behind. If EPA + Canadian environmental regulation forces accelerated replacement, capex could rise 30-40% for years.

# Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP / CPKC) — Complete Research

## Price: $87.30 | P/E TTM: 25.98x | P/E Forward: 23.70x | Div Yield: 0.76% | Market Cap: $78.5B

---

## What It Is

Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CPKC) is the **only Class I railway in the world** operating a single continuous network between **Mexico, the United States, and Canada**. It is dual-listed on NYSE (CP) and TSX (CP). HQ Calgary, Alberta.

Founded as **Canadian Pacific Railway in 1881**—the railway that physically united Canada coast-to-coast and allowed British Columbia to enter the confederation. It is one of the oldest names on the North American stock exchange.

**CEO: Keith Creel**—a direct disciple of Hunter Harrison (the father of Precision Scheduled Railroading). CEO since 2017. ~20,000 employees, ~3,000 locomotives.

## The CPKC Merger

- **Announced**: March 2021 (deal with KCS, $31B stock + cash)
- **Closed**: April 14, 2023 (after 2 years of STB approval with unique conditions)
- **Synergies target**: $1B+ annual run-rate by 2028
- **Synergies captured Q4 2025**: ~$350M run-rate
- **STB oversight**: 7 years from closing (until April 2030)

The merger created a single-line corridor of 32,000 km from Vancouver to Detroit to Houston to Lázaro Cárdenas/Veracruz. **It is the ONLY physical way to move freight between the three countries without transfers**.

## Segment Structure (FY 2025)

| Segment | % Revenue | Drivers |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Bulk** | ~37% | Canadian grains, potash, coal, fertilizers |
| **Merchandise** | ~33% | Chemicals, plastics, energy, forest, autos, metals |
| **Intermodal** | ~30% | Domestic + international containers |

Total revenue FY 2025: CAD $15.1B (+4% YoY). EPS $4.61 (+8.8%). Operating Ratio 56.5% FY (Q4 55.9%, improved by 120 basis points).

## Q4 2025 (results Feb 2026)

| Metric | Q4 2025 | YoY |
|---------|---------|-----|
| Revenue | CAD $3.9B | +1% |
| EPS adj | CAD $1.33 | +3% |
| **Operating Ratio** | **55.9%** | **-120bps** |
| Volume RTMs | +3% | growth mid-single-digit |
| FCF | $850M | strong conversion |

## Q1 2026 — Reports TOMORROW April 29, 2026 (4:30pm ET)

- **Consensus Revenue**: $2.7152B

- Webcast: investor.cpkcr.com
- Replay available until May 6, 2026

## Guidance FY 2026 (issued with Q4 2025)

- Volume growth: mid-single-digit
- EPS growth: low-double-digit
- Operating Ratio target: sub-55% for 2026
- Capex: ~$2.7B (~15% revenue)

CRM.summary_what: Salesforce, Inc. is the world's #1 CRM (Customer Relationship Management) software company with ~22% global market share vs Microsoft Dynamics (~5%), Oracle (~5%), SAP (~5%). Founded in March 1999 by **Marc Benioff** (ex-Oracle, still CEO/Chairman) in San Francisco with the revolutionary idea 'No Software' — delivering enterprise software as a service (SaaS) over the internet instead of on-premise installation. This idea created the entire SaaS category. HQ Salesforce Tower, San Francisco. ~73,000 employees, 200+ countries. **The current portfolio** is organized in 'Customer 360' — the unified platform that integrates everything: (1) **Sales Cloud (~16% revenue)**: the original product, managing leads/opportunities/forecasting; (2) **Service Cloud (~24% revenue)**: the largest today — call centers, customer support, field service. Almost every Fortune 500 company uses it; (3) **Platform & Other (~32% revenue)**: Salesforce Platform (Lightning), Slack ($27B acquisition 2021), Heroku, MuleSoft ($6.5B acquisition 2018). Plus **Tableau ($15.7B acquisition 2019)** = analytics; (4) **Marketing & Commerce (~13% revenue)**: Marketing Cloud, Commerce Cloud (B2B + B2C); (5) **Data Cloud / Data 360 (~10% revenue, fastest-growing)**: enterprise data lakehouse. **112 trillion records ingested FY26** (+114% YoY); (6) **Agentforce (NEW, launched Oct 2024)**: enterprise AI agents platform. Already $800M ARR (+169% YoY) in 18 months. **Informatica acquisition $8B** closed November 18, 2025 ($25/share cash) — adds $1.1B ARR of data integration/governance, accretive within 12 months. Other recent acquisitions: own AI consultancy + data quality firms. Fortune 500 customers: 90%+ use some Salesforce product. Top 10: AT&T, ADP, American Express, T-Mobile, FedEx, Allianz, etc. Marc Benioff personally owns ~3% (~$4.5B). Activists: Elliott (exited 2024), Jeff Smith/Starboard (~1%). Buffett does not own.
CRM.summary_why: CRM at $182.21 is buying the world's #1 CRM, a SaaS leader for 25 years, at its cheapest valuation since the 2004 IPO — while AI (thought to be an existential threat) is demonstrably ACCELERATING the business: (1) **Agentforce $800M ARR +169% YoY = the fastest-growing enterprise AI product in history**: Microsoft Copilot, Google Duet/Gemini Workspace, Adobe Firefly — none reached this ramp in 18 months. 29,000 deals closed (+50% QoQ). 20 trillion tokens processed = 2.4 billion 'agentic work units'. Agentforce's moat is structural: it lives on proprietary customer-360 data only Salesforce has + native integrations with Slack, Tableau, Data Cloud. Microsoft can replicate with Dynamics + Copilot but does NOT have the customer data graph; (2) **Combined Agentforce + Data 360 + Informatica ARR $2.9B (+200%+ YoY)**: this is the growth engine justifying the thesis. $2.9B of ARR growing 200% on a $41.5B total revenue base = ~7% of total revenue already comes from AI products that didn't exist 2 years ago. If they maintain 100% YoY growth another year, it's $5.8B ARR end FY27 = 14% of total revenue; (3) **Informatica acquisition closed Nov 18, 2025 — strategic game-changer**: $8B deal + $25/share cash. Adds $1.1B ARR of data integration, MDM (master data management), data quality, governance. **Accretive within 12 months**. Data integration is the moat Salesforce lacked — for AI agents to work, you need clean + integrated data. Informatica is the world's #1. Combination creates a unique 'AI + CRM + Data' stack in enterprise; (4) **Stock fell -32% YTD = fear overshoot**: $296 to $182 in 12 months. The bear thesis ('AI commoditizes software, Salesforce dies') was plausible in 2024, but 2025-2026 data refuted it. Q4 FY26 beat: revenue $11.2B vs $10.9B expected. cRPO $30B (+11% YoY). Operating margin GAAP 19.7% (+150bps). Free cash flow $15B FY26 (+15%); (5) **Forward price vs. profit 13.65x = the lowest since IPO 2004**: historically CRM traded 30-50x forward during 2014-2022. Compression to 13.65x assumes growth ~3-5% perpetual when FY27 guide is 10-11%. Comparables: ServiceNow 50x forward, Workday 25x, Microsoft 30x, Oracle 20x. CRM at Oracle's multiple = $250 (+37%); (6) **$50B buyback authorization (announced Feb 2026)**: the largest in Salesforce's history — equivalent to 33% of current market cap. Accretive compression to 13.65x price vs. profit is mathematically optimal. Buybacks 2025 already reduced diluted shares -2.8%; (7) **Marc Benioff hands-on, Stewart Butterfield (Slack) exited Q4**: Benioff resumed direct product control post-Slack departure. Focus on 'profitable growth' — operating margin expanding +200bps annual target FY27-FY30; (8) **FY30 target raised to $63B revenue (announced Feb 2026)**: implies CAGR 8.5% from FY26 to FY30. Combined with buybacks, EPS CAGR 15%+. Total annualized return ~20% if target is met; (9) **Activists exited but passive accumulation rose**: Elliott exited 2024 after extracting the transformation to profitable growth. Starboard (Jeff Smith) remains ~1%. BlackRock + Vanguard + State Street exceed 30%+ combined — sticky money; (10) **Upcoming catalysts**: Q1 FY27 reports late May 2026 (Dreamforce annual conference September 2026 — Benioff's keynote typically moves stock).
CRM.summary_risk: The risks are specific: (1) **AI commoditization remains a tail risk**: Microsoft Copilot + Dynamics 365 is aggressively winning enterprise deals. If Microsoft integrates AI agents with Office 365 (already in 95% of Fortune 500), CRM could lose net-new clients. Q4 FY26 net-new client growth only +6% — slower than historical (+15-20%); (2) **Macro enterprise IT spend**: 30% of Salesforce's revenue comes from Financial Services, Telco, Retail — cyclical sectors. If a recession hits in H2 2026, IT budgets cut; deal cycles lengthen; cRPO growth slows. Private software companies (Stripe, Databricks, OpenAI) absorbing IT budget that previously went to Salesforce; (3) **Informatica integration risk**: $8B deal + new operations + 5,800 additional employees. Salesforce has a mixed track record (Slack performed poorly post-deal, MuleSoft well, Tableau well). If Informatica revenue stalls or churn increases = -$5-10/share; (4) **AI cost structure pressure**: Agentforce consumes tokens (Anthropic + OpenAI). If hyperscalers raise pricing, Agentforce margins contract. Today Salesforce 'ConsumeOps' pricing transfers cost to the client — but there's a limit to how much enterprises will pay; (5) **High stock-based compensation**: SBC FY26 $4.2B ~10% revenue. Dilutes shareholders if stock doesn't rise. Buybacks $50B compensate, but net historical dilution has been positive; (6) **Slack underperforming**: $27B deal 2021. Today Slack ARR ~$2.5B vs $4-5B expected. Microsoft Teams continues gaining share. Slack 'Agentforce-ified' attempts recovery but unclear if it works; (7) **Benioff key-man risk**: 60 years old, controls narrative + strategy + product. If he resigns abruptly or falls ill, transition would be traumatic. Bret Taylor (ex-co-CEO) already left 2022 to OpenAI/Sierra; (8) **Low forward price vs. profit might be justified**: if the market is pricing slow growth correctly (5% perpetual, not 10%), $182 is fair. Bull case requires FY30 $63B target real; bear case discounts it to $52B = $150 stock; (9) **Limited China enterprise expansion**: regulatory + geopolitical = CRM doesn't penetrate China. ~$5-7B addressable market not captured; (10) **Q1 FY27 reports late May 2026** — first test post-Q4 FY26 momentum. Any guidance cut = -10-15% day.

# Salesforce (CRM) — Complete Research

## Price: $182.21 | P/E TTM: 23.10x | P/E Forward: 13.65x | Div Yield: 0.97% | Market Cap: $149.15B

---

## What It Is
