DTEGY.summary_why: DTEGY at $33.82 (near 52-week low of $30.31–$40.58) is buying Europe's largest telecom with a 3.5% yield + €2B buyback (~5% total market cap return) when the market is almost entirely discounting the European business after pricing in the T-Mobile US stake. Specific reasons: (1) **'Germany + Europe for almost nothing' — the most quantifiable setup in the thesis**: DT market cap $165B. Its 57% stake in T-Mobile US (TMUS market cap ~$209B) is worth ~$119B. That means the market assigns only ~$46B to the rest of the business — all of Germany (#1 operator, 12.6M homes with fiber, ramping to 25M by 2030), 10 European countries with 8.6M converged customers, and T-Systems IT services. That remainder generated €20.5B of Adj EBITDA AL in 2025. This implies a stub valuation of ~2.2x EV/EBITDA when European telecom peers trade at 5–7x — a brutal discount. (2) **Q1 2026 delivered +7.5% Adj EBITDA AL organic + record Q1 FCF of €5.7B + EPS +7.9%**: the gap between +7.5% organic and +2.0% reported is 100% FX (weak USD suppresses TMUS translation). Underlying operating performance is healthy across all segments. FY26 guidance was raised in the same print. (3) **Growing dividend + record €1.00/share FY2025 (+11%)**: 3.5% yield on the ADR. Plus FY26 buyback up to €2B (~3% market cap retirement) — €471M / 15.6M shares already completed in Q1. Total shareholder return ~5% per year before any capital gain. Payout policy 40–60% of Adj EPS = sustainable ratio, not overleveraged. (4) **T-Mobile US is the best-performing US wireless carrier Q1 2026**: TMUS contribution to DT: revenue +10.9% USD, EBITDA AL +12.9% USD. Leader in 5G mid-band coverage. Mike Sievert (TMUS CEO) continues to execute — postpaid net adds leading the industry. DT's stake ~$119B in TMUS already covers 72% of DT's market cap before even considering Germany/Europe/T-Systems. (5) **Tim Höttges: 12+ years as CEO, extended another 5**: architect of the T-Mobile US + Sprint merger (2020) — the deal that transformed TMUS from #3 to #2 wireless in the US and created a functional Verizon/T-Mobile duopoly. Re-contracted in 2025 = strategic continuity + execution track record. One of the most respected telecom CEOs in Europe. (6) **Germany fiber rollout — structural long-term moat**: 12.6M homes with FTTH at YE 2025, ramping to 25M by 2030. DT passes ~3M homes per year = sustainable capacity. Fiber is a 30–50 year asset — once the cable is in, churn goes to zero. Vodafone Germany and O2 have fallen behind — DT is consolidating #1 mobile + #1 fixed broadband + #1 fiber with converged bundling. (7) **ver.di strike + cable MVNO = temporary noise, not a structural problem**: ver.di is demanding +6.6% pay + €660 bonus (60,000 workers). Will resolve with a ~4–5% pay deal — an absorbable cost (~€500M run-rate against €119B in revenue). Comcast/Charter MVNOs on TMUS are capturing ~39% of net adds = real, but MVNO margins are poor for cable companies and TMUS continues to grow. Both narrative drivers resolve in 2026–2027. (8) **Defensive cash flow + sector resilience**: telecom is ~recession-resistant — people pay their phone and internet bills before almost any other discretionary expense. FCF AL FY2025 €19.5B = ~12% FCF yield on market cap. In a recession scenario, DT outperforms cyclicals by a wide margin. (9) **Analyst consensus Strong Buy + average target €37.11 = ~+24% upside on ADR**: 18 Buy / 0 Hold / 0 Sell on MarketScreener. Wall Street target ~$42/ADR vs $33.82 spot. Re-rating to 14x forward P/E = $39+ = +15% capital + 3.5% yield + 3% buyback = ~22% total return. (10) **Backup thesis: even if TMUS drops 20%, DT is still cheap**: if TMUS falls to $167B, DT's stake = $95B. DT market cap at $165B implies stub Europe = $70B for €20.5B EBITDA AL = 3.4x EV/EBITDA — still a discount. Structural margin of safety via TMUS hedge.
DTEGY.summary_risk: The risks are measurable: (1) **ver.di Germany strike — near-term costs + reputational pressure**: 60,000 workers demanding +6.6% + €660 bonus. 2nd negotiation round collapsed April 27. Strikes expanded May 11–12 to North Rhine-Westphalia, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt, Thuringia (>20,000 employees striking, including T-Systems for the first time). The union is pointing to the €2B buyback as leverage. Resolution likely at a ~4–5% pay increase = ~€500M run-rate cost. If negotiations drag beyond Q3 2026, reputational noise + service disruptions in Germany. (2) **Cable MVNO disruption at TMUS — Comcast/Charter capturing ~39% of net postpaid adds in 2025**: Xfinity Mobile (Comcast, >20M wireless subs) + Spectrum Mobile (Charter) offering 'free for a year' bundles with cable. They capture share without network overhead. TMUS pricing power gets squeezed, ARPU plateaus, net add growth decelerates. If this accelerates in 2026–2027, TMUS contribution to DT grows more slowly. (3) **USD weakness compresses reported translation**: Q1 2026 gap of +7.5% organic vs +2.0% reported is 100% FX. If USD stays weak below 1.20 EUR/USD for all of 2026, the Adj EBITDA AL guidance of €47.5B becomes elusive. Sensitivity: every 5% USD depreciation = ~€1.5B less reported EBITDA AL. (4) **TMUS valuation downside — market cap already fell from $260B to $209B in 2026**: if TMUS continues to drop (cable MVNOs + slowing growth + Verizon competitive response + capex peak), DT's stake value erodes. Every 10% TMUS decline = ~$12B lost in DT's stake. If TMUS falls another 20%, DT's stake worth $95B and the Europe stub discount disappears narratively. (5) **Germany fiber capex peak + slow ROI**: DT is spending €3–5B/year on fiber buildout. Payback is 8–12 years post-installation. If penetration doesn't reach targets or competition (Vodafone, Telefónica Deutschland, alt-nets) pressures pricing, ROI erodes. Plus BNetzA regulation may force wholesale access to competitors on unfavorable terms. (6) **40–60% payout policy is not a 'safe' dividend aristocrat**: if Adj EPS drops 20% in any year (recession, USD shock, FX), the dividend is not contractually protected — it can stagnate or be cut. Recent track record is good (+11% FY2025) but not a guarantee. (7) **Tim Höttges succession 2030+ — no visible successor**: 12 years as CEO + 5 more = ~17 years total at end of term. No successor designated. Christian Illek (CFO) is a candidate but not confirmed. Mike Sievert (TMUS CEO) could be an external candidate. Poorly executed succession = strategic drift risk. (8) **European telecom regulation**: EU push for single market + price caps + free roaming is a multi-year headwind. Spain and Portugal specifically have low ARPU + fierce competition. If Brussels passes new regulation in 2026–2027 (wholesale price caps, spectrum sharing mandates), Europe segment EBITDA AL grows more slowly. (9) **T-Systems is 3% of revenue but a management distraction**: T-Systems has historically been an underperformer — competing with Accenture, IBM, Capgemini in services. EBITDA margin below 10% vs core telecom above 35%. If DT does not spin off or sell T-Systems, it distracts capital + management bandwidth without creating value. Some activists have pushed for a spinout. (10) **Exiting Romania = uncertain execution**: DT + OTE are exiting the Romanian joint venture. If the sale doesn't materialize or prices in below book, a write-down is possible. Europe segment revenue declines due to divestiture. (11) **5G capex cycle peak Germany + USA**: hyperscalers aren't the only ones investing in infrastructure — telcos are in a 5G standalone + fiber + edge capex cycle. If 5G enterprise demand doesn't materialize over the next two years, capex ROI erodes. (12) **Recession risk for B2B (T-Systems + corporate accounts)**: if Germany enters a deep recession in 2026–2027 (manufacturing is already contracting), T-Systems revenue + Germany/Europe corporate accounts are the first to suffer. ~15% of revenue has direct corporate spending exposure.
DTEGY.research_full:
# Deutsche Telekom AG (DTEGY) — Full Research

## Price: $33.82 | P/E TTM: 16.1 | P/E Forward: 12.0 | Div Yield: 3.5% | Market Cap: $165B

---

## What is Deutsche Telekom?

DT is **Europe's largest telecom by revenue** (€119B FY2025) and owner of **57% of T-Mobile US**. Successor to the German state operator Deutsche Bundespost (privatized in 1995).

**History**:
- **1995**: privatization of Deutsche Bundespost Telekom
- **2001**: acquisition of VoiceStream Wireless in the US (seed of T-Mobile US)
- **2014**: **Tim Höttges** becomes CEO
- **April 2020**: T-Mobile US + Sprint merger closes
- **2024–2025**: exercises call options + SoftBank sale → DT at **57.1% of TMUS**
- **2025**: Höttges re-contracted +5 years
- **January 2026**: announces €2B buyback for FY26

## 4 Segments

| Segment | % Revenue | Q1 2026 Revenue | Q1 2026 EBITDA AL |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **T-Mobile US** | ~64% | €19.7B / $23.1B | €7.7B / $9.1B |
| **Germany** | ~21% | €6.3B | €2.7B |
| **Europe** (10 countries) | ~10% | €3.1B | €1.2B |
| **T-Systems** | ~3% | €1.0B | €0.08B |

## T-Mobile US — the anchor

- DT owns **57.1%** post-2024–2025 (call options + SoftBank sale)
- TMUS market cap ~$209B (May 2026)
- **DT's stake worth ~$119B = 72% of DT's market cap ($165B)**
- TMUS = #2 wireless carrier in the US, ~110M postpaid customers
- Leader in 5G mid-band coverage

## Q1 2026 — Reported May 13, 2026

| Metric | Q1 2026 | YoY |
|--------|---------|-----|
| Service Revenue | **€25.0B** | +4.6% organic |
| **Adj EBITDA AL** | **€11.5B** | **+7.5% organic** (+2.0% reported) |
| **FCF AL** | **€5.7B** | Q1 record |
| **Adj EPS** | **€0.54** | **+7.9%** |

## FY2025 — Reported Feb 2026

| Metric | FY2025 | YoY |
|--------|--------|-----|
| Revenue | €119.1B | +4.2% organic |
| **Adj EBITDA AL** | **€44.2B** | +4.7% organic |
| **FCF AL** | **€19.5B** | +2.0% |
| Adj EPS | €2.00 | +5.2% |

## FY2026 Guidance (raised)

- Adj EBITDA AL **~€47.5B**
- FCF AL **>€19.8B**
- Adj EPS **~€2.20**

## Dividend + Buyback

- **FY2025 dividend €1.00/share** (+11% — record)
- Paid April 8, 2026 · Ex April 2
- ADR DTEGY (1:1 ratio) received ~$1.18, yield ~3.5%
- Policy: 40–60% of sustainable Adj EPS
- **FY26 buyback up to €2B** (announced January 5, 2026)
  - Tranche 1: ~15.6M shares / ~€471M (completed Q1)
  - Tranche 2: up to €550M (April–June)
- **Total capital return ~5% of market cap/year**

## Germany Fiber

- **12.6M FTTH homes** at YE 2025
- Target +1M in 2026 (Germany)
- **25M FTTH homes in Germany by 2030**
- Berlin reached 1M FTTH milestone in 2026

## Europe — 10 Countries

Austria, Croatia, Czech Republic, Greece, Hungary, Poland, Slovakia, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Netherlands. **Exiting Romania**.

- 8.6M converged (FMC) customers
- 5G coverage 89.3%

## Why is it trading near its 52-week low?

1. **ver.di strike Germany** — 60,000 workers, +6.6% pay + €660 bonus
2. **Weak USD** compresses TMUS translation (Q1 +7.5% organic vs +2.0% reported = FX)
3. **Cable MVNO at TMUS** — Comcast + Charter capturing ~39% of net postpaid adds in 2025
4. Headline GAAP Q1 -28% (base effects)

## Analyst Consensus

- Rating: **Strong Buy** (18 Buy / 0 Hold / 0 Sell on MarketScreener)
- XETRA average target: €37.11 (range €33–€44)
- ADR target: **~$42** vs $33.82 spot = **+24% upside**

## Anchor Fact — the setup that defines the thesis

**Deutsche Telekom is worth $165B in the stock market. Its 57% stake in T-Mobile US alone is worth ~$119B. This means the market assigns only ~$46B to the rest of the business — all of Germany (12.6M homes with fiber), 10 European countries with 8.6M converged customers, and T-Systems — which together generated €20.5B in Adj EBITDA AL in 2025.**

Implied stub = 2.2x EV/EBITDA vs European telecom peers at 5–7x. A brutal discount.

(Source: TMUS market cap CompaniesMarketCap May 2026 + DT Q1 2026 release)

## Thesis in one line

> Buy Europe's largest telecom with a 3.5% yield + €2B buyback (~5% capital return) when the market values the T-Mobile US stake ($119B) at 72% of total market cap ($165B) — implying all of Germany (12.6M FTTH homes), 10 European countries (8.6M converged customers), and T-Systems (B2B IT) trade at ~2.2x EV/EBITDA vs the 5–7x sector average. Q1 2026 delivered +7.5% Adj EBITDA AL organic + record FCF + guidance raised. Tim Höttges: CEO for 12 years, extended another 5, growing dividend, structural fiber moat.

---

*Research date: May 19, 2026 | Next review: Nov 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
DTEGY.one_liner: Deutsche Telekom owns 57% of T-Mobile US — and that stake alone is worth nearly three-quarters of what the whole company trades for on the stock market.
DTEGY.why_short: You're essentially buying T-Mobile US at a discount and getting the rest of the European business — Germany's #1 telecom with fiber rolling into 25 million homes — nearly for free.
DTEGY.risk_short: If T-Mobile US keeps losing ground to cable companies bundling phone plans, DT's biggest asset grows more slowly, and the discount-to-value story loses its main engine.

EADSY.summary_short: **The #1 commercial aircraft manufacturer in the world** — Airbus overtook Boeing in 2019 and has held the lead ever since. Its flagship product is the **A320 family** (A319/A320/A321), the best-selling aircraft in civil aviation history with **more than 12,487 delivered since 1988**. Also manufactures the A220 (regional), A330 + A350 (long-haul widebodies), helicopters (H125, H145, H160 — #1 globally civil + military) and has a Defence & Space division (Eurofighter Typhoon, A400M military transport, Galileo satellites, Ariane launchers). 3 segments: **Commercial Aircraft ~72% of revenue**, **Helicopters ~12%**, **Defence & Space ~16%**. Legal HQ in Leiden, Netherlands; operational HQ in **Toulouse, France** (where every A320 in the world is assembled). Assembly lines also in Hamburg (DE), Seville (ES), Tianjin (CN) and Mobile, Alabama (US). ~145,000 employees across 35 countries. **Guillaume Faury has been CEO since 2019**, contract renewed in 2025. Trades in Paris as AIR.PA + ADR **EADSY** in the US (ratio: 1 ADR = 1/4 ordinary share). **FY2025 (reported Feb 19, 2026)**: revenue **€73.4B (+6.1% YoY)**, adjusted EBIT **€7.1B (margin 9.7% vs 7.7% in 2024)**, FCF €4.6B, **793 deliveries** (607 A320 family, 93 A220, 36 A330, 57 A350), **record backlog €619 billion / 9,037 aircraft**. **Q1 2026 (reported April 28, 2026)**: revenue €12.7B (-7%), Adj EBIT €300M (-52%), deliveries 114 vs 136 — weak due to Pratt & Whitney GTF engine shortage constraining A320neo ramp. **FY2026 guidance reaffirmed**: ~870 deliveries, Adj EBIT ~€7.5B, FCF ~€4.5B. **FY2025 dividend €3.20/share** (ex April 21, pay April 23, 2026, +6.7% vs €3.00 in 2024) — ADR received ~$0.90, yield 1.89% at $50.12. **Buyback** only for employee compensation (~4.14M shares), NOT aggressive capital return. 2026–2027 catalysts: A320neo target 75/month by end of 2027 (all-time record for civil aviation), A350 12/month by 2028, 737 MAX 7/10 certification (Boeing) now expected late 2026, 777X Boeing slipped to 2027 (delay #6 — Airbus keeps widebody window open). Acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems operations (~4,000 employees) backward-integrates aerostructures. Stock at $50.12 near 52-week low ($44–$64.35), forward P/E 20.8x, yield 1.89%.
EADSY.summary_what:
Airbus SE is **the #1 commercial aircraft manufacturer in the world** + one of the largest aerospace and defense groups on the planet. Legal HQ Leiden, Netherlands; operational HQ Toulouse, France. ~145,000 employees across 35 countries.

**History**:
- **1970**: founded as Airbus Industrie GIE (France/Germany/UK/Spain consortium)
- **2000**: reorganized as EADS (European Aeronautic Defence and Space Company)
- **2014**: rebranded to **Airbus Group** + corporate simplification
- **2017**: final rebrand to **Airbus SE** (Societas Europaea, HQ Leiden)
- **2019**: **Guillaume Faury** becomes CEO (successor to Tom Enders)
- **2019**: Airbus surpasses Boeing in annual deliveries for the first time (has not relinquished the lead since)
- **2020**: COVID — deliveries drop to 566 (from 863 in 2019)
- **2025**: Faury re-contracted +5 years; Airbus announces acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems A220/A320/A350 operations
- **January 2026**: Matthieu Louvot new CEO of Airbus Helicopters (succeeds Bruno Even)
- **May 2026**: Eric Kirstetter new EVP Strategy

**3 Segments**:

**(1) Commercial Aircraft (~72% revenue, ~75% Adj EBIT)** — the core:
- **A220 family** (regional 100–150 seats) — production target 12/month by 2027 (lowered from 14)
- **A320 family** (A319/A320/A321 narrowbody) — best-selling aircraft in history: 12,487+ delivered since 1988. Production target **75/month by end of 2027** = all-time record for civil aviation
- **A330** (widebody) — updated A330neo in production
- **A350** (long-haul widebody) — target 12/month by 2028
- **Commercial backlog Q1 2026**: **9,037 aircraft** vs 8,754 at YE 2025
- **Narrowbody market share**: A320 family ~60% vs Boeing 737 MAX ~40%
- **Widebody market share**: Boeing still leads (787, 777)

**(2) Helicopters (~12% revenue, ~10% Adj EBIT)** — #1 globally civil + military:
- **H125** (light single) — best-seller
- **H145** (light twin) — medical, rescue, military
- **H160** (medium twin) — recent flagship
- **H225M Caracal** (heavy military)
- **Tiger** (combat)
- **NH90** (medium multi-role military)
- Q1 2026 deliveries: 56 (vs 51 prior); revenue stable at €1.6B

**(3) Defence & Space (~16% revenue)** — the 2025–2027 catalyst:
- **Eurofighter Typhoon** — European fighter, partnership BAE + Leonardo + Airbus. Ramping production from 14 to 20 aircraft/year, new orders Italy + Germany + Turkey
- **A400M Atlas** — heavy military transport (weak backlog, <50 aircraft)
- **Satellites**: Galileo (European GPS system), OneSat (flexible satellites), LEO constellations
- **Ariane launchers** (ArianeGroup JV with Safran)
- **FCAS** (Future Combat Air System with France/Germany/Spain) — stalled in Phase 1B; Faury said he would support a 'two-fighter solution'
- Q1 2026: revenue €2.8B **(+7% YoY)**, Adj EBIT €130M (vs €77M = nearly 2x)

**Leadership**:
- **CEO Airbus SE**: **Guillaume Faury** since 2019 (re-contracted 2025)
- **CFO**: Thomas Toepfer
- **CEO Commercial Aircraft**: Christian Scherer
- **CEO Helicopters**: Matthieu Louvot (since April 1, 2026)
- **CEO Defence & Space**: Mike Schoellhorn
- **EVP Strategy**: Eric Kirstetter (since May 18, 2026)

**FY2026 Guidance (reaffirmed at Q1)**:

**Capital Return**:
- **FY2025 dividend €3.20/share** (+6.7% vs €3.00 in 2024)

EADSY.summary_why: EADSY at $50.12 (near 52-week low of $44–$64.35) is buying the #1 commercial aircraft manufacturer in the world with a 10-year backlog (9,037 aircraft, €619B) at a moment of maximum short-term anxiety (Q1 -52% EBIT) that is 100% attributable to a temporary Pratt & Whitney engine bottleneck. Specific reasons: (1) **Backlog €619B / 9,037 aircraft = ~10 years of sold production at the current pace of 870 deliveries/year**: the backlog is 4x larger than Airbus's entire market cap (~€143B). That means revenue visibility through ~2036 without a single new order coming in. No industrial company in the world has this level of visibility. (2) **Structural commercial duopoly Airbus vs Boeing — and Airbus is winning**: has outpaced Boeing in deliveries since 2019 and has not given it back. In narrowbodies (70% of the global market), A320 family holds ~60% share vs Boeing 737 MAX 40%. Boeing is still working through 737 MAX 7/10 cert (slipped to late 2026), 777X (slipped to 2027, delay #6), and quality issues post-Alaska Airlines incident January 2024. No viable third competitor (COMAC C919 still without Western certification). (3) **Q1 2026 -52% is 100% an engine effect — not demand**: the issue is not orders (coming in ~95% faster than 2025) but supply of GTF engines from Pratt & Whitney (RTX). It's a temporary bottleneck that resolves 2026–2027 when P&W finishes replacing the defective turbine blades. Demand intact, supply temporarily compressed = temporary earnings compression. (4) **A320neo ramp to 75/month by 2027 = all-time record in civil aviation**: no aircraft family in history has reached 75/month. With that, Airbus generates ~900 deliveries/year from A320neo alone + ~870–900 total = Commercial Aircraft revenue jumping from €52.6B (FY2025) to ~€65–70B by 2028. Massive operating leverage. (5) **Defence & Space inflecting higher — Europe rearming**: Q1 2026 D&S EBIT nearly doubled YoY (€130M vs €77M). Eurofighter ramping to 20 aircraft/year with new orders from Italy, Germany, Turkey. Post-Ukraine European rearmament generates a multi-year tailwind for the division. If FCAS unlocks 2026–2027 → another catalyst. (6) **Helicopters — #1 globally civil + military — anchored business**: Q1 deliveries +10% YoY to 56 helicopters. Global military market expanding + civil fleets renewing. Less cyclical than Commercial Aircraft, high recurring services revenue. (7) **Disciplined capital allocation**: Airbus has paid a growing dividend since 2017 (uninterrupted except COVID 2020). €3.20/share FY2025 (+6.7%). No aggressive buyback (only employee comp ~4.14M shares) but also no dilution via massive M&A — Spirit AeroSystems is strategic backward integration, not wasteful growth-by-acquisition. (8) **Boeing remains paralyzed — window stays open for Airbus**: 737 MAX cert delays + continued 777X slippage + quality issues post-Alaska Air + IAM 2024 strikes + cash burn. While Boeing repairs its house, Airbus is capturing permanent share. Airlines that switch from Boeing to Airbus rarely switch back (pilot training, parts inventory, maintenance contracts — all stay with Airbus). (9) **Forward P/E 20.8x = reasonable for a 10-year-backlog duopoly**: peers Lockheed Martin 17x, Northrop 19x, RTX 21x, Honeywell 22x. Airbus at 20.8x with (a) 10 years of revenue visibility, (b) ongoing margin expansion (FY25 9.7% vs 7.7% FY24), (c) defence inflection, (d) structural duopoly = mispriced by short-term fear. (10) **Analyst consensus Buy + average target €218 (AIR.PA) = ~+20% upside on EADSY ADR**: 15 Buy / 8 Hold / 0 Sell. Re-rating to P/E 22–23x when P&W engines normalize + Q4 2026 deliveries accelerate = thesis paid.
EADSY.summary_risk: The risks are measurable: (1) **Pratt & Whitney GTF engine shortfall — the #1 driver of Q1 -52%**: P&W (RTX subsidiary) is not delivering engines at the promised rate due to defects in turbine blades requiring replacement. This blocks A320neo deliveries and forced Airbus to lower its ramp guidance (from a firm 75/month by 2027 to 70–75/month). If the situation extends beyond 2027, FY26–27 EBIT suffers materially. CFM (A320 alternative engine) has limited capacity to compensate. (2) **Spirit AeroSystems integration drag 2026**: the acquisition of A220/A320/A350 operations from Spirit (~4,000 employees) adds pressure on cash flow and execution. Spirit had chronic quality issues — Airbus inherits them. If fixes take longer or require more capex, the FCF target of €4.5B erodes. (3) **A320 panel quality issue 2026**: a manufacturing defect in panels affecting additional deliveries was identified. Not catastrophic but adds noise to Q2–Q3 2026 metrics. (4) **Boeing recovery narrative — gap closes 2027–2028**: if 737 MAX 7/10 certifies late 2026 (FAA said 'no major obstacles' in April 2026) + 777X certifies in 2027 + Boeing normalizes 737 production to 52/month by end of 2026, the market rotates from 'Airbus duopoly winner' toward 'normalized duopoly' and Airbus's premium compresses. (5) **Trump tariffs 2026 = wild card**: FY2026 guidance assumes 'currently applicable tariffs.' Any escalation (US tariffs on European aircraft in response to EU tariffs on Boeing aircraft) creates direct margin headwind — Airbus exports ~40% of aircraft to Asia-Pacific/Middle East via USD rotation. (6) **USD/EUR FX**: commercial aircraft revenue is denominated in USD but costs are in EUR. Weaker USD = lower revenue translated to EUR. Q1 2026 already showed the effect. If USD stays weak below 1.20 EUR/USD for all of 2026, the Adj EBIT guidance of €7.5B becomes difficult. (7) **FCAS stalled — Eurofighter as Plan B is not ideal**: Future Combat Air System (Eurofighter successor) has been stuck for years in France vs Germany vs Spain negotiations. Faury acknowledged this. If FCAS is scrapped and Eurofighter ramps to just 20/year, Defence & Space growth is limited. UK Tempest (BAE/Leonardo/Mitsubishi) is a competitor that could capture exports if FCAS collapses. (8) **Helicopters — pricing pressure + competition**: H125 faces Bell 505 (US) low-cost competition. H160 ramp slower than expected vs Sikorsky. Helicopters is ~12% of revenue but ~10% of EBIT — not the core engine, but deterioration here erodes the 'diversified' narrative. (9) **A400M declining backlog**: fewer than 50 aircraft in backlog, no major recent export orders. If remaining orders wind down and no new ones come in, A400M approaches the end of its program — write-down risk. (10) **CEO Faury succession post-2027**: Faury was re-contracted in 2025 but his tenure ends 2027–2030. No successor is clearly identified (Scherer Commercial CEO + Schoellhorn D&S CEO are candidates but neither is designated). Poorly handled succession = execution risk. (11) **Boeing could break the duopoly by cutting prices aggressively**: if Boeing's balance sheet continues deteriorating (cash burn in 2024–2025 was massive), it could accept irrational pricing to preserve share — a narrowbody price war would hurt margins for both.

Ecolab quantifies + charges for those savings — customers pay happily because they save cash + meet ESG targets. Integration risk too — CoolIT is startup culture vs. Ecolab's industrial culture. (2) **Raw materials + energy costs**: caustics, surfactants, and polymers are key inputs. 10-14% surcharge already implemented but margin slippage has been flagged by analysts. If oil >$100 or European gas >€80/MWh sustained, the surcharge won't cover everything. (3) **FX + foodservice slowdown**: ~40% of revenue is ex-US. If the dollar strengthens or a US recession reduces restaurant traffic, the Institutional segment takes a hit. (4) **Trump 2026 tariffs**: Ecolab imports some specialty chemicals + sensor components. Section 122 tariffs an additional +10%. Competes with European chemists (BASF, Solvay) and Japanese (Kao) — if they get carve-outs and Ecolab doesn't, that's share loss. (5) **Surcharge pushback**: large customers (McDonald's, Hilton, Marriott) renegotiate multi-year contracts. (6) **Data center cooling competition — Vertiv has a head start**: Vertiv (VRT) is already public and trades at a forward price vs. profit of ~35x with liquid cooling exposure. CoolIT vs. Vertiv vs. Schneider Electric vs. hyperscaler in-house (Microsoft built its own cooling) — Ecolab is entering a competitive market, not a monopoly. (7) **Bioprocessing dependency** (Life Sciences segment, +11% Q1): if bioprocessing destocking continues or pharma cuts capex, Life Sciences plateaus. (8) **Pest Elimination — labor inflation**: 4,500+ field service specialists. Wage inflation hits margins directly. Hard to pass 100% of pricing through pest control. (9) **Christophe Beck consolidation = single point of failure**: Chair + CEO + President. If he steps down or loses board confidence, transition is uncertain. Co-COOs have a functional split but no peer-CEO talent is clearly identified. (10) **Premium valuation forward P/E 29.4x = no margin for error**: if EPS misses in Q2 or Q3 2026, multiple compression of -10-15% is likely. The market is already paying a premium for the AI cooling thesis — execution must deliver.

EQIX.summary_short: Equinix operates **280 IBX (International Business Exchange) data centers** in **77 cities across 36 countries** — the world's #1 operator of interconnection data centers. What makes it different from Digital Realty (DLR, its rival and our pick #68): it doesn't compete so much on raw megawatts, but on the **density of physical connections** inside each building — more than **500,000 cross-connects** between customers inside its campuses. Once your company plugs its servers into AWS, Azure, Google, Oracle inside the same Equinix building, leaving costs months and millions. Q1 2026: revenue $2.44B (+10%), AFFO +12% (AFFO = the cash a REIT actually generates, the profit measure used for this kind of company), a record 51% EBITDA margin. 11 straight years of dividend increases since its 2015 REIT conversion. EQIX.summary_what:
Equinix Inc. (Nasdaq: EQIX) is the world's #1 operator of **carrier-neutral interconnection** data centers. Founded in 1998 in Silicon Valley by Albert Avery + Jay Adelson. A REIT (a company required to distribute most of its income to shareholders) since 2015.

**Scale (2026)**:
- **280 IBX data centers** (vs. ~270 a year ago)
- **77 markets / metros**
- **36 countries, 6 continents**
- **>10,500 customers**
- **13,700 employees**
- **~3 GW of developable power capacity**
- **854 MW of operational PPAs in 2025** (PPAs = long-term power purchase agreements; vs. 299 MW in 2024)
- **29 PPAs / 1,400+ MW** under contract in 12 countries
- **>500,000 total interconnections** in Q4 2025 (437,700 physical cross-connects + 69,500 virtual via Equinix Fabric)

**Equinix vs. DLR (Digital Realty, our pick #68 at $194.93)**:
- DLR has **more raw megawatts** for hyperscale wholesale (~5 GW buildable, ~2,850 MW operational)
- Equinix has **more metros + more retail colocation density + more interconnections**
- They are the only 2 public data center REITs of scale; the rest is hyperscaler self-build (Microsoft, Amazon, Google build their own)

**3 segments**:
- **Retail Colocation** — most of the revenue, high margin, sticky enterprise customers
- **xScale** — a wholesale joint venture for hyperscalers; JV with **GIC (37.5%) + CPP Investments (37.5%) + Equinix (25%)**; the US JV targets **>$15B / >1.5 GW** of hyperscale capacity on campuses of >100 MW
- **Interconnection** (Equinix Fabric / Internet Exchange / Cloud Exchange Fabric) — Q1 2026 revenue +9% YoY at constant currency

**Leadership**:
- **CEO/President**: **Adaire Fox-Martin** (May 2024, replaced Charles Meyers; comes from Google Cloud, previously SAP/Oracle; on the board since 2020)
- **CFO**: Keith Taylor (announced his 2026 retirement, succession in process)
- Founders: Al Avery + Jay Adelson
EQIX.summary_why: EQIX at $1,068.51 means buying the **world's #1 interconnection data center operator** + the **most densely connected provider in the world** right as AI capacity demand is breaking records and Equinix holds an exclusive partnership with NVIDIA for preconfigured AI factories. Specific reasons: (1) **A network-effect moat — irreplicable**: >500,000 physical cross-connects between customers inside the IBXs. Every new customer that arrives wants to connect with those already there (AWS, Azure, Google, Oracle, Cloudflare, Akamai, all the Tier-1 ISPs). That creates a flywheel that neither Microsoft nor Amazon can replicate — they want to close their ecosystems; Equinix opens its own. (2) **The NVIDIA Instant AI Factory partnership** — Equinix is **the first global provider** of preconfigured DGX GB300 / DGX B300 (Blackwell Ultra) SuperPODs in **45 markets**. For companies that want AI now without buying GPUs or building a data center, Equinix is the on-ramp. (3) **The xScale JV: $15B / 1.5 GW** with GIC + CPP — it funds growth without diluting shareholders or loading up debt. Equinix contributes the land + operating know-how; the partners bring the capital. (4) **Q1 2026 records**: revenue $2.44B (+10%), AFFO $1.065B (+12%) (AFFO = the cash a REIT actually generates), a **record 51% EBITDA margin**, and raised FY2026 guidance. (5) **11 straight years of dividend increases** — a REIT whose dividend grew +10% in Feb 2026 to $5.16/quarter = $20.64 annualized = a ~1.93% yield. (6) **~1 GW of powered land** added under control in Q1 2026 — the pipeline for the next 3-5 years. (7) **The nuclear pivot 2025-2026** — the Stellaria 500 MW power agreement, micro-reactors for data center power. The grid alone isn't enough for AI density; Equinix locked in power 5+ years ahead. (8) **Recent analyst target raises**: Deutsche Bank $1,207, Truist $1,215, Mizuho $1,200 — consensus skewing higher after the Q1 beat. Avg target ~$1,143 = +7% upside (conservative), but the recent targets sit higher. (9) **Diversification alongside DLR**: we already hold DLR (pick #68) — they are the two sides of the data center trade. DLR = more wholesale + AI hyperscale brute force. EQIX = more enterprise interconnection + premium retail. Owning both = covering the whole barrel.
EQIX.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **AI capex overcommitment is the #1**: Q1 2026 revenue **missed** what Wall Street analysts expected ($2.444B vs. $2.516B expected). If the hyperscalers slow down (Microsoft canceled some leases in late 2025), the ~1 GW of speculative land under-earns. The question isn't whether AI demand is real (it is), but whether Equinix is building too much for future prices that may not materialize. (2) **Power scarcity is the binding constraint**: in Northern Virginia, Phoenix, Santa Clara, Dublin, Singapore — no megawatts are available in the short term. Nuclear power agreements help but take years. Equinix can have customers but no power. (3) **Interest rate sensitivity**: REITs sell off when borrowing costs rise. Equinix carries a $14B+ debt stack. If the Fed turns hawkish, the valuation compresses. (4) **Competition**: DLR has more raw MW for wholesale; hyperscalers self-build (Microsoft pushed back on third-party leasing); edge entrants (CoreWeave, Lambda) attack AI-specific workloads. Pricing power eventually gets compromised. (5) **CEO transition risk**: Adaire Fox-Martin has only been there 2 years — still proving herself. CFO Keith Taylor retiring in 2026 = an additional simultaneous transition. (6) **The REIT requirement to pay out 90% of income** limits its ability to reinvest without issuing shares. Every raise dilutes. (7) **Backlog growth depends on hyperscaler decisions**, which are lumpy and opaque. (8) **A rich valuation**: ~25x AFFO. If AFFO growth moderates to single digits, the multiple compresses. (9) **Customer concentration**: the top 10 customers are ~25% of revenue; adverse hyperscaler decisions can hit hard.

## Price: $1,068.51 | AFFO multiple: ~25x | Div Yield: 1.93% | Market Cap: ~$105.6B | Q1 2026 EPS: $4.20

---

## What is Equinix?

**The world's #1 operator of carrier-neutral interconnection data centers**. Founded in 1998 in Silicon Valley by Al Avery + Jay Adelson. A REIT since 2015. 11 straight years of dividend increases.

## Scale (2026)

## 3 Segments

## Anchor Fact

**Equinix has 280 data centers in 77 cities — but what matters isn't the buildings, it's the more than 500,000 cables connecting its customers to each other inside them. Once your company plugs its servers into AWS, Azure, Google, Oracle inside the same Equinix building, leaving costs months and millions. That's why Equinix has raised its dividend 11 years in a row and just reported the highest EBITDA margin in its history (51%) in Q1 2026.**

ESLOY.summary_what: EssilorLuxottica is the French-Italian giant that dominates the entire eyewear value chain. They make the lenses (Essilor: Varilux, Transitions, Crizal, Kodak Lens), design and produce the frames (Luxottica: Ray-Ban, Oakley, Persol, Oliver Peoples, Vogue Eyewear, Arnette, Costa Del Mar, Alain Mikli), hold exclusive licenses for luxury brands (Prada, Chanel, Tiffany, Versace, Burberry, Dolce & Gabbana, Michael Kors, Coach, Ralph Lauren, Giorgio Armani), and own the stores where you buy them (LensCrafters, Sunglass Hut, Pearle Vision, Target Optical, Apex). They also own EyeMed, the #2 vision insurer in the US, completing the cycle: they insure you, examine you, sell you the frames, make the lenses, and deliver them to you. Total vertical integration. The big new growth driver is Ray-Ban Meta — the AI smart glasses they develop with Meta. They sold 7 million units in 2025 (compared to 2M combined in 2023+2024). In 2026, they launch Oakley and Prada versions, expanding the bet.

Eyewear isn't fashion — it's a recurring, non-discretionary expense: 2.5B people worldwide use corrective lenses and replace them every 2-3 years. Stable demand, strong pricing power, and now a growth boost via AI wearables.
ESLOY.summary_risk: Apple's competition in smart glasses (production expected December 2026, launch 2027) threatens Ray-Ban Meta's current advantage — the main trigger for the 34% drop from highs. If Apple captures market share with a 'better-made' product (according to Bloomberg leaks), EssilorLuxottica loses the premium that currently supports its best growth story. Other risks: Trump tariffs of up to 20% on European imports and 36% on Thailand hit US margins (40% of revenue); a class action was filed in February 2026 over price hikes post-tariffs; Warby Parker and direct-to-consumer models erode share in entry-level optics. # EssilorLuxottica (ESLOY) — Complete Research

## Price: $122.75 | P/E: 41.89 | P/E Forward: 25.56 | Div Yield: 1.87% | Market Cap: $112.9B

*Note: ESLOY is the ADR OTC. The primary stock trades on Euronext Paris as EL.PA (1 ESLOY ≈ 0.25 EL.PA).*

---

## What It Is

EssilorLuxottica is the **global monopoly on eyewear** — the result of the 2018 merger between Essilor (French lenses, #1 worldwide in ophthalmic lenses) and Luxottica (Italian frames, #1 worldwide in frames). The group controls **~60% of the eyewear market in the US** and has a comparable global share. In corrective lenses specifically, they hold 42% of the global market share.

It's not just a lens company. It's a complete vertical ecosystem: they design, manufacture, license, distribute, retail, and insure. If you have glasses on your face, they probably came from some part of this company.

## Business Model — Total Vertical Integration

### Own Brands (Luxottica)
| Brand | Segment |
|-------|----------|
| **Ray-Ban** | Iconic, mass-premium, smart glasses |
| **Oakley** | Sports, performance, new Meta AI line |
| **Persol** | Premium Italian, handmade |
| **Oliver Peoples** | Luxury Californian |
| **Vogue Eyewear** | Accessible women's fashion |
| **Arnette** | Youthful, action sports |
| **Costa Del Mar** | Fishing, outdoor premium |
| **Alain Mikli** | Haute couture |

### Luxury Licenses
Exclusive licenses with **Prada, Chanel, Tiffany, Versace, Burberry, Dolce & Gabbana, Michael Kors, Coach, Ralph Lauren, Giorgio Armani, Brunello Cucinelli, Miu Miu, Tory Burch** and more. The renewal with Prada in December 2025 was for 10 years.

### Lenses (Essilor)
| Brand | Specialty |
|-------|--------------|
| **Varilux** | #1 worldwide in progressive lenses |
| **Transitions** | Photochromic (adapt to sunlight) |
| **Crizal** | Premium anti-reflective |
| **Kodak Lens** | Licensed mass brand |

### Own Retail
| Chain | Reach |
|--------|---------|
| **LensCrafters** | ~1,300 stores in the US |
| **Sunglass Hut** | ~2,700 stores globally |
| **Pearle Vision** | ~500 stores in the US |
| **Target Optical** | Within-Target |
| **Apex by Sunglass Hut** | Premium sunglasses |
| **Ray-Ban Stores** | Global flagship stores |

### Insurance
**EyeMed** — the second-largest vision insurer in the US. Closes the loop: insures you, examines you, sells you the frames, makes the lenses, collects insurance.

## The Catalyst: Ray-Ban Meta (Smart Glasses)

Partnership with **Meta Platforms** — renewed and expanded in 2024 for $5B and an additional 10 years.

### Impressive Numbers
- **7 million units sold in 2025** (Ray-Ban Meta + Oakley Meta)
- vs 2 million combined in 2023+2024 → **+250% YoY**
- Described by the company as "the dominant driver" of wholesale growth

### Exploding Market
- 2025: 6M units / $1.2B market
- 2026 projected: **20M units / $5.6B market**
- EssilorLuxottica + Meta have the technical and distribution lead

### 2026 Expansion
- **Oakley Meta HSTN** — sports version, ~$360, weather-resistant (already launched)
- **Prada Meta** — first luxury fashion brand in smart glasses (leaked 2026)
- Next generations with integrated display (Bloomberg rumor 2026)

## FY2025 Results — Historic Record

| Metric | Value | vs 2024 |

| **Q4 Revenue growth** | +18.4% | Acceleration |

| **Adj. Operating Profit** | €4.5B | Record |
| **Free Cash Flow** | €2.796B | Historic record |
| **Net Income** | ~€2.4B | — |

> **First year in EssilorLuxottica's history with double-digit annual growth** at constant exchange rates.

## Why Now

### 1. The Drop is a Gift
- 52w High: **$186.81**
- Current price: **$122.75** → **-34% from highs**
- Reason for the drop: fear of Apple smart glasses + Trump tariffs + rotation out of European luxury
- **Apple doesn't launch until 2027** — EssilorLuxottica has 18+ months of advantage

### 2. Real Monopoly with Pricing Power
- 60% US share, 42% global in corrective lenses
- Can pass tariffs to consumers without losing customers
- 2.5B people worldwide use glasses — non-discretionary demand

### 3. Ray-Ban Meta is a New Business
- High margin, triple-digit growth
- Meta pays for R&D; EssilorLuxottica provides distribution and brand
- Oakley and Prada extensions multiply the TAM in 2026

### 4. Solid 2026 Guidance
- Revenue: **€27-28B**
- Operating margin: **19-20%** (improvement from 16% in 2025)
- Mid-single-digit growth confirmed despite tariffs

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |

| **Market Cap** | $112.9B |
| **P/E TTM** | 41.89x |
| **P/E Forward** | 25.56x |
| **EPS TTM** | $2.93 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 1.87% |
| **Dividend Rate** | $2.25 |
| **52w High** | $186.81 |
| **52w Low** | $109.00 |
| **Drawdown from high** | -34% |

## Analyst Consensus

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Consensus | **Buy** |
| Analysts Buy | 19-22 |
| Analysts Hold | 1-2 |
| Analysts Sell | 0-1 |
| Average Target (EL.PA) | €318-326 |
| Estimated ADR Target | **~$165** |
| **Upside ADR from $122.75** | **+34.4%** |

## Risks

### 1. Apple Smart Glasses (the big bear case)
- Production Dec 2026, launch spring/summer 2027
- Rumored pricing: $499-$799 (premium vs Ray-Ban Meta)
- Bloomberg leak: "similar to Meta product but better built"

### 2. Trump Tariffs
- Up to 20% on European imports, 36% Thailand, 145% China
- Class action Feb 2026 over price hikes post-tariffs
- Mitigation: considering partial production move to the US

### 3. Warby Parker & DTC Erosion
- Warby Parker has gained share in entry-level ranges
- More threat to mass segment than premium

### 4. Premium Valuation
- P/E TTM 41.89x leaves little room for error

### 5. EUR/USD Currency
- Company reports in EUR; ADR in USD

## Conclusion

ESLOY at $122.75 is an opportunity to buy the global eyewear monopoly at a 34% discount from highs, just as:
1. They reported their best year in history (+11.2% revenue)
2. They have the hottest new hardware category (Ray-Ban Meta, 7M units sold)
3. The market punishes them for 2027 fears (Apple) — 18+ months of structural advantage
4. Dividend 1.87% + pricing power to mitigate tariffs
5. Analyst consensus Buy with ~34% upside to target

The boring business (corrective lenses for 2.5B people) anchors the defensive thesis. The exciting business (AI smart glasses) provides the upside.

FCX.summary_short: The #1 copper producer in the US and owner of the giant Grasberg mine in Indonesia (copper + gold). Big trend: AI, data centers, and EVs need massive amounts of copper.
FCX.summary_what: Freeport-McMoRan mines copper, gold, and molybdenum in the US (Morenci, Arizona — the largest copper mine in North America), Indonesia (Grasberg — one of the largest copper and gold deposits in the world), Peru (Cerro Verde), and Chile (El Abra). It produces about 3.4 billion pounds of copper and about 1 million ounces of gold annually.
FCX.summary_why: Copper's long-term trend: AI data centers could use 1.1M tons annually by 2030. Supply shortage projected from 2026+. Grasberg restarting production in Q2 2026 — strong earnings boost. 2026E EPS of $2.95 (+93% vs 2025). JPMorgan projects copper at $5.67/lb in Q2 2026.
FCX.summary_risk: A mud rush at Grasberg (Sept 2025) cut production by about 35% and caused 7 fatalities. Indonesian regulations may tighten concentrate exports. Copper prices have big swings — every $0.10/lb change equals about $400M in earnings.

# Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) — Full Research

## Price: $56.48 | Price vs. profit: 37.0 | Forward price vs. profit: 19.1 | Dividend (the cash they pay you): 1.06% | Market Cap: $81B

---

## What is Freeport-McMoRan?

Freeport-McMoRan is the **largest publicly traded copper producer in the world** and the **#1 in the US**. It operates mines in four countries, with the most important being **Grasberg in Indonesia** — one of the largest copper and gold deposits on the planet.

## Main Operations

| Mine | Country | Type | Notes |
|------|------|------|-------|
| **Grasberg Block Cave** | Indonesia | Underground copper/gold | Mega deposit. Damaged by mud rush Sept 2025. Restart Q2 2026 |
| **Morenci** | US (Arizona) | Open-pit copper | Largest copper mine in North America (FCX 72%) |
| **Cerro Verde** | Peru | Open-pit copper/molybdenum | FCX 53.56% |
| **Bagdad** | US (Arizona) | Open-pit copper/molybdenum | Planned expansion |
| **Safford/Lone Star** | US (Arizona) | Open-pit copper | Growing production |
| **El Abra** | Chile | Open-pit copper | FCX 51% |

## Revenue by Product (FY2025)

| Product | Revenue | % Total |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Copper (cathode)** | $8.1B | 31.5% |
| **Copper (concentrate)** | $6.3B | 24.5% |
| **Copper (refined)** | $4.4B | 17.1% |
| **Gold** | $3.9B | 15.2% |
| **Molybdenum** | $2.0B | 7.8% |
| **Other** | $1.0B | 3.9% |
| **Total** | ~$25.7B | 100% |

Copper = 73% of revenue. It is fundamentally a bet on copper.

## Production (FY2025)

- **Copper:** ~3.4 billion pounds (below guidance of 3.95B due to Grasberg)
- **Gold:** ~1.0 million ounces (below guidance of 1.3M due to Grasberg)
- **Molybdenum:** ~92 million pounds (above guidance)

## 2025 Results (Full Year)

- **Revenue: ~$25.7B**
- **Net Income: $2.20B** (+16.7% YoY)
- **EPS GAAP: $1.53** (+16.7%)

### Q4 2025:
- Revenue: $5.63B (beat estimate of $5.18B)
- Adjusted EPS: $0.47 (beat estimate of $0.28 — +67.9% surprise)
- Net Income: $406M (+48.2% YoY)

## Grasberg Incident — Mud Rush (Sept 8, 2025)

This is the most important event to understand FCX today:
- ~800,000 tons of wet material entered the Grasberg Block Cave mine
- 7 fatalities
- Copper production in Indonesia fell ~39%, gold fell ~85%
- Stock fell 20.4% immediately
- DMLZ and Big Gossan restarted in Oct 2025
- Grasberg Block Cave phased restart from Q2 2026
- 85% of total production at normal rates expected for H2 2026

## Dividend

- **Annual Dividend:** $0.60/share (~1.06% yield)
- **Policy:** Tied to copper price and free cash flow
- **History:** Cut in 2015-2020 due to commodities crisis and pandemic. Reinstated 2021
- **NOT a Dividend Aristocrat** — the dividend is cyclical
- **Payout ratio:** ~39%

## The Copper Megatrend

1. **AI and Data Centers:** Could consume 500K-1.1M tons of copper annually by 2030
2. **Electric Vehicles:** Use 2.9x more copper than conventional cars
3. **Electrification:** Grid modernization, renewable energy, clean infrastructure
4. **Global Demand:** +24% by 2035 (Wood Mackenzie)
5. **Supply Shortage:** Deficit of 150K-400K+ tons projected for 2026

## Catalysts

1. **Grasberg Restart Q2 2026** — Earnings soar as production normalizes
2. **Copper for AI** — Data centers, chips, cooling, all require massive copper
3. **Copper price rising** — JPMorgan: $5.67/lb in Q2 2026. UBS: $5.90/lb by end of 2026
4. **2026E EPS: $2.95** — Nearly double 2025 ($1.53)
5. **Largest US copper producer** — Benefit from "critical minerals" policy
6. **Structural supply shortage** — Few new copper projects coming online

## Main Risks

1. **Copper price swings:** Every $0.10/lb = ~$400M in earnings. Global recession drops price
2. **Indonesia:** Government bans concentrate exports. Resource nationalism
3. **Operational risk at Grasberg:** Mud rush showed catastrophic risk. Uncertain timeline
4. **China = 50% of global demand:** Chinese slowdown directly impacts copper
5. **High trailing price vs. profit (37x):** Only makes sense if 2026E materializes

## Competitive Position

| Metric | FCX | SCCO | TECK | BHP |
|---------|-----|------|------|-----|
| **Market Cap** | $81B | $90B+ | $25B | $160B |
| **Copper Production** | 3.4B lbs | 2.3B lbs | ~700M lbs | ~3.7B lbs |
| **Dividend Yield** | 1.06% | 6%+ | 0.8% | 5%+ |
| **Operating Margin** | ~25% | ~52% | ~30% | ~45% |

## Analyst Consensus

- **Rating:** Buy / Strong Buy (15-16 analysts)
- **Average price target:** $56 - $63
- **Target range:** $42 - $72
- **Implied upside:** +11% to the average target (~$63)

## Conclusion

FCX at $56.48 is a **direct bet on the copper megatrend** — AI, data centers, EVs, and electrification. The Grasberg restart in Q2-H2 2026 is the main catalyst that should nearly double EPS. The risk is real (Grasberg, Indonesia, price swings), but if you believe the world needs more copper (and it does), FCX is the most direct way to bet on that.

---

*Research date: Mar 24, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
FCX.one_liner: Freeport-McMoRan is the largest US copper producer with a giant mine in Indonesia, crucial for tech like data centers and electric cars.
FCX.why_short: Copper is vital for future tech, and demand is expected to outpace supply in a few years. Grasberg mine will boost production in 2026, potentially increasing profits significantly.
FCX.risk_short: An accident at the Grasberg mine cut production and caused fatalities. Plus, Indonesia might limit exports and copper prices fluctuate, impacting profits.

FICO.summary_short: Fair Isaac owns the 'FICO Score' — the credit number nearly every U.S. bank uses to decide whether to lend you money and at what rate. It's a near-monopoly: when you apply for a mortgage, a car loan or a credit card in the U.S., odds are they're looking at your FICO score. It collects a small royalty every time a score is pulled, millions of times a day, at enormous margins. Two businesses: (1) Scores (the FICO brand, hugely profitable) and (2) analytics/decision software for companies. It does NOT pay a dividend — it uses the cash to buy back its own shares (it just authorized another $2.0 billion on June 8, 2026). The 2026 wrinkle: it's the WORST stock in the S&P 500 this year (~-50%) on fears that the mortgage regulator (FHFA/Bill Pulte) will open the door to a competitor (VantageScore). CEO Will Lansing. Q2 FY2026: revenue $691.7M, EPS $11.14.
FICO.summary_what:
Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE: FICO) is the most influential credit-analytics company in the world. It invented the **FICO Score**, the de facto standard the vast majority of U.S. lenders use to measure a consumer's risk. HQ Bozeman, Montana.

**Two segments**:

**(1) Scores** — the crown jewel. Every time a bank, card issuer or mortgage lender pulls a person's FICO score, Fair Isaac collects a small **royalty**. Since this happens millions of times a day across mortgages, auto loans, cards and personal loans, the margin is enormous (it's practically pure software: the model is already built, each additional score pull costs nearly $0). It's a near-monopoly protected by decades of institutional adoption.

**(2) Software** — the FICO platform (including the 'FICO Platform') that helps companies make automated decisions: fraud detection, loan origination, account management. It's the recurring-subscription growth engine.

**Capital allocation**: Fair Isaac pays **NO dividend**. Instead it buys back its own shares aggressively — on June 8, 2026 it authorized an additional **$2.0 billion** of buybacks — shrinking the share count and lifting EPS.

**Leadership**: CEO **Will Lansing**. Q2 FY2026: revenue $691.7M, EPS $11.14.

**The 2026 controversy**: FICO has been the **worst stock in the S&P 500 year-to-date (~-50%)**. The fear: the agency that regulates U.S. mortgages (FHFA, led by Bill Pulte) is pushing for a rival score (**VantageScore**, from the credit bureaus) to be accepted in government-backed mortgages — which would threaten FICO's near-monopoly in that segment.
FICO.summary_why: FICO at $1,144 means buying a near-monopoly with massive margins — the credit number that runs the U.S. financial system — after the market punished it ~50% in a year over a regulatory fear that may be overblown. Specific reasons: (1) **It's a toll booth**: Fair Isaac collects a royalty every time someone pulls a FICO Score, millions of times a day. It manufactures nothing, carries no inventory: it's software with operating margins among the highest in the market. (2) **A decades-deep moat**: banks, regulators, securitization models and contracts are all built around the FICO Score. Changing an entire industry's standard is painfully slow and expensive — that's the moat. (3) **The ~50% drop created the opportunity**: the VantageScore fear is real, BUT the FICO Score dominates far more than just mortgages (credit cards, auto loans, personal loans), where it remains the undisputed king. The punishment may have been excessive. (4) **Aggressive buybacks**: with no dividend, FICO pours its cash into buying back its own shares ($2.0B authorized June 8, 2026); with fewer shares outstanding, each one is worth more. (5) **Pricing power**: it has raised the price per score pull repeatedly without losing customers — a classic monopoly symptom. Analysts: Buy/Strong Buy, average target ~$1,530 (~+34%).
FICO.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Regulatory risk (VantageScore/FHFA) — the #1**: if the U.S. mortgage agency forces or allows the rival VantageScore in government-backed mortgages, FICO loses its near-monopoly in that segment. It's the reason for the ~50% drop in 2026 and the cloud hanging over the stock. (2) **NO dividend**: this is a bet on growth + buybacks; you collect no income while you wait. (3) **Still a demanding valuation**: even after the fall, it trades at ~36x earnings — the market is still paying up for quality; if growth disappoints, there's room to fall further. (4) **Single-product concentration**: a large share of profit comes from the FICO Score; a blow to that franchise (regulatory or competitive) hits very hard. (5) **Mortgage-cycle sensitivity**: when interest rates rise and mortgages and refinancings slow, score-pull volume in that segment drops. (6) **Reputational/political risk**: being an expensive 'toll booth' on consumers' credit makes it an easy target for politicians looking to make credit cheaper.

# Fair Isaac (FICO) — Full Research

## Price: $1,144.02 | P/E TTM: ~35.9x | Div Yield: 0% | Market Cap: ~$27.8B USD

---

## What is Fair Isaac?

The owner of the **FICO Score**, the credit number nearly every U.S. lender uses to decide who gets a loan and at what rate. HQ Bozeman, Montana. Pays NO dividend.

## 2 Segments

| Segment | What it is | Driver |
|----------|--------|--------|
| **Scores** | A royalty on every FICO Score pull | Near-monopoly, massive margin |
| **Software** | FICO Platform: fraud, origination, decisions | Recurring subscription |

## The 2026 controversy

- **Worst stock in the S&P 500 year-to-date (~-50%)**.
- The fear: the mortgage agency (FHFA / Bill Pulte) is pushing to accept **VantageScore** (a rival) in government-backed mortgages.
- It threatens FICO's near-monopoly **in mortgages** (not in cards/auto/personal loans, where it still dominates).

## Q2 FY2026

| Metric | Q2 FY26 |
|---------|---------|
| Revenue | $691.7M |
| EPS | $11.14 |

## Capital Allocation

- Dividend: **$0**.
- **$2.0 billion** of buybacks authorized on **June 8, 2026**.

## Leadership

- **CEO**: Will Lansing.

## Anchor Fact

**Every time someone in the U.S. applies for a mortgage, a car loan or a credit card, the bank almost always looks at a single number to decide: the 'FICO Score'. That company — Fair Isaac — collects a small royalty every time that number is pulled, millions of times a day, without manufacturing a single thing. It's like collecting a toll on the highway that all of the country's credit travels through. In 2026 it became the worst stock in the S&P 500 (down ~50%) on fear the government will let a competitor in — which left it, for the first time in years, on sale.**

## Top 5 Risks

1. **Regulatory risk (VantageScore/FHFA)** — a threat to the mortgage monopoly
2. **NO dividend** — growth + buybacks
3. **Still a demanding valuation** (~36x)
4. **Single-product concentration** (the Score)
5. **Mortgage cycle** — score-pull volume rises and falls with rates

## Analyst Consensus

- Rating: **Buy / Strong Buy**
- Average target: **~$1,530** (~+34% from $1,144.02)

## The Thesis in One Line

> The near-monopoly toll booth on all U.S. credit, with software margins, bought after a ~50% drop driven by a possibly overblown regulatory fear.

---

*Research date: Jun 8, 2026 | Next review: Dec 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
FICO.one_liner: Fair Isaac owns the "FICO Score" — the number almost every bank in America checks before deciding whether to lend you money.
FICO.why_short: Every time a bank checks someone's credit, Fair Isaac collects a small fee — millions of times a day. The stock fell hard in 2026 on fear of a new competitor, which put it on sale for the first time in years.
FICO.risk_short: The big risk is that the government lets a competitor into the mortgage market and chips away at its dominance. It also pays no dividend while you wait.

FLKR.summary_short: FLKR is NOT a company: it's an ETF — a single basket holding ~163 of South Korea's largest companies all at once. Buying one share = owning a small slice of all of them at the same time. It's dominated by the two giants that make the world's memory chips: SK hynix (~31%) and Samsung (~21%), which together are ~half the fund. In plain terms: it's a cheap way (it charges just 0.09% a year) to bet on 'Korea's economy, especially its chip factories,' and it pays you a dividend (~2% a year, twice a year) that comes out of those companies' profits. That's why it joins the portfolio: even though it's a basket, it also pays dividends. It's issued by Franklin Templeton and tracks the FTSE South Korea RIC Capped index.
FLKR.summary_what:
Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF (NYSE Arca: FLKR) is an **exchange-traded fund (ETF)**, not an individual company. An ETF is a basket of many stocks packaged into a single instrument that you buy and sell as if it were one stock. It's issued by **Franklin Templeton**.

**What's inside**: it tracks the **FTSE South Korea RIC Capped index**, which gathers **South Korea's** large and mid-sized companies (~163 of them). By buying one share, you own a fraction of all of them at once.

**The key concentration**: the fund is VERY heavy in technology (~64% of the fund). Its two largest positions are Korea's memory-chip giants:
- **SK hynix** (~30.7%)
- **Samsung Electronics** (~20.8% + preferred shares)

Together = ~half the fund. They're followed by names like SK Square, Hyundai Motor, KB Financial, Hyundai Mobis and Shinhan Financial.

**Costs and income**: it charges a fee (expense ratio) of just **0.09% a year** — dirt cheap, ~$9 for every $10,000. And it pays a **~2% annual dividend (distributed semiannually)**, which comes from the dividends paid out by the Korean companies it holds. Assets (AUM): ~$1.0 billion.

**Why it joins the portfolio**: even though it's a basket and not a single company, **it also pays dividends** — it fits the thesis of 'owning things that pay you' — and provides diversified exposure to a key economy for a minimal fee.
FLKR.summary_why: FLKR at $60.10 is the cheapest, most diversified way to get exposure to South Korea — one of the world's most important chip and hardware economies — while collecting a ~2% dividend as you wait. Specific reasons: (1) **Memory-chip exposure in a single purchase**: SK hynix and Samsung, ~half the fund, sit at the heart of the global AI boom (AI data centers and servers need mountains of memory, which these two manufacture). Buying FLKR is a bet on that theme without picking a single name. (2) **A minimal fee (0.09%)**: ~$9 a year per $10,000 — among the cheapest in the market; nearly all the return stays with you. (3) **The 'Korea discount'**: Korean stocks have historically traded cheap versus global peers (over corporate-governance issues). Reforms underway ('value-up') aim to close that discount — that's the upside catalyst. (4) **It pays you dividends (~2%)**: unlike betting on a single growth company, this basket distributes the dividends its ~163 companies generate, twice a year. (5) **Diversification**: even though it's concentrated in tech, you still own ~163 companies (autos, banks, industry) instead of depending on just one. It's country exposure — cheap, and with income.
FLKR.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Extreme concentration — the #1**: SK hynix (~31%) and Samsung (~21%) are MORE than half the fund, and technology is ~64% of the total. If memory-chip prices fall or those two names stumble, the WHOLE ETF falls with them. It isn't really 'buying diversified Korea': it's mostly a bet on two chip giants. (2) **Geopolitical risk**: tensions with North Korea, regional politics and tech trade wars (Korea sits in the middle of the U.S.-China chip rivalry) can hit hard. (3) **Currency risk (the won)**: the ETF trades in dollars but holds stocks priced in Korean won; if the won weakens against the dollar, your return drops even if the stocks rise. (4) **Memory-chip cyclicality**: the memory business (DRAM/NAND) is famous for its brutal boom-and-bust price cycles — when there's oversupply, margins collapse. (5) **The 'Korea discount' may not close**: corporate-governance reforms have been promised for years; if they don't materialize, the discount persists. (6) **No active management**: an ETF follows its index blindly — if the index is poorly balanced (as here, toward tech), you carry that imbalance.

FMX.summary_short: FEMSA is the owner of OXXO — the convenience stores on every street corner in Mexico (24,455 stores, opening 2–3 new ones EVERY DAY) — and much more. Businesses: (1) OXXO Mexico + expansion into Brazil (full ownership Feb 2026) and Texas (bought 249 stores from Delek for $385M → OXXO Texas); (2) Coca-Cola FEMSA (KOF) — the largest Coca-Cola bottler IN THE WORLD, FEMSA holds 47.2% of equity / 56% of the vote; (3) Health — pharmacy chains; (4) Europe — Valora convenience stores (Switzerland/Germany, 97.77%). It is the largest private employer in Latin America: 392,000+ employees in 18 countries. FULLY EXITED Heineken in 2023–24 to refocus on retail + beverages. Headquartered in Monterrey. CEO José Antonio Fernández Garza-Lagüera (effective Nov 1, 2025); Executive Chairman José Antonio Fernández Carbajal. Trades on the NYSE as ADR FMX (1 ADR = 10 UBD units). FMX.summary_what:
Fomento Económico Mexicano, S.A.B. de C.V. (NYSE ADR: FMX, BMV: FEMSAUBD) is a Mexican convenience retail + beverage bottling conglomerate. Headquartered in Monterrey, Mexico. 392,000+ employees in 18 countries — the largest private employer in Latin America.

**Businesses**:

**(1) Proximity / OXXO Mexico** — the crown jewel. **24,455 convenience stores** (+888 net year over year, pace of ~2–3 per day). Drives massive foot traffic: food, drinks, bill payments, phone top-ups, and increasingly financial services (Spin, its fintech).

**(2) Americas & Mobility** — international proximity expansion: **OXXO Brazil** (full takeover Feb 2026), **OXXO Texas** (bought 249 stores from Delek US for **$385M** to enter the US), plus fuel/mobility stations.

**(3) Coca-Cola FEMSA (KOF)** — FEMSA owns **47.2% of equity / 56% of the vote** in the **largest Coca-Cola bottler in the world**, operating across much of Latin America.

**(4) Health** — pharmacy chains in Mexico, Chile, Colombia, Ecuador.

**(5) Europe — Valora** (97.77%) — convenience and food-on-the-go stores in Switzerland, Germany, Austria, Luxembourg.

**Key structural change**: FEMSA **sold its entire Heineken stake in 2023–24** (was ~15%) and returned capital to shareholders + refocused on its core retail + beverages. No longer a sprawling holding company — now a concentrated bet on defensive Latin American consumer spending.

**ADR structure**: 1 ADR FMX = **10 UBD units**. Trades in USD but the business earns mostly in Mexican pesos (and other LATAM currencies). Leadership: **CEO José Antonio Fernández Garza-Lagüera** (effective Nov 1, 2025); **Executive Chairman José Antonio Fernández Carbajal** (his father). The founding Monterrey families maintain long-term control.
FMX.summary_why: FMX at $118.71 means buying defensive Latin American consumer spending — the convenience store on every corner in Mexico (OXXO) + the world's largest Coca-Cola bottler — while collecting a ~6% dividend. Specific reasons: (1) **OXXO is an unstoppable traffic machine**: 24,455 stores, opening 2–3 per day, with same-store sales +6.0% in Q1 2026. People go to OXXO regardless of whether the economy is up or down — it's routine spending, not discretionary. (2) **Coca-Cola FEMSA is globally defensive**: the world's #1 Coca-Cola bottler, ~4.2 billion cases/year through 2.2M+ points of sale, reaching 276M consumers. Recurring volume, brand pricing power. (3) **Refocus after exiting Heineken**: FEMSA stopped being a sprawling holding company; it sold Heineken 2023–24 and concentrated capital in retail + beverages, its core competence. (4) **Two new growth options**: OXXO Brazil (full control Feb 2026) and OXXO Texas (249 stores from Delek) — taking Mexico's proven model to large markets. (5) **Generous ~6% dividend**: ordinary Ps.47.52 + extraordinary Ps.80.60 = Ps.128.12/ADS in 2026, paid in 4 installments (Apr, Jul, Oct 2026, Jan 2027) — one of the highest yields in the portfolio. (6) **Q1 2026 solid**: revenue Ps.207,784M (~$11.8B) +6.1%, adjusted operating profit +11.2%. (7) **Embedded fintech (Spin)** monetizes OXXO's giant foot traffic base with financial services. (8) Analysts: Buy, average target $124.61 (~+5%) + ~6% yield = ~11% expected total return.
FMX.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Mexican peso / exchange rate — #1**: the business earns in pesos (and other LATAM currencies) but the FMX ADR trades in dollars. If the peso weakens, USD returns fall even if the company sells and earns the same in local currency. It is the dominant risk of any Latin American ADR. (2) **Mexican politics**: the Sheinbaum government, regulatory changes, or trade tensions/tariffs with the US (USMCA review) can hit sentiment and valuation. (3) **OXXO execution in the US**: entering Texas with OXXO is new and doesn't guarantee replicating Mexico's success — different market, different competition (7-Eleven, Buc-ee's), different habits. (4) **Spin (fintech) regulation**: the digital payments business is under CNBV supervision (Mexico's financial regulator); rule changes could slow its growth. (5) **Input costs at Coca-Cola FEMSA**: sugar, PET plastic, aluminum and energy — if they rise, the bottling margin compresses. (6) **Dividend withholding**: Mexico withholds ~10% tax on dividends before they reach the ADR holder. (7) **LATAM concentration**: despite Europe (Valora) and the entry into the US, most of the value is still tied to Mexico and Latin America — exposure to regional macro risk.

FOX.summary_short: Fox Corporation is one of the big US television companies, focused on what people still watch LIVE: news and sports. Three businesses: (1) Fox News — the #1 cable news channel in the US for 20+ years, the crown jewel for profit margins; (2) Fox Sports — NFL, MLB, college football and the English-language rights for the 2026 World Cup; (3) Tubi — its FREE ad-supported streaming platform (100M+ monthly users, already accounting for more than 2% of all TV viewing time in the US). Unlike Disney/Warner, Fox did NOT take on heavy streaming debt: it spun off the studios and kept news + sports (the two genres you can't watch 'later'). FY2025 (June): revenue $16.30B, net income $2.29B, diluted EPS $4.91. Trades at ~12x earnings. Controlled by the Murdoch family — Lachlan Murdoch is CEO + Executive Chairman (the family trust dispute was resolved in his favor in Sep 2025). $12B buyback authorization. FOX.summary_what:
Fox Corporation (NASDAQ: FOX class A no-vote / FOXA class B with vote) is the media company that remained after Disney bought the studios and entertainment assets of 21st Century Fox in 2019. Fox kept the news + live sports assets. Headquartered in New York, ~10,400 employees, operates only in the US.

**3 businesses**:

**(1) Cable Network Programming** — the margin engine. *Fox News* (the #1 US cable news channel for 20+ years by a wide margin), Fox Business, FS1, FS2. Revenue from *affiliate fees* (what cable companies pay to carry the channel) + advertising.

**(2) Television** — the Fox broadcast network (NFL, MLB, college football, Animation Domination) + owned local stations. Home to the premium sports rights.

**(3) Tubi (streaming)** — a FAST (free ad-supported streaming TV) platform: the viewer does NOT pay, they watch ads. 100M+ monthly active users, 2%+ of all TV viewing time consumed in the US. Growing strongly; profitability still being built.

**Ownership structure**: the Murdoch family controls ~39% of the vote via the trust (class B). **Lachlan Murdoch** is CEO and Executive Chairman. The legal dispute between siblings over trust control was resolved in September 2025 in Lachlan's favor, removing governance uncertainty.

**Capital allocation**: modest semi-annual dividend ($0.28 × 2 = $0.56/year, yield ~0.97%) + aggressive buybacks ($12B authorized) — Fox prefers to buy back shares rather than pay a large dividend.

**New**: Fox One, a paid DTC streaming service launched August 21, 2025 at $19.99/month, to bring Fox content to cord-cutters.
FOX.summary_why: FOX at $57.44 means buying the two TV franchises best positioned to survive cord-cutting — news and live sports — at ~12x earnings, with a free streaming asset (Tubi) that the market hasn't priced in yet and the 2026 World Cup as an immediate catalyst. Specific reasons: (1) **Fox News is a near-monopoly for profit margins**: the #1 US cable news channel for 20+ years; affiliate fees are contractual and rise with inflation, providing a very predictable cash floor. (2) **News + sports = content 'you can't watch later'**: unlike scripted shows and movies (where Netflix/Disney burn cash competing), news and sports only have value LIVE, which protects Fox's pricing power. (3) **Tubi is hidden upside**: 100M+ monthly users and 2%+ of all US TV viewing time, charging $0 to the viewer (monetizes through ads). The market prices Fox as a 'declining cable company' and ignores that it already has one of the largest free streaming platforms in the country. (4) **2026 World Cup catalyst (Jun 11 – Jul 19)**: Fox has the EXCLUSIVE English-language rights to all 104 matches in the US (including the final), split between FOX and FS1 — peak audience and advertising right this summer. (5) **Clean balance sheet + buybacks**: none of the subscription streaming debt strangling rivals; $12B buyback authorization reduces the share count and lifts earnings per share. (6) **Valuation**: ~12x earnings (EPS $4.91) vs. the market at ~20x — discounted due to cord-cutting fears. (7) **Governance resolved**: the Murdoch trust dispute settled in Sep 2025. (8) **Q3 FY2026 (reported May 11, 2026)**: revenue $3.99B, adjusted EPS $1.32, stock +8% on the day — the business is executing. Analysts: 'Moderate Buy', average target ~$70–72 (~+25%).
FOX.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Cord-cutting — #1 risk**: most of Fox's profit comes from cable affiliate fees (Fox News, FS1), and every year there are fewer cable households. It is a structural headwind; the question is whether Tubi + Fox One grow fast enough to offset cable shrinkage. (2) **Sports rights cost inflation**: renewing NFL, MLB and events like the World Cup costs billions and rises every cycle. If Fox overpays to keep sports rights, margins compress. (3) **Tubi not yet clearly profitable**: growing in users but ad monetization hasn't translated into clear profitability yet; it is an investment today, a bet on tomorrow. (4) **Murdoch family control**: one family controls the vote via the trust. Strategic decisions and succession depend on the family, not the public shareholders. (5) **US concentration + news/sports only**: Fox doesn't have the geographic or genre diversification of Disney/Comcast; a bad advertising cycle or a blow to news viewership hits directly. (6) **Advertising platform risk**: both broadcast TV and Tubi depend on ad spending, which is cyclical and contracts in a recession. (7) **Fox One without proven traction**: the new paid DTC service ($19.99/month) competes in a saturated market; subscriber numbers are not yet demonstrated.

# Fox Corporation (FOX / FOXA) — Full Research

## Price: $57.44 | Price vs. profit (TTM): ~11.7x | Div Yield: ~0.97% | Market Cap: ~$26B USD

---

## What is Fox?

The media company left over after selling the entertainment assets of 21st Century Fox to Disney in 2019. Fox kept **news + live sports** — the two genres that only matter in the moment. Headquartered in New York, ~10,400 employees, 100% US.

## 3 Segments

| Segment | What it includes | Revenue driver |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| **Cable Network Programming** | Fox News (#1 cable news 20+ years), Fox Business, FS1/FS2 | Affiliate fees + ads |
| **Television** | Fox broadcast network (NFL, MLB, college football), owned local stations | Sports rights + ads |
| **Tubi (streaming)** | Free ad-supported FAST, 100M+ MAU, 2%+ of US TV time | Advertising |

## FY2025 (12 months to June 30, 2025)

| Metric | FY2025 |
|---------|------|
| Revenue | $16.30B |
| Net income | $2.29B |
| Diluted EPS | $4.91 |
| Price vs. profit (TTM) | ~11.7x |

## Q3 FY2026 (reported May 11, 2026)

| Metric | Q3 FY2026 |
|---------|---------|
| Revenue | $3.99B |
| Adjusted EPS | $1.32 |
| Stock reaction | +8% |

## Catalyst: FIFA World Cup 2026 (Jun 11 – Jul 19)

- Fox has the **EXCLUSIVE ENGLISH-LANGUAGE** rights to all **104 matches** in the US, including the **final**.
- Split: FOX (70 matches) + FS1 (34 matches).
- The **Spanish-language** rights belong to Telemundo/Peacock (NOT Fox).
- Peak audience + advertising revenue this summer.

## Tubi — The Hidden Asset

- **100M+ monthly active users**.
- **2%+ of all TV viewing time in the US**.
- FAST model: the viewer does NOT pay; monetizes with ads.
- Growing fast; profitability still being built.

## Capital Allocation

- Semi-annual dividend $0.28 × 2 = **$0.56/year** (yield ~0.97%) — intentionally small.
- **$12B buyback authorization** — Fox prefers buying back shares.
- Clean balance sheet: none of the subscription-streaming debt strangling rivals.

## Governance

- Murdoch family controls ~39% of the vote via trust (FOXA class B).
- **Lachlan Murdoch**: CEO + Executive Chairman.
- Legal sibling dispute over trust control **resolved Sep 2025 in Lachlan's favor** → governance uncertainty removed.

## Fox One (New)

- Paid DTC streaming service launched **Aug 21, 2025** at **$19.99/month**.
- To capture cord-cutters.
- No subscriber numbers demonstrated yet.

## Anchor Fact

**Fox owns Tubi, a platform where you watch movies and shows for FREE — no subscription, just ads. More than 100 million people already use it every month, making up more than 2% of ALL the TV viewing time in the US. While Netflix and Disney fight over who can charge more, Fox built something enormous by charging users $0 — and the market still prices Fox as a 'declining cable company'.**

## Top 5 Risks

1. **Cord-cutting** — most profit comes from cable, which shrinks every year
2. **Sports rights cost inflation** — NFL/MLB/World Cup get more expensive every cycle
3. **Tubi not yet clearly profitable** — investment today, bet on tomorrow
4. **Murdoch family control** — one family controls the vote
5. **US + news/sports concentration** — no diversification like Disney

## Analyst Consensus

- Rating: **Moderate Buy**
- Average target: **~$70–72** (~+25% from $57.44)

## The Thesis in One Line

> The two TV franchises best positioned to survive cord-cutting — news and live sports — at ~12x earnings, with Tubi (free streaming, 100M+ users) as hidden upside and the 2026 World Cup as an immediate catalyst.
