*Research date: Jun 1, 2026 | Next review: Dec 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
FOX.one_liner: Fox Corporation owns Fox News, Fox Sports, and Tubi — a free streaming service used by over 100 million people — focusing on the TV content people still watch live.
FOX.why_short: News and live sports are the two types of TV that can't be watched later, which protects Fox from streaming competition. It also owns Tubi, a free platform with 100M+ users that the market barely prices in, and has the exclusive US English rights for the 2026 World Cup.
FOX.risk_short: Most of Fox's income comes from cable TV fees, and fewer households subscribe to cable every year. The key question is whether its free streaming platform Tubi and its new paid service Fox One can grow fast enough to make up the difference.

FTNT.summary_short: Fortinet builds the digital 'armored door' for businesses: it sells firewalls — boxes and software that sit between a company's network and the internet, inspecting everything coming in and out to block hackers and viruses. Nearly every bank, hospital, factory and government needs this and pays again and again for the protection and the updates. Its edge: everything runs on its own operating system (FortiOS) and a homegrown security chip, making it faster and cheaper than rivals — record margins. Right now it's riding a 'refresh' cycle: millions of boxes sold years ago are reaching end-of-life and customers have to replace them. Pays NO dividend. CEO Ken Xie (founder). CAUTION: at $146 it trades EXPENSIVE (~57x earnings) and analysts' average price target is ~$110 — BELOW the purchase price. It's an extremely high-quality company bought at a demanding price.
FTNT.summary_what:
Fortinet, Inc. (NASDAQ: FTNT) is one of the largest **cybersecurity** companies in the world. Its core product is the **firewall**: hardware and software placed between a company's network and the open internet, inspecting all traffic to block hackers, malware and intruders. HQ Sunnyvale, California.

**The technical edge**: unlike many rivals, Fortinet's entire ecosystem runs on **its own operating system (FortiOS)** and a **security chip designed in-house (the 'SPUs')**. That hardware+software integration makes it faster and cheaper to operate, which translates into **margins among the industry's best** (a record 35.8% operating margin in Q1 2026).

**How it makes money**: it sells the equipment (firewalls) once, then charges **recurring subscriptions** for security, support and updates — the services business is the stable, growing part.

**Where it grows**: (1) a **refresh cycle** — millions of firewalls sold years ago are reaching the end of their useful life and customers must replace them (product sales rose 41% in Q1 2026); (2) **OT** — security for industrial and factory equipment; (3) **SASE** — cloud-delivered security for remote workers.

**Capital allocation**: Fortinet pays **NO dividend**. Leadership: founder and CEO **Ken Xie**. Q1 2026: revenue $1.85 billion (+20%), adjusted EPS $0.82 (+41%), record free cash flow of $1.01 billion.

**The honest warning**: at $146 the stock trades at ~57x earnings and the **analysts' average price target (~$110) sits BELOW** the purchase price — consensus 'Hold'. Wall Street thinks the business is excellent but the stock has already run too far.
FTNT.summary_why: FTNT at $146 means buying one of the most profitable cybersecurity machines in the world in the middle of an equipment-refresh cycle — while openly acknowledging you're paying a demanding price. Specific reasons: (1) **An exceptionally high-quality business**: in-house integration of hardware (the SPU chip) + software (FortiOS) gives it margins the competition (Palo Alto, Cisco) can't match — a record 35.8% operating margin. (2) **The refresh cycle**: millions of firewalls sold 5+ years ago are expiring all at once; customers are FORCED to replace them, which sent product sales up +41% in a quarter and led Fortinet to raise its full-2026 guidance. (3) **A structural necessity**: cybersecurity isn't optional — every company, bank, hospital and government MUST spend on this, whether the economy rises or falls; it's defensive, recurring spending. (4) **Growth in OT and SASE**: two large, expanding markets (industrial security and cloud security) where Fortinet is gaining ground. (5) **Torrential cash flow**: a record $1.01 billion of free cash flow in one quarter. BUT, honestly: (6) **the price is high** — ~57x earnings and an analyst target (~$110) BELOW the current price. The thesis is 'quality at an expensive price': it works if the refresh cycle and growth keep surprising; if not, there's little margin for error.
FTNT.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Expensive valuation — the #1, and it's serious**: at $146 it trades at ~57x earnings and the analysts' AVERAGE price target is ~$110 — meaning Wall Street sees ~24% of DOWNSIDE, not upside — with a 'Hold' consensus. Buying above the analysts' target leaves very little margin for error: if growth disappoints, the stock can fall hard. (2) **NO dividend**: you collect no income while you wait; it's a 100% bet on growth. (3) **The refresh cycle ending**: the firewall 'refresh' is a temporary tailwind; when it ends, product sales growth normalizes and could disappoint a market that's paying for high growth. (4) **Fierce competition**: Palo Alto Networks, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Zscaler and others fight over the same market; a price war or share loss hits the margin. (5) **Hardware dependence**: unlike 100%-cloud rivals, a meaningful part of Fortinet is still physical-box sales — more cyclical than pure software. (6) **Technology concentration**: it's a single bet on cybersecurity; an industry architecture shift (everything to the cloud) could erode its hardware-based edge.

GD.summary_short: General Dynamics is one of the largest U.S. defense contractors, with four businesses: (1) **Marine** — it builds the U.S. Navy's nuclear submarines (the Virginia and Columbia classes) at its Electric Boat shipyard; (2) **Aerospace** — it makes Gulfstream private jets, the planes of the ultra-rich, at ~$40-80 million apiece; (3) **Combat** — Abrams tanks and Stryker vehicles; and (4) **Technologies** — IT services for the government. The moat: there are only TWO shipyards in all of the U.S. capable of building a nuclear submarine, and GD is one of them — the Navy literally cannot buy from anyone else for decades. It's a Dividend Aristocrat (28+ straight years of dividend increases), paying ~1.87%. CEO Phebe Novakovic. HQ Reston, Virginia. Q1 2026: revenue $13.5 billion (+10%), record order backlog of $131 billion (+48%). Analysts: Buy, target ~$392 (+15%).
GD.summary_what:
General Dynamics Corporation (NYSE: GD) is one of the **largest defense and aerospace contractors in the United States**, organized into four segments:

**1. Marine Systems** — through its **Electric Boat** shipyard, it builds the U.S. Navy's **nuclear submarines** (the **Virginia** and **Columbia** classes). It's the company's deepest moat.

**2. Aerospace** — it makes **Gulfstream** private jets, the planes corporations and billionaires buy (~$40-80 million apiece), with a multi-year order book.

**3. Combat Systems** — **Abrams** tanks, **Stryker** vehicles and wheeled armored vehicles for armies.

**4. Technologies (GDIT)** — information-technology services and mission systems for the U.S. government.

**How it makes money**: long-term contracts with governments (mostly the U.S.) and Gulfstream jet sales. Revenue is highly predictable because much of the work is already under contract years in advance.

**Leadership**: chairman and CEO **Phebe Novakovic** (since 2013). HQ in **Reston, Virginia**.

**Q1 2026**: revenue $13.5 billion (+10%), EPS $4.10, and a **total order backlog of $131 billion (+48% in one year)** — with 38 Gulfstream deliveries, a first-quarter record.
GD.summary_why: GD at $340.87 means buying a defense contractor with a nearly impossible-to-replicate moat, years of work already under contract, and a dividend that has risen every year for almost three decades. Specific reasons: (1) **The submarine moat**: only TWO shipyards in all of the U.S. can build a nuclear submarine, and GD (Electric Boat) is one — the Navy has no 'plan B' and certifying another would take decades. (2) **A $131 billion order backlog**: nearly 2.4 times its annual revenue (~$54 billion) — years of guaranteed work, no matter the economy — and it grew +48% in a single year. (3) **Gulfstream**: the luxury private-jet business, with strong demand from the ultra-rich and a multi-year order book. (4) **Dividend Aristocrat**: 28+ consecutive years of dividend increases — it pays ~1.87% and that payment grows every year. (5) **A defense tailwind**: with elevated geopolitical tensions (Ukraine, the Indo-Pacific, the Middle East), U.S. and allied defense budgets are rising. (6) **Reasonable valuation**: ~21x earnings — neither cheap nor expensive — for one of the world's most solid defense franchises. Analysts: Buy, target ~$392 (~+15%).
GD.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Dependence on the U.S. military budget — the #1**: most of its revenue comes from the U.S. government; defense spending cuts, budget fights in Congress or government shutdowns can delay payments and programs. (2) **Submarine execution risk**: building nuclear submarines is about as complex as it gets; delays, cost overruns or a shortage of skilled labor at Electric Boat (a known industry bottleneck) eat the margin. (3) **The Gulfstream cycle**: private jets are a cyclical luxury — in a recession, corporations and the wealthy put off buying $50 million planes. (4) **A concentrated customer**: one dominant customer (the U.S. government) means little bargaining power if political priorities shift. (5) **Fixed-price contracts**: many defense contracts are fixed-price; if inflation or costs rise more than planned, GD absorbs the difference. (6) **The valuation isn't a bargain**: at ~21x earnings it already trades at a fair multiple — the upside depends on delivering, not on buying cheap.

GE.summary_short: GE Aerospace makes the engines that keep airplanes flying. After the old General Electric split into three companies in 2024 (engines, energy and health), this is the pure 'engine company.' Its star engine, the LEAP, is the ONLY engine on the Boeing 737 MAX — built through a 50/50 partnership with France's Safran. But the real business isn't selling the engine once: it's the 'razor and blades' model — they sell the engine almost at cost and then charge for DECADES on spare parts and maintenance for the ~80,000 engines already flying. More than 70% of its commercial revenue comes from that after-sale service. It pays a small dividend (~0.58%) on purpose: it reinvests to grow. CEO Larry Culp. HQ Evendale, Ohio. Q1 2026: revenue $12.4 billion (+25%), orders $23.0 billion (+87%). HEADS UP: it's priced expensively (~40x profit) and analysts see little upside (~+8%, target ~$351).
GE.summary_what:
GE Aerospace (NYSE: GE) is the **jet-engine** business left after the old General Electric was split into three independent companies in 2024: **GE Aerospace** (engines), **GE Vernova** (energy) and **GE HealthCare** (medical equipment). This is the pure engine piece.

**What it does**: it designs, builds and services jet engines for commercial airlines (Boeing, Airbus) and for military use. Its highest-volume engine is the **LEAP**, built through **CFM International — a 50/50 partnership with France's Safran**. The LEAP is the **only engine available on the Boeing 737 MAX** and one of two options on the Airbus A320neo.

**How it makes money (the key)**: the model is 'razor and blades.' It sells the new engine with little or no margin, and then earns high-margin money over **20-30 years** selling **spare parts and maintenance** for that engine. With ~80,000 engines already flying, every plane in the sky with a GE/CFM engine is a service-revenue 'annuity.' **More than 70% of commercial revenue comes from that after-sale**, not from selling new engines.

**Leadership**: Chairman and CEO **Larry Culp** (since the April 2024 spin-off). HQ in **Evendale, Ohio** (Cincinnati area).

**Q1 2026**: revenue $12.4 billion (+25%), adjusted EPS $1.86 (+25%), and total orders of **$23.0 billion (+87%)** — LEAP shop visits rose more than 50%.
GE.summary_why: GE at $324.65 is buying the world's largest jet-engine business, with a decades-long recurring-revenue model and a giant order book — while acknowledging you're paying a high price. Specific reasons: (1) **'Razor and blades' model**: it sells the engine almost at cost and then charges for parts and maintenance for 20-30 years; over 70% of commercial revenue is that high-margin, very predictable after-sale service. (2) **Giant installed base**: ~80,000 engines already flying (2.3 billion flight hours) — each one a service annuity. (3) **The LEAP, almost a monopoly in its niche**: it's the ONLY engine on the Boeing 737 MAX; as Boeing ramps production, GE sells more engines and, above all, more future service. (4) **Order book of ~$211 billion**: more than 4x its annual revenue — years of work already booked. (5) **Air-traffic tailwind**: people fly more and more, and every flight hour wears out engines that have to be serviced. (6) **Momentum**: orders +87% in one quarter, guidance raised. BUT honestly: (7) **the price is demanding** — ~40x profit and an analyst target (~$351) implying only ~+8% upside; a great company at little discount. Analysts: Strong Buy, target ~$351 (~+8%).
GE.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Expensive valuation — #1**: at ~40x profit and with an average analyst target (~$351) implying only ~+8% upside from the buy price, there's little room for error; if growth disappoints, an expensive stock falls hard. (2) **Dependence on the air cycle**: if a recession, a pandemic or a shock (fuel, geopolitics) cuts flying, engine wear drops and with it the service revenue — the heart of the business. (3) **Boeing dependence**: the LEAP is the only engine on the 737 MAX; if Boeing has production or quality problems (as has happened), GE sells fewer new engines. (4) **Supply chain**: building engines is extremely complex; parts shortages or bottlenecks can slow deliveries and repairs. (5) **Small dividend (~0.58%)**: if you want immediate income, this isn't it — the thesis is growth + recurring service. (6) **CFM partnership risk**: the LEAP depends on the 50/50 partnership with Safran; any friction with the French partner would hit its most important engine.

The business is simple: it digs one ounce of gold out at a cost of ~$1,645 and sells it for more than $4,000 — it keeps the difference, and right now that difference is huge. GFI.summary_what:
Gold Fields Limited (NYSE: GFI) is one of the **largest gold miners in the world**, headquartered in **Johannesburg, South Africa**, with mines running in **South Africa, Australia, Ghana, Peru, Chile and Canada**.

**How it makes money**: about as simple as it gets. It pulls gold out of the ground at a **cost (AISC) of ~$1,645 per ounce** and sells it at the market price — today **above $4,000 an ounce**. The difference is its profit. When gold goes up and costs stay flat, every ounce is worth a lot more.

**2025 production**: **2.44 million ounces (+18%)**, with free cash flow rocketing **+391%**.

**The new jewel — Salares Norte (Chile)**: a freshly-started mine that pulls gold out at just **~$450-600 an ounce** — one of the cheapest on the planet — and that on its own generated **$808 million** in cash. It also adds **Windfall (Canada)** through its partner Osisko.

**Leadership**: CEO **Mike Fraser**. HQ **Johannesburg, South Africa**.
GFI.summary_why: GFI at $33.60 is buying gold that also pays you rent, right when gold is at record highs and its new mines are dirt-cheap to run. Specific reasons: (1) **The margin is huge**: it digs at ~$1,645 an ounce and sells above $4,000 — keeping ~$2,400 per ounce, and it produces 2.44 million ounces a year. (2) **Earnings are exploding**: free cash flow rose +391% in 2025; that's why it trades at ~23x what it earned last year but only ~10x what it's expected to earn next year. (3) **Salares Norte, the cheap mine**: it pulls gold out at ~$450-600 an ounce — one of the cheapest in the world — and already generated $808 million; it's almost pure profit as long as gold stays expensive. (4) **Geographic spread**: mines in six countries, so a problem in one doesn't sink it. (5) **It pays you ~3.15% dividend**: unlike gold bars (which you just store), here you collect rent while you wait. (6) **Gold as a safe haven**: with global uncertainty, central banks buying gold and borrowing costs falling, the wind is at its back. Analysts: Buy, target ~$58 (+74%).
GFI.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **The price of gold — #1**: the whole thesis depends on gold staying expensive; if it falls, the margin compresses fast and the stock hurts more than the metal. (2) **Country / mining risk**: it operates in South Africa, Ghana and Peru — places with risk of power cuts, labor disputes, royalty changes or political instability. (3) **Execution risk**: a new mine like Salares Norte can hit operational snags, extreme weather (it's in the Atacama desert, at high altitude) or cost overruns. (4) **Rising costs**: if energy, diesel or labor go up, the ~$1,645 AISC rises and the margin shrinks. (5) **Currency**: a big chunk of its costs are in South African rand, Australian dollars and other currencies — FX swings move its numbers. (6) **It's cyclical and volatile**: gold miners amplify the metal's moves in both directions — not a stock for weak stomachs.

GILD.summary_what:
Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ: GILD) is a **biotech pharma** company and the global leader in treating **HIV** (the virus that causes AIDS), headquartered in **Foster City, California**.

**How it makes money**: it sells prescription medicines, mostly for HIV. Its franchises:

**1. HIV — the heart of the business (~$5 billion per quarter)**: its **Biktarvy** pill is the #1 HIV treatment in the U.S. (over 52% share) and sells ~$3.4 billion a quarter. **Descovy** is for prevention.

**2. HIV prevention — the new catalyst**: **Yeztugo (lenacapavir)**, a long-acting shot given just **twice a year** to prevent infection.

**3. Oncology (cancer)**: **Trodelvy** (breast cancer) and CAR-T cell therapies through its **Kite** unit.

**4. Liver and others**: hepatitis B/C/D and Veklury (the COVID antiviral).

**Leadership**: CEO **Daniel O'Day**. HQ **Foster City, California**.

**Q1 2026**: revenue ~$7 billion (+4%), HIV sales $5 billion (+10%). Yeztugo billed $166 million in the quarter (+72% over the prior one) and Gilead raised its full-year forecast for that drug to ~$1 billion.
GILD.summary_why: GILD at $121.79 is buying the global HIV leader — a huge, stable business — right as it launches a shot that could redefine HIV prevention, while paying ~19x profit with a ~2.69% dividend. Specific reasons: (1) **HIV dominance**: Biktarvy is the #1 pill in the U.S. (>52% share) and generates ~$3.4 billion a quarter — a very predictable river of cash. (2) **Yeztugo, the catalyst**: a twice-a-year shot that prevented HIV with nearly 100% effectiveness in trials; it's already on track for ~$1 billion in its first year and could be a blockbuster for years. (3) **Diversification**: oncology (Trodelvy) and cell therapies (Kite) add growth beyond HIV. (4) **Rising dividend**: it pays ~2.69% and has raised it ~10 years in a row — it pays you rent while you wait. (5) **Reasonable valuation**: ~19x profit (~15x next year's) for a moated franchise with a big launch underway. (6) **The backdrop**: analysts see the stock at ~$159 (~+30%) on average. Analysts: Buy, target ~$159 (~+30%).
GILD.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Biktarvy dependence — #1**: a single drug generates >$13 billion a year; when it loses patent protection, cheap generics will eat its sales. The good news: Gilead reached settlements pushing U.S. generic entry to **2036** — that delays the problem, it doesn't remove it. (2) **HIV competition**: GSK/ViiV (with its Cabenuva/Apretude injectables) and Merck (with islatravir) are going after the same market. (3) **Yeztugo launch execution**: much of the growth thesis depends on the shot selling well — insurance coverage, adoption pace. (4) **Oncology pipeline**: if Trodelvy or Kite's cell therapies disappoint, the diversification weakens. (5) **Drug-pricing pressure**: the IRA law lets Medicare negotiate prices — a regulatory risk to its drugs. (6) **Binary regulatory risk**: it has pending FDA decisions (e.g. Aug 27, 2026) that can swing the stock sharply either way.

---

## What is Gilead?

A **biotech pharma** company and global leader in **HIV**. NASDAQ: GILD. HQ Foster City, California. CEO Daniel O'Day.

## Franchises

|------|-----------|
| **HIV (core)** | **Biktarvy** (#1 in the U.S., >52% share, ~$3.4B/qtr) + Descovy |
| **HIV prevention** | **Yeztugo (lenacapavir)** — twice-a-year shot |

| **Liver / others** | Hepatitis B/C/D, Veklury (COVID) |

## The catalyst: Yeztugo (lenacapavir)

- A long-acting shot, just **twice a year**, to prevent HIV.
- In trials (PURPOSE-1) it prevented HIV with **nearly 100%** effectiveness — essentially zero infections.
- Q1 2026: $166M (+72% over the prior quarter). Full-year guidance raised to **~$1 billion**.

**Gilead launched a shot, Yeztugo (lenacapavir), given just TWICE a year that, in its trials, prevented HIV with nearly 100% effectiveness — essentially zero infections among thousands of participants. Two shots a year instead of a daily pill: it's arguably the most powerful tool ever for stopping HIV transmission, and it's only just starting to sell.**

## Top Risks

GSK.summary_what:
GSK plc is **one of the world's top 10 pharmaceutical companies** — a pure-play biopharma focused on vaccines, HIV/infections, oncology, and respiratory diseases after its Haleon demerger. HQ London (New Oxford Street, since July 2024), ~66,800 employees, sales in >75 countries.

**History**:
- **1842**: Allen & Hanburys founded (UK predecessor)
- **1989**: SmithKline Beckman + Beecham → SmithKline Beecham
- **2000**: Glaxo Wellcome + SmithKline Beecham → **GlaxoSmithKline (GSK)**
- **May 16, 2022**: rebrand to 'GSK plc' (new logo, ticker reaffirmed)
- **July 18, 2022**: **Haleon demerger** (Sensodyne, Panadol, Voltaren, Centrum spin-off) — GSK becomes pure-play biopharma
- **Jan 1, 2026**: **Luke Miels** becomes CEO (replaces Emma Walmsley)

**4 therapeutic areas**:

**(1) Vaccines (~30% revenue)**:
- **Shingrix** (shingles/herpes zoster, FY2025 £3.6B, #1 globally) — >90M doses distributed in the US since 2017
- **Arexvy** (RSV for adults, launched 2023, FY2025 ~£0.8B; Q1 2026 -10% due to narrow CDC eligibility)
- **Bexsero/Menveo** (meningitis)
- **Boostrix/Infanrix** (DTaP infant)

**(2) HIV/Specialty Medicines (~30% revenue)** — via ViiV Healthcare (GSK 78% + Pfizer 11.7% + Shionogi 10%):
- **Dovato/Tivicay** (oral HIV)
- **Cabenuva** (monthly/bi-monthly injectable HIV, FY2025 £1.3B — first long-acting HIV therapy)
- **Apretude** (injectable HIV prevention/PrEP)

**(3) General Medicines (~25% revenue)**:
- **Trelegy Ellipta** (COPD triple therapy, FY2025 £3.0B)
- **Nucala** (severe eosinophilic asthma)
- **Anoro/Incruse Ellipta** (COPD)

**(4) Oncology (~10% revenue, fastest-growing)**:
- **Jemperli** (endometrial cancer, dostarlimab)
- **Blenrep** (multiple myeloma, **returned to US market Oct 2025** after 2022 withdrawal)
- **Ojjaara** (myelofibrosis)

**HQ**: London, New Oxford Street (since July 2024). **Employees**: ~66,800.

**Leadership**:
- **CEO**: **Luke Miels** since January 1, 2026 (replaced Emma Walmsley, CEO 2017-2025)
- **Chair**: Sir Jonathan Symonds (since 2019)

**Q1 2026 (reported April 29, 2026)**:

| Metric | Q1 2026 | YoY |
|--------|---------|-----|
| Sales | **£7.6B** | +5% CER (+2% AER) |
| Core op profit | — | +10% CER |
| **Core EPS** | — | +9% CER |
| Specialty Medicines | — | **+15%** |
| Shingrix | — | +1% |
| **Arexvy** | — | **-10%** (CDC narrow) |
| Trelegy | — | +6% |
| Cabenuva | — | +9% |

**Q1 2026 dividend**: **17p**
**FY2026 expected**: **70p** total (ADR yield ~4.13% in USD)
**FY26 buyback**: **£2B** (announced, completing 2026)

**M&A 2026**:
- **35Pharma $950M** (closed) — pulmonary hypertension asset
- **Siran Biotech up to $1B** — siRNA hepatitis B platform
GSK.summary_why: GSK at $50.33 means buying one of the world's top 10 pharma companies at a forward P/E of 9.1x — the cheapest multiple in global Big Pharma — right as (1) it has 14 blockbuster medicines slated for launch between 2025-2031, (2) it just closed 2 key M&A deals in 2026, (3) Blenrep returned to the US market in October 2025, and (4) new CEO Luke Miels took over on January 1, 2026 with an execution mandate. Specific reasons: (1) **Q1 2026 delivered +5% CER in sales with specialty medicines +15%**: revenue £7.6B, core op profit +10% CER, core EPS +9% CER. Beat quality is excellent — specialty (injectable HIV Cabenuva +9%, Jemperli oncology) offsetting soft vaccines (Arexvy -10% due to CDC narrow eligibility). Reaffirmed FY26 guidance + launched £2B buyback completing in 2026. (2) **Shingrix anchor — >90M doses distributed in the US since 2017**: the #1 global shingles (herpes zoster) vaccine. CDC recommends for adults 50+. Estimate: only ~45% of eligible adults vaccinated → ~70 million adults 50+ still UNVACCINATED in the US = an 8-12 year growth runway. FY2025 Shingrix revenue £3.6B (~$4.5B). This is the thesis anchor: a giant recurring-revenue product with visible runway. (3) **Forward P/E 9.1x = deep discount vs. Big Pharma**: peers Pfizer 9.8x, Roche 13x, Sanofi 11x, Novartis 14x, Merck 11x, AZN 16x. GSK trades at a ~25-40% discount to peers despite having: a 4.13% dividend, £2B buyback, 14-blockbuster pipeline, and 2 M&A deals closed in 2026. Re-rating to 11x forward P/E = $61 = +21% capital gain + 4% yield = ~25% total return. (4) **Luke Miels new CEO since January 1, 2026**: comes from Macquarie + prior roles at AstraZeneca/Roche/GSK Brand. His mandate: execute the multi-year plan to grow from £31B revenue (2025) to £40B in 2031 (+29% in 6 years). Activist investor (Bluebell) already pushing for capital returns + execution focus — Miels is the operator. (5) **2 M&A deals in 2026**: 35Pharma $950M (closed, pulmonary hypertension asset) + Siran Biotech up to $1B (siRNA hepatitis B platform). Both are smart tuck-ins in growing therapeutic areas — siRNA is the technology behind Alnylam's and Ionis's success. (6) **Blenrep returned to the US market in October 2025**: GSK withdrew Blenrep (multiple myeloma) in 2022 after a trial failure. It reformulated it + ran new DREAMM-7 + DREAMM-8 trials (data 2025) + received FDA re-approval in October 2025. A potential $2-3B peak blockbuster in five years — pure upside vs. conservative consensus. (7) **Growing dividend: 17p in Q1 2026, FY2026 expected 70p, yield ~4.13% USD**: pays a consistent quarterly dividend. Plus £2B buyback completing in 2026 = ~$2.5B = ~3% of market cap retired. Total shareholder return (yield + buyback) = ~7% before any capital gain. (8) **Cabenuva injectable HIV is revolutionary**: the first time in history HIV patients do NOT need a daily pill — an injection every 1 or 2 months. Q1 2026 +9%. Initial adoption concentrated in the US/Europe, expansion into Asia/LatAm underway. Cabenuva FY2025 £1.3B with a $3-5B peak runway. (9) **Zantac (ranitidine) litigation settled — overhang removed**: GSK paid ~$2.2B in settlements in 2024-2025 to resolve the mass Zantac-vs.-cancer litigation. Already absorbed on the balance sheet, no further surprises. (10) **Solid anchor: ~70M adults 50+ unvaccinated for Shingrix in the US** + 14 blockbusters pipeline 2025-2031 + £40B revenue target = 6-year growth visibility at the cheapest multiple in Big Pharma.
GSK.summary_risk: The risks are measurable: (1) **Arexvy -10% Q1 2026 — CDC narrowed RSV eligibility**: in 2024 the CDC limited the RSV vaccine to adults 75+ (previously 60+) plus 60-74 with high risk. GSK requested an expansion to 50+ at the next ACIP meeting. If ACIP rejects the expansion or COVID-like vaccine fatigue continues, Arexvy plateaus at ~£1B vs. its initial target of £2.5B+. (2) **Trelegy patent cliff 2027-2028**: the #1 COPD blockbuster (£3.0B FY2025) loses US exclusivity in 2027-2029. Generics arrive → revenue cliff of -50-70%. GSK needs Nucala/Trelegy follow-on inhaler ramp-up + new launches to offset. If the new products don't scale quickly, the gap will be visible in 2028-2030. (3) **Shingrix competition from Pfizer/Moderna mRNA shingles**: both pharma companies are developing mRNA versions. If Phase 3 data is positive in 2026-2027, Shingrix loses share in new patients. Plus saturation: ~45% of eligible US adults already vaccinated → growth moderates. (4) **Luke Miels first-year execution risk**: new CEO since January 1, 2026. Q1 2026 was solid, but the next 3 quarters will determine if he can execute the £40B 2031 plan. If Q2 2026 disappoints or guidance is reset, the market will punish. (5) **Bluebell activist pressure**: activist investor pushing for more aggressive capital returns + execution focus. If GSK fails to deliver visible catalysts in H2 2026, board conflicts are possible. Also a management distraction. (6) **35Pharma + Siran integration risk**: both deals closing in 2026. Integration risk + R&D execution risk. If Siran's siRNA hepatitis B doesn't deliver positive data in 2027-2028, up to $1B contingent payment is gone. (7) **FX headwind — GSK reports in GBP, sells in USD**: if the dollar weakens vs. GBP, AER revenue growth erodes (Q1 2026 already: +5% CER but +2% AER on FX). If GBP strengthens above $1.40, growth optics are damaged. (8) **Residual litigation — Zantac spillover**: even though the mass litigation is settled, new high-value individual claims may emerge in Europe/Canada. GSK already spent $2.2B; any new wave is an overhang. (9) **Blenrep re-launch commercial execution**: launched October 2025 post-FDA approval. Must recapture oncologist mindshare in the US after 3 years off-market. Competing with Darzalex Faspro (J&J), Carvykti (Legend/J&J), Tecvayli (J&J). If payer pushback or slow adoption, the $2-3B peak revenue target slips from 5 to 7-8 years. (10) **UK/EU regulatory backdrop — drug pricing pressure**: UK NHS + EU joint procurement pushing lower prices. GSK derives ~40% of revenue ex-US, direct exposure. Plus, the Trump administration's push for "Most Favored Nation" pricing in Medicare could reduce the US-vs.-Europe pricing spread.

HCA.summary_what:
HCA Healthcare is **the largest private hospital system in the United States**, founded in **1968 in Nashville, Tennessee** by Dr. Thomas Frist Sr. (father of former Senator Bill Frist) and Jack Massey. Today it operates **188 hospitals + more than 2,400 outpatient sites** (surgery centers, freestanding ERs, urgent care, physician clinics, imaging) in **19–20 U.S. states + the United Kingdom**. ~280,000 employees. **CEO: Sam Hazen** (Chairman + CEO since January 1, 2019, 41+ years at HCA). **CFO: Mike Marks** since May 2024 (~30 years at HCA).

**Geographic footprint** (heavy sunbelt concentration): **Texas (73 hospitals)** + **Florida (49)** = >50% of total. Other core markets: Tennessee (HQ), Virginia (HCA Virginia), Colorado (HCA HealthONE Denver), Utah/Idaho (MountainStar Healthcare), Kansas/Missouri, Las Vegas, Nashville (TriStar). UK operates 6 hospitals in London under HCA UK.

**Flagship brands/systems**: **HCA Florida** (largest network in the state), **HCA Houston**, **HCA Virginia**, **HCA HealthONE** (Colorado), **MountainStar** (Utah/Idaho), **TriStar Health** (Tennessee), and **Sarah Cannon Research Institute** — the oncology research arm with >4,500 clinical trial enrollments/year and >750 first-in-human studies/year, contributing to the majority of new oncology therapies approved by the FDA in the past decade (UK + US presence).

**2025 revenue structure ($75.6B total, +6.4% YoY)**: acute-care hospitals ~75–80% of revenue, outpatient ~15–20%, other ~5%. ~50% of revenue comes from combined Medicare/Medicaid, ~40% from commercial/managed care, ~10% other (uninsured, self-pay). The outpatient segment (surgery centers, freestanding ERs, urgent care) is where HCA is growing fastest — higher margin, less regulated.

**Aggressive historical capital return**: HCA is one of the most prolific buyback programs in the S&P 500 — since 2017 it has retired >$50B in shares (more than 50% of current market cap). Combines a growing dividend (raised 8.3% in January 2026) with a $10B buyback program authorized.
HCA.summary_why: HCA at $429.01 means buying the **#1 U.S. hospital operator** at a forward P/E of **12.94x** — a historical discount vs. its own 5-year average (~14.5x), right when the market is over-pricing the headwind from ACA subsidy expiration and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Specific reasons: (1) **Q1 2026 was cleaner than the pricing suggests — reported April 24, 2026**: revenue $19.109B (+4.3% YoY) in line, EPS GAAP $7.15 (+10.9% YoY) in line with consensus, same-facility revenue per equivalent admission +3.1%. Weak volume (respiratory admissions -42% YoY due to a very weak flu season) is one-off, not structural. (2) **FY2026 guidance reaffirmed**: revenue $76.5–$80.0B, Adj EBITDA $15.55–$16.45B, **diluted EPS $29.10–$31.50** = midpoint $30.30, implied fwd P/E ~14.2x on midpoint. If HCA hits the high end, fwd P/E falls to 13.6x. (3) **Massive capital return quantified**: new **$10B buyback** authorization announced January 27, 2026 (=10.5% of current market cap). Q1 2026: $1.571B in repurchases (3.157M shares), $9.179B remaining. Dividend raised +8.3% to $0.78/quarter ($3.12/year forward). Share retirement run-rate: ~5–7% per year = a compounding mechanism outside of operating growth. (4) **ACA subsidy headwind already priced in**: $150M Q1 + guided $600M–$900M FY2026 = midpoint $750M ÷ revenue $78B = ~1% impact. Real pain but contained. Uninsured admissions +16% YoY = HCA captures volume that previously paid through ACA. If Congress renews subsidies in Q3–Q4 2026 debate, there is reversal upside not priced in. (5) **OBBBA Medicaid phase-down not until January 1, 2028**: state-directed payments capped, but 10pp/year phase-down = 5 years of runway. HCA has time to reprice and shift mix. Tennessee (HCA's home state) had increased state-directed payments by +$3.2B before the law = high base going into phase-down. (6) **Sarah Cannon Research as a hidden crown jewel**: >4,500 oncology trial enrollments/year, >750 first-in-human studies/year, contributor to the majority of FDA oncology approvals in the past decade. Potentially worth $5–10B standalone in a spin-out — not priced in HCA. (7) **Sunbelt concentration = structural demographics**: TX+FL are the fastest-growing U.S. states (TX +1.5%/year, FL +1.2%/year), with an influx of retirees (medical consumption per capita 2–3x average). HCA holds the #1 hospital share in Houston, Dallas-Fort Worth, Austin, Miami, Tampa and Orlando. Demand is structurally rising. (8) **212,000 babies/year = ~5.9% of ALL U.S. births** delivered in HCA hospitals. Irreplaceable OB volume. (9) **Analyst consensus 19–27 analysts**: ~37% Strong Buy + ~37% Buy + ~21% Hold; mean target ~$505 = **+17.7% upside**. Post Q1, several cut targets but maintained Buy.
HCA.summary_risk: The risks are specifically measurable: (1) **ACA enhanced subsidy expiration confirmed January 1, 2026** — premiums more than doubled on average. **Q1 2026 already cost $150M; HCA guided $600M–$900M FY2026** ($750M midpoint). ACA admissions -15% YoY. If uninsured admissions don't compensate (Q1: +16% YoY) and bad-debt expense surges, the hit could slide toward the high end. Without legislative renewal in 2026, FY2027 run-rate could be similar or worse. (2) **One Big Beautiful Bill Act (signed July 4, 2025)**: CBO estimates >$1T in federal healthcare cuts + 10M more uninsured nationally. Caps state-directed Medicaid payments at **100% Medicare in expansion states / 110% in non-expansion**; existing arrangements phase down **10 percentage points/year from January 1, 2028**. Tennessee had increased state-directed payments by +$3.2B before the law = high base going into phase-down. (3) **Operating margin visibly compressing**: 12.0% Q1 2026 vs. 12.7% Q1 2025 = -70bps. If labor inflation (nursing wages, agency staff) accelerates + payer mix shifts toward expanded Medicaid (lower reimbursement), margins may keep compressing 50–100bps additionally in 2026–2027. (4) **Respiratory volume -42% YoY in Q1 2026**: hurts comparables — very weak flu season (not structural but weighs on the quarter). If 2026–27 season is also not strong, Q4 2026 comparables suffer. (5) **Geographic concentration TX+FL >50%**: hurricanes (Helene + Milton 2024 cost ~$250M total: $50M Q3 + ~$200M Q4 2024). 2025 season without a major new hit. 2026 season remains a recurring risk (June–November). A major hurricane in Houston or Tampa can erase $200–400M of revenue + repair capex. (6) **Premium vs. peers**: forward P/E 12.94 vs. Tenet (THC) and Universal Health (UHS) trading lower — HCA trades at a modest premium justified by scale/margins but narrative compression can equalize multiples. (7) **Recurring legislative risk**: every change of administration can bring new Medicare reimbursement cuts, surprise billing legislation (No Surprises Act), 340B drug pricing reform, and site-neutral payment reforms (HCA operates many hospital outpatient departments that receive higher payments than independent clinics — site-neutral would eliminate that premium). (8) **Labor cost trends**: nurse-to-patient staffing ratios (California already mandated, Massachusetts and NY proposing) increase mandatory headcount. Agency nursing rates still 30–40% above pre-pandemic. Healthcare professional salary inflation runs 4–5% annually vs. CPI ~2.5%. (9) **Tort/litigation**: no material new cases identified in the past 6 months, but healthcare is a structurally litigious industry — there are always class actions, billing fraud allegations (False Claims Act), antitrust scrutiny in markets where HCA holds >50% local share.

# HCA Healthcare (HCA) — Full Research

## Price: $429.01 | P/E TTM: 14.78 | Forward P/E: 12.94 | Div Yield: 0.72% | Market Cap: $95.17B

---

## What is HCA Healthcare?

HCA Healthcare is **the largest private hospital system in the United States**, founded in **1968 in Nashville, Tennessee** by Dr. Thomas Frist Sr. (father of former Senator Bill Frist) and Jack Massey. Originally Hospital Corporation of America. Today it operates **188 hospitals + more than 2,400 outpatient sites** (surgery centers, freestanding ERs, urgent care, physician clinics, imaging) in 19–20 U.S. states + the United Kingdom.

CEO: **Sam Hazen** (January 1, 2019, 41+ years at HCA). CFO: **Mike Marks** (May 2024).

## Geographic Footprint

| State | Hospitals |
|-------|----------|
| Texas | 73 |
| Florida | 49 |
| Tennessee (HQ) | ~16 |
| Virginia | ~13 |
| Colorado | ~8 |
| 14 other states + UK | ~29 |

**TX + FL = >50% of total** = concentrated exposure to hurricanes and state Medicaid policy (state-directed payments).

## Q1 2026 — Reported April 24, 2026

- **Revenue**: $19.109B (+4.3% YoY), in line with consensus $19.08B
- **Diluted EPS GAAP**: $7.15 (+10.9% YoY from $6.45). Non-GAAP consensus: $7.13
- **Same-facility admissions**: +0.9% YoY
- **Same-facility equivalent admissions**: +1.3% YoY
- **Same-facility revenue per equivalent admission**: +3.1% YoY
- **Operating income**: $2.29B / **margin 12.0%** (vs $2.33B / 12.7% in Q1 2025) — margin contracted
- **Cash from ops**: $2.014B (+22.0% YoY)
- **Key headwind**: respiratory admissions **-42% YoY** (very weak flu season)
- **Buybacks Q1**: 3.157M shares for $1.571B; **$9.179B available** post Q1

## FY2026 Guidance (reaffirmed in Q1)

| Metric | Range |
|--------|-------|
| Revenue | $76.5–$80.0B |
| Adj EBITDA | $15.55–$16.45B |
| Net income | $6.5–$7.0B |
| **Diluted EPS** | **$29.10–$31.50** |

## Capital Return — Recent Increase

- **Dividend raised from $0.72 → $0.78 quarterly** announced January 27, 2026 (with Q4 2025 results). Forward annualized: **$3.12/year** (yield ~0.73% at current price).
- **New $10B buyback program** authorized January 27, 2026.
- Historical buybacks: HCA has retired >$50B in shares since 2017 (>50% of current market cap).

## Macro Catalysts and Risks

### ACA Enhanced Subsidies — Expired January 1, 2026

Congress did not renew them at end of 2025. Individual health premiums more than doubled on average. **Q1 2026 impact on HCA: $150M**. **FY2026 guidance: $600M–$900M total hit** (midpoint $750M ≈ ~1% of revenue). ACA admissions -15% YoY (low end of guided -15/–20% range). Uninsured admissions +16% YoY as partial offset.

### One Big Beautiful Bill Act (signed July 4, 2025)

The CBO estimates >$1T in federal healthcare cuts + 10M more uninsured nationally. Limits state-directed Medicaid payments to **100% Medicare in expansion states / 110% in non-expansion**; existing arrangements phase down **10 percentage points/year from January 1, 2028**. Tennessee had increased state-directed payments by +$3.2B before the law.

### Hurricanes (recurring risk)

Helene + Milton 2024: ~$250M total impact ($50M Q3 + ~$200M Q4 2024). 2025 season with no material reported hit. 2026 season (June–November) remains a risk.

## Brands & Crown Jewels

- **HCA Florida** — largest network in the state
- **HCA Houston / HCA Virginia / HCA HealthONE (Colorado)**
- **MountainStar Healthcare** (Utah/Idaho)
- **TriStar Health** (Tennessee)
- **Sarah Cannon Research Institute** — >4,500 oncology trial enrollments/year, >750 first-in-human studies/year. Contributed to the majority of FDA oncology approvals over the past decade. UK + US presence.

## Anchor Facts

- **HCA delivers ~212,000 babies a year** = **~5.9% of all U.S. births** (CDC: ~3.6M births/year). More than any other health system in the country.
- HCA holds the #1 hospital market share in Houston, Dallas-Fort Worth, Austin, Miami, Tampa and Orlando.

## Analyst Consensus (May 2026)

- **Composite rating**: Buy. MarketBeat (19 analysts): 37% Strong Buy + 37% Buy + 21% Hold + 5% Sell + 0% Strong Sell.
- **Mean target**: ~$505 (TipRanks 19 analysts $510.21; broad consensus 27 $505.04; median 45 $490, range $368–$525).
- **Upside vs. $429.01**: +17.7% to the mean target.
- Post Q1 2026: several analysts cut targets (weak volumes) but maintained Buy rating.

## Key Quantified Risks

1. **ACA subsidy lapse**: $600M–$900M FY2026 ($750M midpoint = ~1% revenue). Without legislative renewal, FY2027 run-rate similar.
2. **OBBBA Medicaid phase-down**: begins January 1, 2028, 10pp/year, over 5 years.
3. **Operating margin compressed**: -70bps Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025.
4. **TX+FL >50% concentration**: hurricanes + state policy.
5. **Labor cost inflation**: nurse-to-patient ratios, agency rates 30–40% above pre-pandemic.

## Thesis in One Line

> Buy the #1 U.S. hospital operator at a forward P/E of 12.94x — a historical discount — with a new $10B buyback (10.5% of market cap), +8.3% dividend, and >$50B shares retired since 2017. ACA + OBBBA headwinds are priced in; sunbelt demographics + Sarah Cannon are not.

---

*Research date: May 4, 2026 | Next review: Nov 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
HCA.one_liner: HCA Healthcare is the largest hospital operator in the U.S. — with 188 hospitals and over 2,400 care sites — navigating changes in government subsidies that could affect its revenue.
HCA.why_short: HCA is attractively priced because the market is overreacting to the reduction in government subsidies. The company remains fundamentally strong and is returning a lot of money to shareholders through buybacks and a growing dividend.
HCA.risk_short: The main risk is that the government's removal of health subsidies pushes more patients to go uninsured, which could hurt HCA's revenue if it can't offset that with enough paying patients.

HEI.summary_short: The **world's largest independent supplier** of FAA-PMA (Parts Manufacturer Approval) parts — generic FAA-approved parts that replace OEM engine and airframe parts at **25–50% of the price**. Founded 1957, controlled by the Mendelson family since 1990 when Larry Mendelson took control and transformed it from $26M in revenue → ~$4.5B (~170x), market cap **>$37B (>1,500x), 22–23% CAGR over 35 years**. **Larry Mendelson passed away on September 27, 2025, at age 87**; succession already executed per plan: **Eric Mendelson** and **Victor Mendelson** are now **Co-Chairmen + Co-CEOs** (Eric heads Flight Support Group, Victor heads Electronic Technologies Group). Q1 FY2026 (reported **February 25, 2026**, fiscal Oct-end): revenue $1.18B (+14.4% YoY) beat consensus $1.15B; **EPS $1.35 (+12.5%)** vs. estimate $1.27 (+6.3% beat); record net income **$190.2M (+13%)**; organic growth **12% in FSG, 6% in ETG**. **FSG margin expanded to 24.5%** (from 23.4%); **ETG compressed to 19.8%** (from 23.1%) due to unfavorable defense/space mix. FY2026 guidance: revenue $5.01B. Bolt-on M&A model: 4 acquisitions closed/announced in 2025–2026 (Millennium Intl, Rosen Aviation, EthosEnergy A&C closed Feb 5, 2026, Sherwood Avionics Apr 6, 2026). PMA library: **>19,500 approved parts + >90M parts delivered with ZERO Service Bulletins, ADs or in-flight shutdowns traceable to HEICO**. Yield 0.09% is misleading — the stock has compounded so much that the absolute dividend is barely visible, but a semi-annual payment sustained for decades. Dominant risk: P/E TTM **53.64x is a real premium** vs. A&D peer ~37x. Analyst consensus Buy, mean target ~$373–376 = **+38% upside**.
HEI.summary_what:
HEICO Corporation is **the world's largest independent supplier of FAA-PMA aftermarket parts** for commercial and defense engines and aircraft, founded in **1957 in Hollywood, Florida**. The company has two share classes — **HEI (Common Stock, 1 vote)** trades at a premium vs. **HEI.A (Class A, 1/10 vote)** which trades at a discount; economically identical. This position is in HEI Common.

**Mendelson family leadership**: **Larry Mendelson** took control in 1990 when the company billed $26M and transformed it into ~$4.5B in revenue + ~$37B market cap (22–23% CAGR over 35 years). **He passed away on September 27, 2025, at age 87**. Succession executed per pre-existing plan: **Eric A. Mendelson** and **Victor H. Mendelson** are now **Co-Chairmen of the Board + Co-CEOs**. Eric continues to lead the **Flight Support Group (FSG)**; Victor leads the **Electronic Technologies Group (ETG)**.

**Revenue structure (2 segments)**:

**(1) Flight Support Group (FSG)** — the anchor aftermarket parts segment, ~70% of revenue. Includes **HEICO Parts Group** (>19,500 FAA-PMA approved parts, >500 new/year, >90M parts delivered with ZERO Service Bulletins/Airworthiness Directives/in-flight shutdowns traceable to HEICO), engine accessory & component repair (Wencor, EthosEnergy A&C), avionics repair (Millennium International), in-flight entertainment for business aviation (Rosen Aviation). Customers: global commercial airlines (Delta, United, American, Lufthansa, Emirates) + military operators. Q1 FY2026: $820M revenue (+15% YoY), op income $200.7M (+21%), **margin 24.5%** (expanding).

**(2) Electronic Technologies Group (ETG)** — ~30% of revenue, heavily skewed toward defense/space/aerospace (**81.3% of the segment**). Sub-electronics for missiles, satellites, electronic warfare, RF and microwave; medical/telecom non-aero (~18.7%). Customers: Lockheed Martin, Boeing Defense, Raytheon, NASA/ESA, Northrop Grumman. Q1 FY2026: $370.7M revenue (+12% YoY), **margin compressed to 19.8%** (from 23.1%) due to unfavorable defense/space mix.

**Bolt-on M&A model**: HEICO closes ~10+ acquisitions/year, typically pays 7–9x EBITDA, retains management, decentralizes operations. **Wencor (closed Aug 4, 2023, $2.05B = $1.9B cash + $150M HEI.A)** is the largest historical acquisition and remains the post-2023 anchor. Recent acquisitions: **Millennium International** (FSG, 2025), **Rosen Aviation** (via Mid Continent Controls, May 2025), **EthosEnergy Accessories and Components** (announced Dec 2025, closed Feb 5, 2026), **Sherwood Avionics and Accessories** (Apr 6, 2026, 80% acquired).
HEI.summary_why: HEICO at $270.34 means buying the most proven compounder in the aerospace aftermarket — 35 years of **22–23% CAGR** ($100,000 invested in 1990 would be worth >$100M today) — at a **forward P/E of 42.56x** that is indeed a premium but has been the historical range throughout the modern compounding era. Specific reasons: (1) **Q1 FY2026 was a wide beat — reported Feb 25, 2026**: revenue $1.18B (+14.4% YoY) beat consensus $1.15B; EPS $1.35 vs. estimate $1.27 (**+6.3% beat**); record net income $190.2M (+13%); operating profit $260M (+14.6%). Organic growth **12% in FSG, 6% in ETG**. Q2 FY2026 guidance: EPS ~$1.60. **Q2 FY2026 reports May 26, 2026** (22 days) — first test post-OBBBA legislation. (2) **FSG margin expanding to 24.5%** (from 23.4% Q1 2025): the anchor segment is accelerating margins via Wencor synergies + scale in the PMA portfolio. ETG margin 19.8% compressed by a one-off mix — management guided a rebound. (3) **Aging fleet thesis intact**: Boeing 737 MAX targeting 47/month in 2026 → 52 → 57; 787 already at 7/month, targeting 10/month. But the gap between OEM delivery rates and airline demand → fleets operated longer → greater aftermarket parts consumption and engine shop visits. HEICO has PMA for 737NG, 737 MAX, 757, 767, 777, 787 + Airbus. (4) **Structural PMA moat**: >19,500 FAA-PMA approved parts, >500 new/year, >90M delivered with **ZERO Service Bulletins, Airworthiness Directives, or in-flight shutdowns traceable to HEICO**. PMA allows **25–50% savings vs. OEM** on that specific item — savings any airline's CFO approves immediately. Every new FAA regulation requiring replacement parts generates demand. (5) **Mendelson succession already executed (not an emerging risk)**: Larry transitioned to Executive Chairman early 2025; Eric+Victor had already been operating as Co-CEOs for years. After the September 27, 2025 death, escalation to Co-Chairmen was smooth; HEICO stated 'does not anticipate any changes in operations.' Both brothers have decades of experience in their respective segments. The 'family compounder' continues. (6) **Active M&A pipeline**: 4 acquisitions closed/announced in the past 12 months (Millennium, Rosen, EthosEnergy A&C, Sherwood Avionics). Bolt-on model proven over 35 years. Each deal adds 2–5% EPS accretion + diversifies the portfolio. (7) **Defense sustainment tailwind**: ETG skewed 81.3% toward defense/space/aerospace — global defense budgets rising (Trump prioritized defense, NATO 5% target, Asia-Pacific tensions); MRO sustainment is a protected category. (8) **Conservative capital structure**: HEICO has completed 15 stock splits/dividends since 1995, continues paying semi-annual dividends (~$0.24/year, yield 0.09% — minimal because the stock has compounded so much). No excessive debt; balance sheet allows continued M&A pace. (9) **Analyst consensus Buy**: ~12 analysts, 60% bullish; mean target ~$373–376 (range $278–$415); high target Citi $415 (Jan 13, 2026); RBC $375. Implied upside +38% vs. $270.34.
HEI.summary_risk: The risks are specifically measurable: (1) **Real premium valuation — P/E TTM 53.64x / forward 42.56x**: aerospace & defense peer average ~37x; fair-value multiple based on growth + ROIC ~30x. **There is no margin for error if sentiment changes**. If HEICO misses a quarter or the market rotates out of premium compounders, the correction can be -20–30% with nothing changing operationally. The premium has been the historical range since 2010 but it is not a law of physics. (2) **ETG margin compression Q1 FY2026**: drop from 23.1% → 19.8% due to unfavorable defense/space mix. Management guided a rebound but it must be confirmed — if it's structural (e.g., low-margin contracts won) rather than one-off, FY2026 EPS guidance suffers. (3) **Boeing/Airbus production ramp risk**: if OEM deliveries accelerate aggressively (737 MAX 47→52→57/month, 787 7→10/month), airlines receive new aircraft and aftermarket demand on aging fleets moderates. The aging fleet thesis works while OEM keeps underdelivering — if it exits that slot, the thesis neutralizes. (4) **Recession exposure**: less travel = fewer engine cycles = fewer shop visits = fewer PMA parts consumed. HEICO suffered in the GFC 2008–09 (revenue -10%) and COVID 2020 (revenue -20%). If recession 2026–27, aftermarket is cyclical, though more resilient than OEM. (5) **M&A integration risk at 10+ deals/year**: every year HEICO digests ~$200–500M in bolt-ons. If a large deal goes wrong (Wencor at $2.05B was the first large test — so far so good), the roll-up thesis comes into question. (6) **Family succession — Larry died Sept 2025**: Eric+Victor are solid but the loss of the dealmaker who architected the compounder is a qualitative risk. The third Mendelson generation is not yet proven in decision-making roles. If Eric or Victor (approximately 62 and 58 years old) step back in 5–10 years, there is a family leadership vacuum. (7) **Premium vs. HEI.A spread**: HEI Common trades at ~10–15% premium over HEI.A. Investors wanting cheaper exposure can rotate to HEI.A, pressuring the spread. Historically stable but not guaranteed. (8) **Defense budget reversal**: if a new administration (US, Europe) cuts defense budgets aggressively, ETG at 81% defense suffers. Trump-era prioritization is bullish but Congressional cycles change. (9) **PMA approval slowdown**: if the FAA tightens criteria for approving new PMA parts (under OEM pressure), the >500 new/year pipeline slows. Historically the FAA has been neutral, but pressure points exist.

# HEICO Corporation (HEI) — Full Research

## Price: $270.34 | P/E TTM: 53.64 | Forward P/E: 42.56 | Div Yield: 0.09% | Market Cap: $37.72B

---

## What is HEICO?

HEICO Corporation is **the world's largest independent supplier of FAA-PMA (Parts Manufacturer Approval) aftermarket parts** for jet engines, airframes and electronic components. Founded in **1957** in Hollywood, Florida. The Mendelson family took control in **1990** when Larry Mendelson — an acquirer trained in private equity — saw the potential.

**What followed is one of the most complete compounding track records in the U.S. public market:**

| Metric | 1990 | 2026 |
|--------|------|------|
| Revenue | $26M | ~$4.5B |
| Market cap | below nine-figure | ~$37.72B |
| Multiplier | 1x | **>1,500x** |
| CAGR | — | **22–23% for 35 years** |

A $100,000 investment in 1990 would be worth **>$100 million today**. ~59,337% total return including dividends.

## Share Classes

| Symbol | Voting Rights | Price |
|--------|--------------|-------|
| **HEI (Common)** | 1 vote/share | **$270.34** (this position) |
| HEI.A (Class A) | 1/10 vote/share | ~$215 (discount) |

Economically identical — the premium reflects the voting rights. This position is HEI Common.

## Mendelson Succession — Critical Change (Sept 2025)

**Larry Mendelson (Laurans A. Mendelson) passed away on September 27, 2025, at age 87**. He had been serving as Executive Chairman; succession was executed per plan:

- **Eric A. Mendelson** and **Victor H. Mendelson** are now **Co-Chairmen + Co-CEOs**
- **Eric** continues to lead the Flight Support Group (FSG)
- **Victor** continues to lead the Electronic Technologies Group (ETG)
- HEICO stated it "does not anticipate any changes in operations"

Larry had transitioned to Executive Chairman early in 2025; Eric+Victor had already been operating as Co-CEOs for years. A smooth transition.

## Q1 FY2026 — Reported February 25, 2026

(Fiscal year ends October 31)

- **Revenue**: $1.18B (+14.4% YoY), beat consensus $1.15B
- **EPS**: $1.35 vs. estimate $1.27 (**+6.3% beat**); +12.5% YoY from $1.20
- **Record net income**: $190.2M (+13% YoY)
- **Operating profit**: $260M (+14.6% YoY)
- **Operating cash flow**: $178.6M
- **Organic growth**: 12% FSG, 6% ETG; remainder via M&A

### By Segment

| Segment | Revenue Q1 | YoY | Op Margin | vs Q1 2025 |
|---------|-----------|-----|-----------|------------|
| FSG | $820.0M | +15% | **24.5%** | +110bps (expanding) |
| ETG | $370.7M | +12% | **19.8%** | -330bps (compressed by mix) |

**ETG defense/space/aerospace mix**: $301.2M (81.3%) | Other (medical, telecom): $69.5M (18.7%).

### Guidance

- **Q2 FY2026** (reports May 26, 2026): EPS ~$1.60
- **FY2026 revenue**: $5.01B

## PMA Moat — The Core of the Model

HEICO Parts Group is **the world's largest independent supplier** of FAA-PMA parts:

- **>19,500 FAA-PMA approved parts**
- **>500 new parts/year** (regular pipeline)
- **>90 million parts delivered historically** with **ZERO Service Bulletins, Airworthiness Directives, or in-flight shutdowns traceable to HEICO**
- **25–50% savings vs. OEM** on that specific item
- Approvals for **737NG, 737 MAX, 757, 767, 777, 787** + various Airbus programs

PMA allows airlines to buy generic FAA-approved parts instead of OEM parts — immediate savings with no regulatory penalty. One of the cleanest moats in industrials.

## Recent Acquisitions

| Deal | Date | Segment | Notes |
|------|------|---------|-------|
| **Wencor** (anchor) | Aug 4, 2023 | FSG | $2.05B = $1.9B cash + $150M HEI.A — largest ever |
| Millennium International | 2025 | FSG | 90% of stock; commercial avionics repair |
| Rosen Aviation | May 2025 | FSG | via Mid Continent Controls; in-flight entertainment for business aviation |
| EthosEnergy Accessories & Components | closed Feb 5, 2026 | FSG (via Wencor) | engine accessory + component repair; 175 employees; >175,000 sq ft US + Scotland |
| Sherwood Avionics & Accessories | Apr 6, 2026 | FSG | 80% acquired; management retains 20% |

Model: ~10+ deals/year, ~7–9x EBITDA paid, management retained, decentralized.

## Capital Return

- **Semi-annual dividends** (NOT quarterly) — most recent: January 17, 2025 ($0.110/share). Yield ~0.09%, ~$0.24/yr.
- **15 stock splits/dividends since 1995**. Most recent: 5-for-4 split on both classes.
- Absolute dividend small because the stock has compounded so much — cash returns first to M&A and reinvestment in compounding.

## Key Catalysts

- **Boeing ramp**: 737 MAX 47/month in 2026 → 52 → 57; 787 7/month → 10/month in 2026. HEICO has PMA for both.
- **Aging fleet thesis**: delivery gap vs. demand → older fleets in service → more aftermarket parts consumption and engine shop visits.
- **Defense sustainment tailwind**: ETG 81% defense/space; global budgets rising.
- **EthosEnergy A&C integration** post-Feb 2026: expands engine accessory repair capabilities.
- **Analyst target**: Citi $415 (Jan 13, 2026); RBC $375.

## Analyst Consensus (May 2026)

- **Rating**: Buy. ~12 analysts, 60% bullish.
- **Mean target**: ~$373–376
- **High**: $415 (Citi)
- **Low**: $278–330
- **Upside vs. $270.34**: **+38%** to mean target.

## Key Quantified Risks

1. **Real premium valuation** — P/E TTM 53.64x / forward 42.56x vs. A&D peer ~37x.
2. **ETG margin compression** — 23.1% → 19.8% Q1 FY2026; need to confirm rebound in Q2.
3. **OEM ramp risk** — if Boeing/Airbus accelerate too fast, aging fleet thesis moderates.
4. **Recession exposure** — aftermarket is cyclical (revenue -10% GFC, -20% COVID).
5. **M&A integration risk** — pace of 10+ deals/year.
6. **Family succession** — Larry died Sept 2025; Eric+Victor are solid but the architect is gone.

## Thesis in One Line

> Buy the most proven aerospace aftermarket compounder in the public market (22–23% CAGR for 35 years, >1,500x since 1990) in its historical premium range, with FSG margins expanding, an active M&A pipeline and the aging fleet thesis intact. The Mendelson succession happened and was smooth.

# Howmet Aerospace (HWM) — Complete Research

## Price: $227.90 | P/E: 61.43 | P/E Forward: 41.46 | Div Yield: 0.2% | EPS: $3.71 | Market Cap: $91.7B

---

## What is Howmet Aerospace?

Howmet Aerospace is the **world leader in aerospace engineering components**. They make the critical parts that go INSIDE airplane engines—the blades that spin at extreme temperatures of 2,000+ degrees. If Boeing, Airbus, Pratt & Whitney, or GE Aerospace build a plane or engine, there is a very high chance that Howmet made some of the most important parts.

Founded in 1888, Howmet was part of Alcoa before separating in 2020 as an independent company focused 100% on high-precision aerospace components.

## Business Segments (4 Divisions)

| Segment | Revenue | Description | Growth 2025 |
|----------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Engine Products** | ~35% | Blades, rings, rotating parts for airplane engines | +20% |
| **Fastening Systems** | ~25% | Fasteners, bolts, aerospace connections | Solid growth |
| **Engineered Structures** | ~25% | Titanium ingots, forgings for fuselages and landing gear | Focused on defense |
| **Forged Wheels** | ~15% | Forged aluminum wheels for heavy trucks | Stable cash generator |

## 2025 Results (Full Year)

- **Record Revenue: $8.25B** (+11% YoY)
- **Q4 Revenue: $2.2B** (+15% YoY—strongest quarter)
- **Net Income Q4: $372M** (+18% YoY)
- **Adjusted EBITDA Margins: 30.1%** (company record)
- **Adjusted EPS: $1.05 in Q4** (+42% YoY)
- **Full Year EPS: $3.71**
- **Engine Products grew +20%** in the last quarter
- Commercial grew 13%, defense 20%, gas turbines 32%

## 2026 Guidance

- **Revenue: ~$9.1B** (~10% growth)
- **Adjusted EPS: $4.35 - $4.55** (~20% growth)
- **Target EBITDA margins: 30%+** by year-end
- Strong ongoing demand from commercial aviation and defense

## CAM Acquisition ($1.8B)

- **Consolidated Aerospace Manufacturing** from Stanley Black & Decker
- **Price: $1.8 billion**
- **CAM Revenue: ~$490M** annually
- **Products:** Precision fasteners and connections for aerospace/defense
- **Brands:** Bristol Industries, 3V Fasteners, Moeller, Aerofit, Voss Industries
- **Expected Close:** H1 2026
- Significantly strengthens the Fastening Systems segment

## Dividend and Shareholder Return

- **Annual Dividend: $0.48/share** (~0.2% yield)
- **Consecutive Increases:** 5 years
- **Quarterly Payment:** $0.12
- The company prioritizes growth over dividends—it's a growth story

## Competitive Advantages (Moat)

1. **World leader in aerospace blades:** The parts that spin inside engines at 2,000+ degrees require extreme precision metallurgy—few in the world can do it
2. **Captive customers:** Once an engine is designed with Howmet parts, switching suppliers is nearly impossible. 30+ year programs
3. **Relationships with all OEMs:** Boeing, Airbus, Pratt & Whitney, GE Aerospace, Rolls-Royce—all depend on Howmet
4. **Aerospace supercycle:** Record plane orders + aging fleet = demand for parts for years
5. **Expanding margins:** EBITDA margins rose to 30.1%—real pricing power
6. **Very high entry barrier:** Certifications, advanced metallurgy, and decades of track record

## Catalysts

1. **Commercial aviation supercycle** — Record backlog from Boeing + Airbus = years of parts demand
2. **CAM Acquisition ($1.8B)** — Adds $490M in revenue and strengthens position in fasteners
3. **Expanding margins** — Target of 30%+ EBITDA margins
4. **Defense growth +20%** — Military budgets rising globally
5. **EPS growing 20%+ annually** — Guidance of $4.35-$4.55 for 2026
6. **Industrial gas turbines +32%** — Energy demand drives this segment

## Main Risks

1. **Demanding valuation:** P/E forward of 41x is very expensive—priced for perfection
2. **Dependence on Boeing:** If Boeing continues with production issues, it limits parts deliveries
3. **Recent correction:** Already down ~12% from February highs—could continue to drop if the market gets nervous
4. **CAM integration:** Large acquisitions always carry execution risk
5. **Economic cycle:** If air travel demand drops (recession), demand for new parts drops too

## Geographic Presence

Operates in **13+ countries** with **38 plants** worldwide:
- **U.S.:** 15 states (HQ in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania)
- **Europe:** France, Germany, UK, Hungary, Czech Republic, Netherlands, Morocco
- **Americas:** Canada, Mexico, Brazil
- **Asia-Pacific:** Japan, China, Australia

## Competitive Position

| Metric | **HWM** | TDG (TransDigm) | SPR (Spirit Aero) | HEI (HEICO) |
|---------|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Revenue** | $8.25B | $8.5B | $7.4B | $4.1B |
| **EBITDA Margin** | 30.1% | 49% | ~12% | ~25% |
| **P/E Forward** | 41x | 37x | N/A | 55x |
| **Dividend Yield** | 0.2% | 0% | 0% | 0.1% |
| **Focus** | Engine + structure components | Aftermarket components | Fuselages | Electronic/flight components |

## Analyst Consensus

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Rating | **Strong Buy** |
| Average Price Target | **~$280** |
| Current Price | $227.90 |
| **Upside to Target** | **~23%** |
| EPS 2026 Guidance | $4.35 - $4.55 |

## Conclusion

HWM at $227.90 is a **bet on the aerospace supercycle**—the company that makes the most critical parts inside airplane engines that power Boeing and Airbus. With record revenue, 30% margins, EPS growing 20%+ annually, and the CAM acquisition as a catalyst, Howmet has an exceptional business. The main risk is valuation: at a P/E forward of 41x, the market is already pricing in a lot of growth. For a diversified portfolio, HWM complements our position in GE Aerospace—if GE makes the engines, Howmet makes the critical parts inside them.

---

*Research date: Mar 27, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
HWM.one_liner: Howmet Aerospace makes essential airplane engine parts for giants like Boeing and Airbus, achieving record revenue of $8.25 billion in 2025.
HWM.why_short: It had a strong quarter with $2.2 billion in revenue and a 42% profit growth. It plans to grow further with a $1.8 billion acquisition.
HWM.risk_short: The stock is very expensive compared to its profits. If Boeing has issues or profits fall short, the price could drop. It's already down 12% since February.

It operates over 50 decentralized businesses in about 20 countries. In 2025, it achieved record revenue of $3.46B with $617M in free cash flow.

# IDEX Corporation (IEX) — Full Research

## Price: $197.85 | P/E: 30.87 | P/E Forward: ~24.0x | Div Yield: 1.48% | Market Cap: $14.7B

---

## What is IDEX?

IDEX Corporation is a global manufacturer of **precision-engineered pumps, fluid technology, and fluidics solutions** for critical markets. With over 50 decentralized businesses operating in about 20 countries, IDEX specializes in highly engineered products where reliability is essential.

## Business Segments

| Segment | 2025 Revenue (est.) | % of Total | Description |
|----------|---------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Health & Science Technologies (HST)** | ~$1,490M | ~43% | Components for life sciences, optics, microfluidics, semiconductors, data centers |
| **Fluid & Metering Technologies (FMT)** | ~$1,210M | ~35% | Industrial pumps, flow meters, equipment for water/chemicals/energy |
| **Fire & Safety / Diversified Products (FSDP)** | ~$760M | ~22% | Fire pumps, rescue tools, fastening systems |

## Key Brands

| Brand | Segment | Product |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Viking Pump** | FMT | Positive displacement rotary pumps (#1 worldwide) |
| **Hale Products** | FSDP | Fire truck pumps |
| **Jaws of Life (Hurst)** | FSDP | Hydraulic vehicle rescue tools |
| **BAND-IT** | FSDP | Industrial fastening systems |
| **Microfluidics** | HST | High-pressure processors for pharma/biotech |
| **Mott Corporation** | HST | Advanced filtration of sintered porous materials |

## Key Numbers 2025

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Revenue** | $3.46B (+6% reported, +1% organic) |
| **Net Income** | $483M |
| **EPS** | $6.41 |
| **Adjusted EPS** | $7.95 (+1% YoY) |
| **Gross Margin** | 44.5% |
| **Operating Margin** | 20.2% |
| **Free Cash Flow** | $617M (FCF conversion 103%) |
| **Q4 Orders (record)** | $979M (+20% reported, +16% organic) |

## Dividend — 17 Years of Consecutive Growth

- **Annual Dividend:** $2.92/share ($0.73 quarterly)
- **Yield:** 1.48%
- **Payout Ratio:** ~44%
- **123 consecutive quarters** paying dividends (30+ years)
- **17 consecutive years** of increases

## Analyst Analysis

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Consensus | **Buy** |
| # Analysts Buy | 7 |
| # Analysts Hold | 6 |
| # Analysts Sell | 0 |
| Average Price Target | **$218** |
| High Price Target | $250 |
| Low Price Target | $170 |
| **Upside to Target** | **+10.2%** |

## Growth Catalysts

1. **AI/Data Center Demand** — Record HST orders for liquid cooling and semiconductors
2. **Mott Corporation** — Advanced filtration acquisition opens clean water markets
3. **2026 Guidance** — EPS $8.15-$8.35
4. **$60M in savings from optimization**
5. **Strong balance** — debt/equity of 0.48x for more acquisitions

## Main Risks

1. **High price** — P/E of 30.87x is expensive for +1% organic growth
2. **Weakness in FMT and FSDP** — Chemical, energy, and agriculture markets are sluggish
3. **Dependence on AI/data centers** — If AI CapEx cycle slows, HST loses momentum
4. **Mott integration** — Execution risk
5. **Cyclical exposure** — Vulnerable to global recessions

## Current Valuation

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Price | $197.85 |
| Trailing P/E | 30.87x |
| Forward P/E | ~24.0x |
| Div Yield | 1.48% |
| FCF Yield | ~4.2% |
| 52-Week High | $217.16 |
| 52-Week Low | $153.36 |

## Conclusion

IDEX is a high-quality industrial company with dominant positions in critical niches. The momentum in data centers/AI via HST is real (record orders +34% organic), and the acquisition of Mott adds strategic capabilities. The growing dividend (17 years), solid FCF, and exposure to water and AI megatrends justify a position.

ITUB.summary_short: Itaú Unibanco is **Brazil's #1 private bank by assets** + the **most valuable brand in all of Latin America** (Brand Finance ~R$25B). 70M customers in Brazil + a presence in Argentina, Chile, Colombia, Paraguay and Uruguay. Q1 2026 delivered a **24.8% ROE** (return on equity — profit per dollar of the bank's own capital; vs. JPMorgan 17%, European banks 10%) and net income +10.4% YoY to R$12.3B. The one-of-a-kind feature: **it pays a dividend EVERY MONTH** — on the first business day. Almost no large bank in the world does this; REITs do, banks almost never. The ADR ITUB (1:1 ratio with the ITUB4 preferred share) trades at 8.6x forward price vs. profit with a ~5% USD yield. CEO Milton Maluhy Filho since Feb 2021. Controlling families: Setúbal + Villela + Moreira Salles (via IUPAR).
ITUB.summary_what:
Itaú Unibanco Holding S.A. (NYSE ADR: ITUB, BVMF: ITUB4) is **Brazil's #1 private bank by assets** and **the largest bank in Latin America**. The result of the 2008 merger of Banco Itaú + Unibanco. HQ São Paulo.

**Brand**: the most valuable brand in all of Latin America per Brand Finance (~R$25.0B, the consistent ranking leader since 2017).

**Business segments**:
- **Retail Banking** — 70M+ customers in Brazil, ~32% retail market share (Q2 2025). 29.8M monthly active mobile app users. ~15M already migrated to the Super App.
- **Wholesale + Investment Bank** — Itaú BBA is the #1 M&A and debt capital markets franchise in Brazil/LATAM.
- **Asset Management** — Itaú Asset.
- **Cards & Payments** — Itaucard. Pix-on-WhatsApp rolled out to the entire customer base (August 2025).
- **Latam Affiliates** — operates in Argentina, Chile, Colombia, Paraguay, Uruguay.

**Leadership**:
- **CEO**: **Milton Maluhy Filho** (since Feb 2021).
- **Chairman**: **Pedro Moreira Salles**.
- **Vice-Chair / ex-CEO**: Roberto Setúbal (still influential).
- **Controlling families**: Setúbal + Villela + Moreira Salles via the IUPAR holding company. Long-term oriented, low-turnover board.

**ADR structure**: 1 ITUB ADR = 1 ITUB4 preferred share (not 1:1 with the common ON share — important). Depositary: BNY Mellon.

**Capital**: Basel ratio 15.2%, LCR 215%, NSFR 124.8% — over-capitalized vs. the requirement.
ITUB.summary_why: ITUB at $7.97 means buying Brazil's #1 private bank + LATAM's most valuable brand, with a 24.8% ROE (better than JPM and far above European banks), paying a dividend every month at a ~5% USD yield, at 8.6x forward price vs. profit. Specific reasons: (1) **Monthly dividend + JCP — one of a kind**: it pays R$0.01765 gross / R$0.0150 net per share on the 1st business day of every month (rare outside REITs). Plus a **supplementary JCP** (Juros sobre Capital Próprio, a Brazilian dividend-like payout that is tax-deductible for the bank): in Feb 2026 it declared R$3.85B = R$0.34888/share gross. **Effective USD yield ~5% for the ADR** (after Brazil's 15% withholding tax on JCP). 2025 payout was 73% of adjusted profit = R$33.7B distributed. (2) **24.8% ROE in Q1 2026** (26.4% Brazil-only) — a record for banks of its size globally. JPMorgan ~17%, Bank of America ~14%, European banks ~10%. Itaú generates 25 cents of profit for every dollar of its own capital. (3) **A strong Q1 2026**: net income +10.4% YoY to R$12.3B, lending margins resilient despite the high Selic (Brazil's benchmark interest rate), and 90+ day NPLs (loans gone unpaid) pristine at 1.9% (flat YoY). (4) **The Selic is coming down** — the Copom (Brazil's rate-setting committee) cut to 14.50% on April 28-29, 2026 (from 14.75%). If the cutting cycle continues, there's room for a valuation re-rating: Brazilian banks have historically traded at 12-14x forward when the Selic falls. (5) **BRL +11.5% YoY against the USD** — a real tailwind for ADR holders. Every monthly dividend is worth more in dollars. (6) **Pix dominance + Open Finance**: Itaú is the #1 Pix issuer in Brazil. Pix-on-WhatsApp live since August 2025 for the whole base. Open Finance accelerates cross-selling. (7) **A capital fortress**: a 15.2% Basel ratio is very high vs. the 11.5% minimum. It allows continued dividends + share buybacks without stressing the balance sheet. (8) **Average analyst target of $9 for the ADR = +13% upside**, plus the ~5% USD cash yield = ~18% expected total return over 12 months.
ITUB.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Brazil fiscal risk — the #1**: if Lula breaks the arcabouço fiscal (the spending cap), the market re-prices the Selic upward, the BRL weakens, and the whole banking sector gets punished. The Copom warned on May 5, 2026 that it may pause cuts over Iran-US risk — a Selic stuck at 14.50% for longer = credit stress. (2) **BRL devaluation hits USD ADR returns**: Itaú earns in BRL; every 5% the BRL loses against the USD = -5% on the ADR's returns even if the local bank performs perfectly. The current BRL strength (+11.5% YoY) is no guarantee. (3) **The credit cycle**: 1.9% NPLs (loans gone unpaid) is very low — it can only go up. If Brazil enters a recession or the Iran-US oil shock creates stress, NPLs rise → provisions rise → profit falls. (4) **Nubank (NU) competition**: ~100M Brazilian customers — it keeps pressuring Itaú's retail fee pool. Itaú's defense is the Super App + the corporate franchise + complex credit (Nu has no wholesale arm or strong asset management). (5) **Lula → Lula 2026? → the 2027 election**: periodic political noise affects Brazil sentiment. (6) **JCP tax treatment**: if Brazil reforms the JCP (periodic rumors of eliminating its tax deductibility), the effective payout falls. (7) **Itaú is concentrated in Brazil, ~85% of net income** — the LATAM diversification is marginal. (8) **A Selic floor**: if rate cuts stall at 13-14%, the re-pricing of financial stocks doesn't happen; the valuation stays stuck.
