LEU.summary_short: Centrus Energy is the ONLY US-owned company that enriches uranium — the fuel for nuclear power plants — on US soil. For decades, Russia and Europe dominated this process; now, with the war and the ban on importing Russian uranium, the US needs to produce it at home, and Centrus is the essential piece. It operates the Piketon, Ohio plant — the only one licensed to produce HALEU (high-assay low-enriched uranium), the fuel needed by next-generation nuclear reactors (SMRs). It holds a US Department of Energy order worth ~$900M and a pipeline of signed orders totaling ~$3.9 billion through 2040. Does NOT pay a dividend: all cash goes toward expanding capacity. Q1 2026: revenue $76.7M, EPS $0.45. CEO Amir Vexler. Trades at a high valuation (~58x earnings) because the market is paying for a strategic and irreplaceable asset for US energy and nuclear independence.
LEU.summary_what:
Centrus Energy Corp. (NYSE American: LEU) is a US nuclear fuel company. Its core business is **uranium enrichment**: taking natural uranium and raising the concentration of the U-235 isotope to the level a reactor needs to generate electricity. Headquartered in Bethesda, Maryland; production plant in Piketon, Ohio.

**Two segments**:

**(1) LEU (Low-Enriched Uranium)** — sells standard nuclear fuel to electric utilities that operate nuclear plants in the US and worldwide. Historically bought some material from third parties (including Russia) and resold it; now rebuilding its own domestic enrichment capacity.

**(2) HALEU (High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium)** — the strategic crown jewel. Uranium enriched to higher purity (up to ~20%), the fuel required by **small modular reactors (SMRs)** and next-generation advanced reactors. Centrus is the **only NRC-licensed facility** to produce HALEU in the US, at its Piketon, Ohio plant.

**Why this matters**: after the invasion of Ukraine, the US banned imports of Russian enriched uranium (Russia controlled ~40–50% of the global enrichment market). That left a massive gap in the Western nuclear supply chain. Centrus is the US government's bet on restoring domestic production: it received a **Department of Energy (DOE) order worth ~$900M** and holds a **pipeline of signed orders worth ~$3.9 billion** through 2040.

**Leadership**: CEO **Amir Vexler**. The company does NOT pay a dividend — it reinvests everything in expanding enrichment capacity. Q1 2026: revenue $76.7M, EPS $0.45. The stock is highly volatile: it fell from ~$189 to ~$165 after a Q1 that came in below expectations.
LEU.summary_why: LEU at $167.07 means buying the ONLY US-owned producer capable of enriching uranium on US soil, right when the country is legally required to stop depending on Russia for its nuclear fuel. Specific reasons: (1) **Strategic HALEU monopoly**: Centrus is the only licensed facility to produce the fuel (HALEU) that next-generation nuclear reactors need. Without that fuel, the entire SMR industry cannot start — and Centrus is the bottleneck. (2) **Geopolitical tailwind**: the US banned Russian enriched uranium; Russia controlled ~40–50% of the global market. Rebuilding Western enrichment capacity is now a national security priority, backed by federal funding. (3) **Government backing**: DOE order worth ~$900M + pipeline of signed orders worth ~$3.9 billion through 2040 — very long-term revenue visibility. (4) **Nuclear renaissance**: AI data center electricity demand is pushing tech giants (Microsoft, Amazon, Google) to sign nuclear power agreements; more reactors = more demand for enriched fuel. (5) **Capacity expansion catalyst**: each new centrifuge cascade Centrus brings online at Piketon multiplies production capacity. Analysts: 'Moderate Buy', average target ~$268 (~+60%).
LEU.summary_risk: Risks: (1) **Very high valuation — #1**: at ~58x earnings, the price already bakes in a lot of optimism. If capacity expansion is delayed or HALEU orders arrive more slowly, the stock can correct sharply (as it already did: -13% after a weak quarter). (2) **No dividend**: it is 100% a growth bet; you receive no income while you wait. (3) **Government dependency**: a large part of the investment case depends on DOE funding and contracts; a change in political priorities or federal budget hits directly. (4) **Technical execution**: enriching uranium is among the most complex and regulated processes in existence; any operational, licensing (NRC) or safety issue can halt production. (5) **Extreme volatility**: it is a 'theme stock' that moves with nuclear energy headlines, not stable fundamentals — not for the faint of heart. (6) **SMR demand still early**: next-generation reactors are still being built; if their deployment is delayed by years, HALEU demand takes longer to materialize.

# Centrus Energy (LEU) — Full Research

## What is Centrus?

The **only US-owned company** that enriches uranium — the nuclear fuel — on US soil. Headquartered in Bethesda, Maryland; plant in Piketon, Ohio. Does NOT pay a dividend: reinvests everything in capacity.

## 2 Segments

| Segment | What it is | Note |
|----------|--------|------|
| **LEU (low enrichment)** | Standard nuclear fuel for power plants | Rebuilding domestic production |
| **HALEU (high enrichment)** | Fuel for next-generation reactors (SMRs) | ONLY NRC-licensed plant in the US |

## The Geopolitical Case

- After the Ukraine invasion, the US **banned imports of Russian enriched uranium**.
- Russia controlled **~40–50% of the global enrichment market**.
- Centrus = the US bet on restoring domestic production.
- **DOE order: ~$900M** | **Pipeline of signed orders: ~$3.9 billion** through 2040.

## Leadership

- **CEO**: Amir Vexler.
- Dividend: **$0** (100% reinvestment).

## Anchor Fact

**There is ONE single company with US government permission to make the fuel that next-generation nuclear reactors need — and it's Centrus. For decades, this fuel came mostly from Russia. When the US banned purchases from Russia after the war, a giant gap opened up: someone had to produce it at home. Centrus is that company, and the Department of Energy itself signed it an order worth ~$900 million to do so.**

# Lockheed Martin (LMT) — Full Research

## Price: $610.17 | Price vs. profit: 28.4 | Forward price vs. profit: 20.5 | Dividend (the cash they pay you) Yield: 2.26% | Market Cap: $141B

---

## What is Lockheed Martin?

Lockheed Martin is the **world's #1 defense contractor** and the largest supplier to the Pentagon. They make the **F-35 Lightning II** (the most advanced and widely produced fifth-generation fighter jet), the **HIMARS** (the artillery system that changed the war in Ukraine), and NASA's **Orion spacecraft**. With a backlog of **$194 billion**, they have secured work for years.

## Business Segments (FY2025)

| Segment | Revenue | % Total | Growth | Key Programs |
|----------|---------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Aeronautics** | $30.3B | 40% | +5.7% | F-35, F-16, C-130J, F-22 sustainment |
| **Missiles & Fire Control** | $14.5B | 19% | +13.9% | HIMARS/GMLRS, PAC-3 MSE, JASSM, hypersonics |
| **Rotary & Mission Systems** | $17.3B | 23% | +0.3% | Sikorsky (CH-53K, Black Hawk), Aegis, radars, cyber |
| **Space** | $13.0B | 17% | +4.4% | Orion, GPS III, hypersonics, missile defense |

## 2025 Results (Full Year)

- **Revenue: $75.0B** (+6% YoY)
- **Segment Operating Profit: $6.7B** (+11%)
- **Net Income: $5.0B**
- **EPS: $21.49** (-4% due to $479M pension charge)
- **Free Cash Flow: $6.9B**
- **Backlog: $194B** (record — 4th consecutive year of growth)
- **Book-to-Bill: 1.2x** (receiving more orders than delivering)
- **Buying back their own shares: $3.0B** (6.6M shares)

### Key Deliveries 2025:
- **F-35:** 191 jets delivered (record)
- **PAC-3 MSE:** 620 interceptors (record)

## 2026 Guidance

- **Revenue:** $77.5B - $80.0B
- **EPS:** $29.35 - $30.25 (+39% vs 2025)
- **Free Cash Flow:** $6.5B - $6.8B
- **Capital Investment:** ~$5B (+35% vs 2025)

## Dividend and Shareholder Return

- **Annual Dividend:** $13.80/share (~2.26% yield)
- **Consecutive years of increase:** 24
- **5-year Dividend Growth:** 6.38% CAGR
- **Payout ratio:** ~63%
- **Total returned in 2025:** $6.1B (dividends + buybacks)
- **Beta:** 0.20 — very low big price swings

## Backlog by Segment

| Segment | Backlog |
|----------|---------|
| **Aeronautics** | $59.4B |
| **Missiles & Fire Control** | $46.7B |
| **Rotary & Mission Systems** | $47.7B |
| **Space** | $39.8B |
| **Total** | **$194B** |

$194B = 2.6x annual sales. In just H2 2025, they received $65B+ in new orders.

## Competitive Advantages (Moat)

1. **F-35 Monopoly:** No other fifth-generation fighter jet in Western production. 3,500+ planes contracted
2. **Pentagon Relationship:** #1 contractor with $313B in contracts (2020-2024)
3. **$194B Backlog:** 2.6 years of revenue visibility
4. **Extreme Switching Costs:** Countries buying F-35 are locked in for decades
5. **National Security:** Classified programs are impossible for competitors to replicate
6. **Production Scale:** F-35 learning curve reduces costs every year

## Defense Megatrend

- **NATO raising to 5% of GDP by 2035** (was 2%). Will multiply defense budgets in Europe
- **US defense up ~15% in FY2026**
- **Active Conflicts** (Ukraine, Middle East) accelerate ammunition replenishment
- **New Threats** (hypersonics, drones) require massive defense investment

## Catalysts

1. **NATO 5% GDP** — 32 countries multiplying defense spending, F-35 is the standard
2. **EPS 2026E: $29.80** — +39% growth with firm guidance
3. **Record $194B Backlog** — 2.6x sales, secured pipeline
4. **Ammunition Replenishment** — HIMARS, PAC-3, JASSM with unprecedented demand
5. **International F-35** — Greece, Romania, Czech Republic, more countries in line
6. **US Defense Budget +15%** — LMT is the #1 beneficiary

## Main Risks

1. **F-35 Purchase Cut:** Pentagon cut nearly 50% in FY2026 (Air Force from 48 to 24, Navy from 17 to 12)
2. **Block 4/TR-3 Issues:** $6B cost overrun, years of delay, 4,000+ parts missing
3. **F-35 Maintenance Costs:** Lifecycle cost projected to exceed $2 trillion (GAO)
4. **F-35 Concentration:** ~30% of total revenue. Any disruption has a disproportionate impact
5. **Political Change:** Defense cuts or government efficiency initiatives could affect

## Competitive Position

| Metric | LMT | RTX | NOC | GD |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **Revenue** | $75B | $85B | $42B | $46B |
| **Backlog** | $194B | $217B | $90B | $92B |
| **Dividend Yield** | 2.26% | 2.2% | 1.7% | 2.0% |
| **Pentagon Contracts (5 years)** | $313B | $145B | $81B | $116B |
| **Defense Focus** | 100% | 65% | 90% | 65% |

## Analyst Consensus

- **Rating:** Hold / Neutral (split)
- **Average Price Target:** $596 - $665 (varies by source)
- **Median:** $660
- **Target Range:** $432 - $740
- **Implied Upside:** +8% to median target ($660)

## Conclusion

LMT at $610.17 is a **literal defensive buy** — the world's largest defense contractor with 2.6 years of revenue visibility and 24 years of growing dividends. The geopolitical context (NATO to 5%, active conflicts, US budget +15%) is the most favorable for defense in decades. The main risk is executing the F-35 Block 4 and concentration in a single program. But with EPS guided to +39% in 2026 and a beta of 0.20, it's one of the most stable positions in the portfolio.

MCD.summary_short: The world's largest restaurant chain with over 45,000 locations in more than 100 countries. Its franchise model (95% franchised) generates predictable cash coming in through rents and royalties. 50 consecutive years of increasing dividends (Dividend Aristocrat).
MCD.summary_what: McDonald's isn't really a burger business — it's a real estate and franchise business. The company owns or leases most of the land and buildings where its 45,356 restaurants operate, and subleases them to franchisees. Only about 5% of the restaurants are directly operated by McDonald's. It makes money in three ways: (1) fixed and variable rents on properties, (2) royalties of about 4% on each franchisee's sales, and (3) direct sales from the few company-owned restaurants. This 'collecting rent' model is why it has operating margins of about 46% — extraordinary for the food industry. Its competitive edge is its unmatched global scale, bargaining power with suppliers, and the #1 brand recognition in fast food.
MCD.summary_why: McDonald's is at a digital turning point. Its MyMcDonald's Rewards program already has about 210 million active users (19% more than last year), generating $40 billion in global digital sales. 2025 revenue grew 4% to $26.9 billion with operating margin expanded to 46.1%. The company opened 2,276 new restaurants in 2025 and aims for 50,000 total by 2027. Additionally, it's launching energy drinks and craft sodas to compete with Starbucks in the high-margin beverage segment. Free cash flow grew 8% to $7.2 billion, and the company returned $7.1 billion to shareholders through dividends and buying back their own shares.
MCD.summary_risk: The main risk is pressure on low-income consumers: the CEO warned of a 'two-tier economy' where traffic from lower-income customers has dropped about 10%, and tariffs are raising input costs. If consumer weakness spreads to the middle class, volumes could drop significantly. Additional risks: intensifying price wars in fast food, wage inflation pressuring franchisee margins, exposure to around 100 countries with varied geopolitical risks, and negative equity from massive share buybacks limiting financial flexibility in a crisis.

# McDonald's Corporation (MCD) — Complete Research

## Price: $306.26 | Price vs. profit: 25.5 | Forward Price vs. profit: 23.2 | Div Yield: 2.43% | Market Cap: $215B

---

## What It Is

McDonald's is the **largest restaurant chain in the world**, with **45,356 locations in over 100 countries**. But calling it a "burger restaurant" would be incorrect — it's fundamentally a **real estate and franchise business**. 95% of its restaurants are operated by franchisees who pay rents and royalties to McDonald's.

The brand is recognized by practically everyone on the planet. The Golden Arches are one of the most powerful commercial symbols in history.

## Business Model

McDonald's makes money from **three main sources**:

| Source | Description | Approx % Revenue |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Franchise rents** | Fixed and variable rents on properties McDonald's owns/leases | ~40% |
| **Royalties** | ~4% on gross sales from each franchisee | ~25% |
| **Company-owned restaurants** | Direct sales from ~5% of locations operated by the company | ~35% |

The brilliance of the model: rents and royalties are **high-margin income** (they don't pay for ingredients, employees, or operations). That's why the consolidated operating margin is **46.1%** — extraordinary for any industry, and absurd for the food sector.

McDonald's also owns or leases most of the land where the restaurants operate. This gives it **double leverage**: it collects rent AND royalties. If a franchisee fails, McDonald's retains the property and installs another operator.

## Why Now

**1. Digital Revolution:**
- MyMcDonald's Rewards: **210 million active users** (+19% YoY)
- Digital sales: **$40B** across 70 markets
- Goal: 30% of delivery orders originating in the app by 2027

**2. Accelerated Expansion:**
- 2,276 new restaurants opened in 2025
- Goal: **50,000 restaurants** by 2027 (about 4,644 to go)

**3. Beverage Innovation:**
- Launch of energy drinks, craft sodas, and refreshers
- Tested in 500+ restaurants in 2025
- Competing directly with Starbucks in a **high-margin segment**

**4. Solid 2025 Results:**
- Revenue: $26.9B (+4%, +2% in constant currency)
- Operating income: $12.4B (+6%)
- EPS: $11.95 (+5%)
- Free cash flow: $7.2B (+8%)
- $7.1B returned to shareholders

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Revenue (2025) | $26.9B |
| Net Income (2025) | $8.56B |
| Operating Margin | 46.1% |
| Net Profit Margin | 31.85% |
| Free Cash Flow | $7.2B |
| FCF Yield | ~3.3% |
| Revenue Growth (2025) | +4% |
| EPS Growth (2025) | +5% |
| Dividend/Share | $7.44 annual |
| Dividend Yield | 2.43% |
| Payout Ratio | ~60% |
| Consecutive years of dividend increase | **50 years** (Dividend Aristocrat) |
| P/E TTM | 25.5x |
| P/E Forward | 23.2x |

**Note on negative equity:** McDonald's has bought back so many shares that its shareholders' equity is negative. This does NOT mean the company is in trouble — it's a result of aggressively returning capital to shareholders. The free cash flow of $7.2B demonstrates the business's strength.

## Main Risks

1. **Two-tier economy:** Traffic from low-income consumers has dropped about 10%. If weakness spreads to the middle class, volumes are affected.

2. **Price wars in fast food:** Competitors like Burger King, Wendy's, and Chick-fil-A are intensifying value promotions. This can erode franchisee margins.

3. **Wage inflation:** Minimum wage increases in multiple states/countries pressure operating costs for franchisees.

4. **Geopolitical exposure:** Operating in 100+ countries involves currency, regulatory, and political risks.

5. **Financial leverage:** Negative equity and high debt limit flexibility in the event of a prolonged crisis.

## What Wall Street Analysts Expect

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Consensus | **Buy** |
| # Analysts Buy | 20 |
| # Analysts Hold | 15 |
| # Analysts Sell | 2 |
| Average Price Target | **$342** |
| High Price Target | $407 |
| Low Price Target | $260 |
| **Upside to Target** | **+11.7%** |

---

*Research date: Apr 15, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
MCD.one_liner: McDonald's, the world's largest fast-food chain, makes money through franchises and has increased its payouts to shareholders for 50 years straight.
MCD.why_short: McDonald's is boosting its digital presence with a loyalty program that already has millions of users, increasing its online sales. It's also expanding its drink menu to compete with Starbucks.
MCD.risk_short: The biggest risk is that lower-income people are spending less at McDonald's, and if this affects more people, sales could drop. It also faces higher costs and tough competition in fast food.

MCO.summary_short: Moody's Corporation is one half of the global credit rating duopoly, alongside S&P Global, rating over 80% of the world's public and private debt. Their Q1 2026 report (released 2 days ago) showed adjusted earnings per share of $4.33 (beating expectations by $0.32), an adjusted operating margin of 53.2%, and increased 2026 guidance to $16.40-$17.00. Moody's Analytics, which makes up 63% of their revenue, is 96% recurring subscriptions, making it the most software-like business on Wall Street disguised as a ratings agency. They have increased their dividend for 17 consecutive years (+14.8% in 2026) with a payout ratio of just 28%.
MCO.summary_what: Moody's Corporation is one of the two dominant credit rating agencies in the world, along with S&P Global and, at a distance, Fitch. Founded in 1909 by John Moody in New York, it operates in two main segments: (1) Moody's Investors Service (MIS) — about 37% of revenue, the traditional ratings business. It rates corporate, sovereign, structured (like MBS, ABS, CLOs), and local government bonds. They assign the letter grades (Aaa, Aa1, Baa2, etc.) that determine if a bond is 'investment grade' or 'junk.' They charge issuers for new ratings and an annual fee to maintain them; (2) Moody's Analytics (MA) — about 63% of revenue, the data and software business. It sells research, economic data, credit risk software (like BondX, CreditView), catastrophe models (RMS, acquired for $2B in 2021, used by insurers for modeling hurricanes, earthquakes, climate risk), and KYC/AML services (Bureau van Dijk, a database of over 500 million global companies). 96% of MA's revenue is from recurring subscriptions. CEO since 2021: Robert Fauber, who has been with Moody's for 12 years, previously leading corporate strategy and mergers & acquisitions. Headquarters are in 7 World Trade Center, NYC, with about 16,000 employees in over 40 countries. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway has been the largest shareholder since 2000, holding 24.7 million shares (about 13% of the company), a position Buffett calls an 'eternal hold.'
MCO.summary_why: At $457.97, MCO represents one of the clearest investment cases in the portfolio — it's essentially buying a toll booth on all the world's debt: (1) Regulatory duopoly with insurmountable barriers: Moody's and S&P control over 80% of the global ratings market. Becoming a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (NRSRO) requires SEC approval, and no serious new entrant has been approved in decades. Fitch is a distant third (~15% share), and beyond that, no one else matters. Combine this advantage with over 115 years of audited history and you have a moat that's essentially a data monopoly created and protected by regulators; (2) Refinancing wave 2026-2027: $2 trillion in U.S. investment-grade and high-yield corporate bonds are maturing in the next 24 months. Each must be refinanced (new issuance), and each new issuance means a fee for Moody's. The peak volume is arriving now; (3) Structural private credit boom: The private credit market (direct lending, BDCs, CLOs) grew from $500 billion (2019) to $2 trillion (2026) and is expected to reach $4 trillion by 2030. Each deal requires ratings, and Moody's dominates CLO ratings with about 45% share. This is a new growth line that didn't exist five years ago; (4) Moody's Analytics as disguised SaaS: 63% of revenue is from MA, 96% recurring, with a retention rate over 95%. This is Snowflake-like economics in the body of a 115-year-old company. It grew 11% year-over-year in Q1 2026. The valuation of the MA segment alone would justify over $350 per share; (5) Sustained Q1 2026 beat: Two days ago (April 22), they reported adjusted EPS of $4.33 versus $4.01 expected (an 8% beat), revenue of $2.08 billion (up 8% YoY), and an adjusted operating margin of 53.2%. Full-year 2026 guidance was raised to adjusted EPS of $16.40-$17.00 (versus $16.00-$16.50 previously) with $2.5 billion in share buybacks announced; (6) Dividend growth machine: 17 consecutive years of dividend increases (Contender — two years shy of Aristocrat status). Five-year dividend growth rate of 10.9%. Annual dividend increased by 14.8% in March 2026 to $4.12 with a payout ratio of just about 28% — there's room to double it without affecting capital expenditures or buybacks; (7) Berkshire's eternal hold: Warren Buffett has been buying MCO since 2000, owns 24.7 million shares (about 13% of the company), and has never sold a single one — he calls it 'one of the best franchises in the world.' It's the strongest imaginable endorsement for a long-term Dividend Growth portfolio.
MCO.summary_risk: The main risk is the concentration in debt issuance volume. MIS (37% of revenue) is issuance-based — if a market shock (another 2008 crisis, liquidity crunch, unexpectedly aggressive Federal Reserve) cuts corporate bond issuance, MIS revenue could drop 15-25% in a quarter. Q4 2022 was a reminder: high-yield issuance fell 80% YoY, MCO shares dropped 40%. Other risks include: (1) Constant regulatory pressure: Rating agencies have been politically unpopular since 2008 (Moody's and S&P were blamed for giving AAA ratings to subprime bonds that failed). The SEC, EU (ESMA), and UK (FCA) maintain strict oversight. Any new regulation (e.g., mandatory public panel ratings instead of issuer-paid) poses an existential risk; (2) Historical litigation: In 2017, they paid $864 million to the DOJ and 21 states for pre-2008 ratings. New cases arise — in 2024, they had to pay an additional $11 million for data errors in MA. The plaintiffs' bar remains active; (3) Priced expensively: Price vs. profit TTM is 33.6x and forward is 27.4x — it's not cheap. Compare with S&P Global (SPGI) at 32x forward — similar, but both are premium versus the market at 18x. Any miss could lead to significant downside — the stock is priced for sustained growth; (4) Dependence on interest rates and spreads: A high-rate environment slows new issuance (companies wait to refinance when rates drop). The reverse is also true — very narrow spreads reduce the premium Moody's charges in complex deals; (5) AI disruption in ratings? In the long term, machine learning-based credit scoring models (e.g., Bridgewater, AQR, some funds) could bypass rating agencies. Moody's invests heavily in AI (partnership with Microsoft in 2024 for Moody's Assistant), but the technology could commoditize the 'opinion' they sell; (6) Berkshire sale (unlikely but possible): If Buffett ever sells his 24.7 million shares, it would be a 10%+ drop overnight. 26 years without selling, but post-Buffett Berkshire funds might decide differently; (7) Limited China/Asia exposure: Unlike competitors like Fitch or DBRS, Moody's has a low presence in emerging markets in Asia — a potential missed opportunity if that market develops; (8) Dependence on corporate culture: Robert Fauber has been CEO since 2021 — if he ever leaves, succession is critical because the CEO here must balance regulator-issuer-investor with rare political skill.

# Moody's Corporation (MCO) — Complete Research

## Price: $457.97 | P/E TTM: 33.6x | P/E Forward: 27.4x | Div Yield: 0.91% | Market Cap: $81.8B

---

## What It Is

Moody's Corporation is one of the **two dominant credit rating agencies in the world** (along with S&P Global; and, at a distance, Fitch). Founded in 1909 by John Moody in New York — the same person who in 1900 published the first standardized manual of U.S. railroad bonds, creating the credit analysis industry from scratch.

Today: ~16,000 employees in 40+ countries, HQ in 7 World Trade Center (NYC).

**CEO: Robert Fauber** — since January 2021. 12 years at Moody's before the CEO job — led corporate strategy, M&A, and the MIS segment. Harvard College + Wharton MBA. Reputation for operational focus + capital discipline.

## Segment Structure (FY 2025)

| Segment | % Revenue | What It Sells | Model |
|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Moody's Investors Service (MIS)** | ~37% | Corporate, sovereign, structured (CLOs, MBS, ABS), municipal bond ratings | Issuance fees (at issuance) + surveillance fees (annual) |
| **Moody's Analytics (MA)** | ~63% | Data, research, credit risk software, RMS (catastrophe models), Bureau van Dijk (KYC/AML) | Recurring subscription (96% of MA's revenue) |

Revenue FY 2025: ~$7.3B. Revenue TTM: ~$7.87B. Guidance FY 2026 revenue growth: 8-10%.

## The Business — Explained in Detail

### MIS: The Toll on the World's Debt

When a company (Apple, Exxon, a local government) wants to issue bonds, **they have to pay Moody's or S&P to rate them**. Without a rating, institutional investors cannot buy (many mandates require investment-grade bonds with a specific rating).

**Typical fees**:
- Investment-grade corporate issuance: 0.04-0.07% of face value
- High-yield bond: 0.06-0.10%
- Complex CLO: $500K-$2M flat fee
- Annual surveillance: $50K-$500K per issuer

With $15T+ in corporate bonds + $32T in global sovereign debt in circulation, each rating = revenue. And when the debt is refinanced (bonds mature every 5-10 years), they pay again. It's a structural toll road.

**Market share**: Moody's + S&P combined ~80% of all rated issuances in the U.S. and EU. Fitch ~15%. Others (DBRS, Kroll, Egan-Jones) splitting the 5%.

### MA: The Disguised SaaS

This is the segment the market systematically underestimates. Moody's Analytics sells:

1. **Research & Ratings Data** — access to its 100+ years historical database. Mandatory for any serious bank.
2. **Risk Management Software** — CreditView, BondX, ERM models. Used by 85%+ of global Tier 1 banks.
3. **RMS (Risk Management Solutions)** — acquired in 2021 for $2B. Hurricane, earthquake, wildfire, climate risk models. Every global insurer uses them for reinsurance pricing.
4. **Bureau van Dijk** — acquired in 2017 for $3.3B. Database of 500M+ companies globally. Used for KYC, AML, due diligence.

**Economic dynamics**: 96% recurring revenue. Retention rate 95%+. Net Revenue Retention (NRR) ~110%. Operating margin ~32%. That's a SaaS profile — with 115 years of durability.

The market values SaaS pure-plays at 10-15x revenue. MA would generate $5B in revenue 2026 — implying $50-75B of EV just for MA. MCO as a whole (both segments) has an EV of ~$85B. The ratings (MIS) are practically 'free optionality.'

## Warren Buffett and Berkshire Hathaway

- **First purchase**: 2000, when Moody's spun off from Dun & Bradstreet
- **Current position**: 24.7M shares (~13% outstanding), valued at ~$11.3B
- **Has never sold a single share** in 26 years
- Buffett to shareholders: "The Moody's thesis is the same as 20 years ago — it's one of the best business models in the world."
- Has said he wouldn't buy it today at the current price (admitted in 2018), but holds because it's "the franchise."

This is the only significant Buffett stock pick that has never been touched. No stronger endorsement possible.

## Q1 2026 — The Pivotal Quarter (April 22, 2026)

Two days ago they reported:

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Q1 2025 | YoY | vs Est |
|---------|---------|---------|-----|--------|
| Revenue | $2.08B | $1.91B | +8.95% | miss $2.082B est |
| Adj EPS | $4.33 | $3.92 | +10.5% | **beat $4.01 (+$0.32)** |
| GAAP EPS | $3.73 | $3.45 | +8.1% | - |
| Adj Op Margin | 53.2% | 52.1% | +110 bps | - |
| Op Cash Flow | $893M | $720M | +24% | - |
| Free Cash Flow | $844M | $670M | +26% | - |

By segment:

| Segment | Revenue Q1 2026 | YoY |
|----------|-----------------|-----|
| MIS (ratings) | $770M | +4% |
| MA (analytics) | $1.31B | +11% |

**Capital return Q1 2026**:
- $1.5B in buybacks (first quarter)
- $185M in dividends
- Total: $1.7B (~2% of the market cap in a quarter)

**Guidance FY 2026 raised**:
- Adj diluted EPS: **$16.40-$17.00** (vs $16.00-$16.50 previous)
- Revenue growth: 8-10%
- Adj operating margin: 51-52%
- Buybacks: ~$2.5B (vs $2.0B previous)

The market reacted moderately: MCO +3% post-earnings. The stock had already risen ~12% YTD in anticipation.

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Price** | $457.97 |
| **Market Cap** | $81.8B |
| **P/E TTM** | 33.6x |
| **P/E Forward** | 27.4x |
| **EPS TTM** | $13.66 |
| **EPS Forward (guide midpoint)** | $16.70 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 0.91% |
| **Annual Dividend** | $4.12 |
| **Payout Ratio** | ~28% |
| **52w High** | $518.32 |
| **52w Low** | $378.45 |
| **Revenue TTM** | $7.87B |
| **Revenue Growth YoY** | +8.95% |
| **Profit Margin** | 31.9% |
| **Operating Margin** | 44.5% |
| **ROE** | ~55% |
| **FCF TTM** | ~$2.9B |
| **Buyback 2026 announced** | $2.5B |

## Dividend History

- **17 consecutive years of dividend increases** (Dividend Contender — 2 years shy of Aristocrat status)
- **5-year DGR**: 10.91% annually
- **10-year DGR**: 11.8% annually
- **Annual dividend**: $4.12/share (quarterly $1.03)
- **Last increase**: March 2026, +14.8% (from $3.59 to $4.12)
- **Payout ratio**: ~28% (extremely conservative — ample room)
- **Next ex-dividend**: expected June 2026

## Why Now

### 1. Refinancing Wave 2026-2027
$2T in U.S. IG + HY corporate bonds maturing in the next 24 months. Each one = new fee for Moody's. The peak is arriving now.

### 2. Private Credit Boom
Private credit market grew from $500B (2019) to $2T (2026), projected $4T by 2030. Moody's has ~45% share in CLO ratings. New structural growth line.

### 3. Moody's Analytics Accelerating
MA grew +11% YoY in Q1 2026 — accelerating. It's the fastest-growing + highest-margin recurring segment. The market hasn't realized that 63% of revenue is SaaS.

### 4. Raised Guidance Post-Q1
Management showed confidence by raising guidance midpoint +2.5% post-Q1 — signal of high visibility in the issuance pipeline.

### 5. Aggressive Buybacks
$2.5B announced for 2026 = ~3% of the market cap. Over the next 4 years that's ~12% of shares retired — compound growth for EPS.

### 6. Reasonable Valuation in Context
P/E forward 27.4x vs SPGI 32x (direct peer). MCO has ~15% discount vs the only exact comparable. Both are premium vs S&P 500 at ~18x — but justified by the moat and recurring revenue profile.

### 7. Berkshire Hathaway as Anchor
Buffett's 13% position that never sells = psychological floor for the stock. As long as Berkshire doesn't sell (and hasn't in 26 years), there's a structural demand base.

## Risks

### 1. Dependence on Issuance Volume
MIS (37% of revenue) is issuance-based. A shock that halts bond issuance (2008-like liquidity crisis, unexpectedly aggressive Fed) = MIS revenue drops 15-25% in a quarter. Q4 2022: HY issuance fell 80% YoY, MCO stock -40%.

### 2. Perpetual Regulatory Pressure
Rating agencies have been politically unpopular since 2008. SEC, ESMA (EU), FCA (UK) maintain strict oversight. Periodic proposals (most recent, 2024 in European Parliament) to create a public "Euro Rating Agency" to compete — hasn't passed, but the risk exists.

### 3. Historical and Future Litigation
- 2017: paid $864M to DOJ + 21 states for pre-2008 subprime ratings
- 2024: $11M additional for data errors in MA
- Plaintiffs' bar remains active — any dramatically wrong rating can trigger class-action

### 4. Not-Cheap Valuation
27.4x forward isn't cheap absolute. If the macro environment turns hostile (higher rates, widening spreads), the multiple can contract to 20-22x — that's -15 to -25% downside from multiple alone.

### 5. Long-Term AI Disruption
ML-based credit scoring from hedge funds and tech companies (Palantir, AQR, Bridgewater) could eventually bypass rating agencies for sophisticated investors. Moody's invests heavily in AI (Moody's Assistant with Microsoft, 2024) to get ahead — but the technology could commoditize the 'expert opinion' they sell.

### 6. Hypothetical Berkshire Sale
If Buffett or post-Buffett Berkshire ever decides to sell the 24.7M shares, it would be -10% overnight. Hasn't happened in 26 years, but post-Buffett funds might have different criteria.

### 7. Dependence on Interest Rates
Very high rates → companies postpone issuance waiting for lower rates. Very narrow spreads → reduced premium for complex ratings. Current rate environment of ~4% US 10-year is the sweet spot — if it moves dramatically in either direction, it affects.

### 8. Fauber Succession
CEO since 2021. If he leaves, succession is critical because the job requires balancing regulators + issuers + investors + employees with specific political skill. Poor succession = multi-year execution risk.

## Conclusion

MCO at $457.97 lets you buy:

1. **Half of a regulatory duopoly** with an impossible-to-penetrate moat (NRSRO status + 115 years of data)
2. **Toll on $47T+ of global debt** with refinancing wave 2026-2027 guaranteeing volume
3. **SaaS with 96% recurring (MA)** disguised as a ratings agency — 63% of revenue is substantially better business than the market recognizes
4. **Dividend Contender**: 17 years of dividend increases, payout ratio 28%, DGR 10-11%
5. **Aggressive capital return**: $2.5B buybacks + $800M dividends = $3.3B returned to shareholders in 2026 (~4% of market cap)
6. **Berkshire anchor**: Buffett owns 13%, never sold — the strongest endorsement in quality compounding
7. **Q1 2026 momentum**: $0.32 EPS beat, raised guidance, adj op margin 53.2%

It's not cheap absolute — it's paying a fair price for an exceptional business. The thesis isn't multiple-expansion; it's **a compounder held for 10+ years with 9-11% annual EPS growth + 0.9% dividend + 3% buyback yield = ~13-15% total annualized return**.

If your thesis is "buy businesses with mathematically impossible-to-replicate moats and hold them for decades," Moody's is one of the top 5 stocks in the S&P 500 by that definition.

---

*Research date: April 24, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
MCO.one_liner: Moody's rates global debt for countries and companies, recently reporting better-than-expected earnings and raising its profit outlook.
MCO.why_short: Moody's acts like a toll booth, charging whenever a country or company borrows money. It controls nearly the entire ratings market with S&P, and its financial analysis business is steadily growing.
MCO.risk_short: The biggest risk is if companies stop issuing debt due to a crisis, Moody's could earn less. Regulatory changes could also impact its business.

MDLZ.summary_short: The global snack giant. Owners of Oreo (#1 worldwide in cookies), Cadbury, Toblerone, Milka, Trident, Tang, Philadelphia, and Ritz. Presence in 150+ countries with revenue of ~$36B.
MDLZ.summary_what:
Mondelez International is one of the largest snack companies in the world, created in 2012 as a spin-off from Kraft Foods. Based in Chicago, it operates in 150+ countries generating ~$36B in annual revenue.

Its portfolio includes some of the most globally recognized snack brands: Oreo (the #1 cookie in the world), Cadbury (iconic chocolate in the UK/India/Australia), Milka (leading chocolate in Europe), Toblerone (premium Swiss chocolate), Philadelphia (cream cheese), Trident (gum), Tang (powdered drink), Ritz (crackers), belVita (breakfast biscuits), and Halls (lozenges).

Mondelez generates ~75% of its revenue outside the US, with dominant positions in emerging markets where snack consumption is growing faster. Operating margin of ~17% with a target to expand to 18-19%.
MDLZ.summary_why:
Mondelez is the perfect defensive play: snack brands that people buy in any economy. Oreo, Cadbury, and Milka have real pricing power — they're low-cost impulse buys that withstand recessions.

At $57, MDLZ trades at a price vs. profit forward of 16.9x — a significant discount compared to its historical average of ~22x. The 20% drop from highs ($71) is due to concerns over cocoa costs, but these are already normalizing. Dividend of 3.5% with over 10 years of consecutive growth and a sustainable payout ratio of ~55%.

Wall Street analysts expect a Buy with an average target of ~$68 (19% upside). Mondelez combines defense (essential snacks), growth (emerging markets), and income (growing 3.5% dividend).
MDLZ.summary_risk:
The costs of raw materials (especially cocoa and sugar) have put pressure on how much they keep per sale — the price of cocoa tripled in 2024-2025, and although it's decreasing, big price swings persist and can squeeze earnings.

Additional risks: significant currency exposure (75% revenue outside the US) with a strong dollar eroding reported results; increasing regulation of processed foods and labeling in key markets (EU, Mexico, India); intense competition from local brands in emerging markets; and risk of changing consumer habits towards 'healthy' snacks that could displace traditional categories.

MLI.summary_what: Mueller Industries is one of the largest manufacturers of copper, brass, aluminum, and plastic products in America. Founded in 1917, the company operates through three segments: Piping Systems (copper pipes and fittings), Industrial Metals (brass and aluminum casting), and Climate (HVAC and refrigeration components). Its flagship brand, Streamline®, has been favored by plumbers and HVAC technicians for over 90 years. Mueller provides the essential, unseen components that keep plumbing, air conditioning, and refrigeration running in homes, commercial buildings, and industries. It operates with about 4,800 employees through subsidiaries like Mueller Streamline Co., Mueller Brass, ATCO Rubber Products, and Mueller Impact.
MLI.summary_why: Mueller Industries is a cash flow machine hidden in plain sight. With revenue of $4.2B and a profit margin of about 18%—extraordinary for an industrial company. The price of copper is at record highs, and Mueller benefits directly. At $113, MLI trades at a price vs. profit ratio of 13.5x—a significant discount for a company with revenue growth over 10%, industry-leading margins, and a spotless balance sheet. Modest but growing dividend (0.9%) with aggressive buying back of their own shares reducing the share count. Analysts have a Buy consensus with an average target of about $143 (27% upside). Mueller is the perfect infrastructure pick: every new house, every new HVAC system, every renovation uses its products.
MLI.summary_risk: Mueller relies on the price of copper—if prices drop significantly, profit margins could shrink, and revenue might fall, as copper is a raw material and pricing component. Additional risks include the cyclical nature of the construction sector (new housing and commercial); competition from plastic alternatives (PEX) replacing copper pipes in some uses; geographic concentration in North America; and the risk of an economic slowdown impacting demand for HVAC and plumbing.

At a forward price vs. profit ratio of 13.5x, Mueller trades at a significant discount compared to similar industrials. The price of copper at record highs directly benefits its results. And with a payout ratio of just 15%, there is enormous room for dividend growth and buybacks.

The infrastructure boom, copper at highs, and the energy transition are long-term catalysts. With a 27% upside according to analysts, active buybacks, and a dividend with enormous growth potential, Mueller is the infrastructure pick no one is seeing.

---

MLI.one_liner: Mueller Industries makes copper pipes and parts for plumbing and air conditioning, benefiting from high copper prices.

MLI.risk_short: If copper prices drop, Mueller could earn less. It also faces competition from plastic pipes and relies heavily on the construction market in North America.

The remaining 10% is corporate/unallocated. Iconic products invented at 3M include Scotch Tape (1925), Scotch-Brite (1958), Post-it Notes (1974, by accident — engineer Spencer Silver invented a weak adhesive in 1968 while looking for a strong one), Thinsulate, Command strips, N95 respirators, and reflective sheeting (used on all traffic signs worldwide). He is an engineer (Princeton + Wharton MBA), and upon joining 3M, he did what no one had done in 30 years: a complete portfolio review. He identified over 60 product lines for review and closed or sold over 30 in 2025. Radical simplification; (3) Post-Solventum clarity: in April 2024, they spun off Solventum (NYSE: SOLV) — the entire Health Care segment (~$8B revenue, high margins but required integration). It left 3M as a pure industrial company. Each now competes separately — perfect focus; (4) PFAS exit executed: 3M promised to exit PFAS (forever chemicals) manufacturing by the end of 2025 — they delivered. They paid a $440M installment of the $12.5B settlement for water utilities on April 15, 2026. The settlement is structured over 13 years (2023-2036): each year it becomes more manageable relative to free cash flow ($5B+ annually); (5) Combat Arms earplug settlement closed: $6B agreed in 2023, payable in installments until 2029 — removes the second large legal overhang; (6) Reasonable valuation post-cleanup: priced at 21.3 times past earnings and 17 times future earnings, it's not cheap in absolute terms but reasonable for an industrial with margins expanding 70-80 basis points this year according to guidance. Honeywell trades at 24 times; Emerson Electric at 23 times; Illinois Tool Works at 24 times — MMM at about 17 times future earnings has the 'is it still a good idea?' discount; (7) Capital return restored: dividend $3.12 (yield 2.14%) with payout ratio about 46% — very sustainable. Buyback program of $7.5B authorized in November 2025, executing about $1.5B/year; (8) Portfolio of unique moats: Scotch, Post-it, Command are category-defining with dormant pricing power. 3M has over 130,000 active patents. Other risks: (1) Industrial cyclicality: about 60% of revenue correlates with global manufacturing production. # 3M Company (MMM) — Full Research

## Price: $145.48 | P/E TTM: 21.33 | P/E Forward: 16.92 | Div Yield: 2.14% | Market Cap: $79.7B

---

## What It Is

3M is the **most iconic science-intensive industrial conglomerate in the US**. Founded in 1902 in Two Harbors, Minnesota, as Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing Company — originally to extract and sell corundum (an abrasive stone). That failed, but while searching for alternatives, they invented the first waterproof sandpaper. That mindset — "if the plan doesn't work, invent something new with what you have" — became their culture.

Today: about 61,000 employees, over 70 plants in the US, over 80 international plants, operations in more than 200 countries, over 60,000 products marketed. HQ in Saint Paul, Minnesota.

**CEO: William "Bill" Brown** — since May 2024, first external CEO in about 40 years. Previously CEO of L3Harris Technologies (2018-2023), where he quadrupled the stock. Engineer (Princeton, Wharton MBA). Reputation for operational rigor, lean manufacturing, and portfolio simplification.

## Post-Solventum Structure (April 2024)

| Segment | % Revenue 2025 | Key Products |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Safety & Industrial** | ~43% | Industrial Scotch tapes, abrasives, N95 respirators, PPE, sealants, electrical insulations |
| **Transportation & Electronics** | ~32% | Automotive adhesives, optical films (iPhone/TV/monitor screens), fiber optics, aerospace materials |
| **Consumer** | ~15% | Scotch, Post-it, Command, Scotch-Brite, Nexcare, Filtrete, Ace |
| **Corporate/Other** | ~10% | Residual revenues and unallocated costs |

Revenue FY 2025: ~$24B (post-spin). Versus pre-spin when it was ~$32B with Health Care included.

## The Lost Decade (2014-2024)

Before understanding the buy thesis, it's important to understand what went wrong:

### 1. The Inge Thulin → Mike Roman Era (2014-2024)

Inge Thulin (CEO 2012-2018) led 3M conservatively. Mike Roman (2018-2024) maintained the course. But during that decade:

- **Organic growth stalled**: 2014-2023 average organic growth ~1.2% annually. Well below comparable industrials (Honeywell ~3%, Illinois Tool Works ~3%).
- **Portfolio inflated**: they kept adding SKUs without cutting. Operating complexity rose, margins fell.
- **R&D as % revenue dropped**: historically 6%+ → by 2024 it was ~4.8%. The innovation pipeline dried up.
- **Poor brand management of Scotch/Post-it**: consumer lines grew below inflation — lost share to Henkel in Europe and private labels in retail.

### 2. Litigation Avalanche

Two simultaneous legal catastrophes:

**PFAS (2019-present)**: 3M pioneered PFAS (perfluoroalkyl substances) — the "forever chemicals". They used them in Scotchgard, tapes, firefighting foams. Since the 90s, there's evidence they cause cancer. In 2019, massive lawsuits emerged from states and water utilities. In June 2023, 3M agreed to the **$12.5B settlement for US drinking water systems** (final court approval in April 2024). Structured over 13 years — annual payments ~$440M in April. Separate lawsuits from individual states, personal injury suits, and European regulation continue. 3M exited PFAS manufacturing by the end of 2025 (fulfilled).

**Combat Arms Earplugs (2018-2023)**: 3M sold earplugs (Combat Arms v2) to the US military. Veterans alleged defects causing hearing loss. More than **260,000 individual lawsuits** — the largest multidistrict litigation in US history. In August 2023, 3M agreed to a **$6B settlement**, payable in cash + stock over 2024-2029.

Combined: ~$18.5B in liabilities, nearly three times 3M's annual earnings.

### 3. Solventum Spinoff (April 2024)

3M announced in July 2022 that it would spin off its Health Care segment (wound care, dental, hospital software). Completed April 1, 2024, as **Solventum Corporation** (NYSE: SOLV).

Reasons for the spin:
- Health Care had different market dynamics (more growth, more capital-intensive, regulated)
- Freed up capital management attention to focus on industrial
- A "pure" Health Care ticker captured a premium valuation (SOLV trades at ~25x forward vs MMM ~17x)

But the spin required cutting the dividend:
- 3M pre-spin: annual dividend ~$6.00 (yield ~5% at $120)
- Post-spin (April 2024): dividend fell to ~$2.80 (adjusted for Solventum distributed)
- **Lost "Dividend King" status**: 67 consecutive years of raising the dividend → truncated.

That triggered massive sales: investors who bought 3M *specifically* for the Dividend Aristocrat status rotated to Johnson & Johnson, PG, KO. That group won't return.

## William Brown — The Turnaround (May 2024-present)

Mike Roman resigned in April 2024 right after the Solventum spin. The board did something 3M hadn't done in ~40 years: hire an external CEO.

**William M. Brown** was CEO of L3Harris Technologies (merged Harris + L3 in 2019). Under his leadership:
- L3Harris stock: $153 (August 2018) → $210 (April 2023) → ~4x over the S&P industrial index
- Consolidated over 60 plants into 30
- Expanded operating margin ~400 basis points
- Executed $8B in non-core divestitures

At 3M, Brown has done in 24 months what Mike Roman didn't do in 6 years:

### 1. Radical Portfolio Review (June 2024)
First CEO in over 30 years to conduct a full review of the 60,000+ SKUs. Result:
- ~30% of products identified as "low strategic value" or "low margin"
- Plan to rationalize 30-40% of those over 2025-2027

### 2. Plant Consolidation
- 2024: closed 7 plants (mainly in the US and Mexico)
- 2025: closed/sold 12 more plants
- Total target: ~25 plants closed/sold by 2027 (20% of the footprint)

### 3. Headcount Reductions
- September 2024: announced 6,000 global cuts
- April 2025: 2,500 more
- Total: ~8,500 reduction (~12% of the global workforce)

### 4. Operating Margin Recovering
- FY 2023: op margin GAAP ~16%
- FY 2024: 19.8%
- FY 2025: 21.5%
- Q1 2026: **23.2%** (+230 basis points YoY)

### 5. R&D Reinstated
- Brown announced in Q3 2025 call that R&D will be restored to ~5.5% of revenue (vs 4.8% current)
- New labs in Austin and Singapore operating from 2026

## Q1 2026 Earnings — The Pivotal Moment (April 21, 2026)

Results (2 days ago):

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Q1 2025 | YoY |
|---------|---------|---------|-----|
| GAAP Revenue | $6.00B | $5.92B | +1.3% |
| Adj EPS | $2.14 | $1.88 | +13.8% |
| Adj Op Margin | 23.2% | 20.9% | +230 basis points |
| GAAP EPS | $1.23 | $2.05 | -40%* |
| Free Cash Flow | $1.1B | $0.9B | +22% |

*GAAP EPS fell due to one-time charges (restructuring + PFAS settlement payment)

By segment:
| Segment | Organic Growth |
|----------|----------------|
| Safety & Industrial | +2.5% |
| Transportation & Electronics | +1.1% |
| Consumer | -1.2% |

**FY 2026 Guidance Raised**:
- Adj EPS: $8.50-$8.70 (prev $8.40-$8.60)
- Organic sales growth: ~3%
- Operating margin expansion: +70-80 basis points over FY 2025

The market reacted: MMM +7% on April 21, closing at $145+ after the call.

## Financial Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Price** | $145.48 |
| **Market Cap** | $79.7B |
| **P/E TTM** | 21.33x |
| **P/E Forward** | 16.92x |
| **EPS TTM** | $6.82 |
| **EPS Forward (guide midpoint)** | $8.60 |
| **Dividend Yield** | 2.14% |
| **Annual Dividend** | $3.12 |
| **Payout Ratio** | ~46% |
| **52w High** | $177.41 |
| **52w Low** | $134.85 |
| **Drawdown from high** | -18.0% |
| **Revenue FY 2025** | ~$24B |
| **Adj Op Margin FY 2025** | 21.5% |
| **ROE** | ~28% |
| **FCF LTM** | ~$4.8B |
| **Net Debt** | ~$10B |
| **Buyback authorized (Nov 2025)** | $7.5B |

## Why Now

### 1. Q1 2026 Confirmed Turning Point
+230 basis points margin expansion is not noise — it's the first sustained evidence that Brown's work is translating into numbers. The market is just starting to process.

### 2. Valuation vs Peers
Honeywell 24x forward, Emerson 23x, ITW 24x, Illinois Tool Works 24x. MMM at 17x forward — the cheapest pure industrial in the sub-sector with documented turnaround.

### 3. Legal Overhang Capped
PFAS ($12.5B) and Earplugs ($6B) are both structured. It's not total risk elimination but the "tail" is now calculable — the market can model, not fear.

### 4. Capital Return Restored
Dividend raised to $3.12 (first increase post-spin) + $7.5B buyback authorized = clear board signal that capacity was restored.

### 5. Spinoff Clarity
A pure industrial, without Health Care. Easy to analyze, easy to compare vs peers. The multiple will normalize.

### 6. Consumer Discretionary Recovery
If the US avoids recession in 2026, Transportation (auto OEM) and Consumer (Scotch, Post-it) segments can have a better year. Guidance assumes flat growth there — upside exists.

## Risks

### 1. PFAS — The Long Tail
The $12.5B settlement covers US water utilities, not covered: (a) individual states (NJ, NH, MN are litigating separately); (b) personal injury (individual cancer plaintiffs); (c) European regulation (ECHA may impose new limits in 2027); (d) future federal actions. Analysts estimate additional exposure of $5-15B.

### 2. Industrial Cyclicality
About 60% of revenue is pro-cyclical. If global manufacturing enters a recession in 2H 2026, organic growth can return to flat. Guidance +3% assumes soft landing.

### 3. Internal Culture vs External CEO
William Brown is the first external CEO in over 40 years. 3M culture is dense: "15% time" (engineers dedicate 15% of time to own projects — that's how Post-it was born), average tenure ~15 years, constant internal rotation. Brown comes from top-down defense contractor culture. Cultural clashes can undermine execution.

### 4. Dividend Aristocrat Status Lost
67 years of increases broken in 2024. There's no way to regain that rating — the investors who buy specifically Dividend Kings (there are dedicated ETFs like NOBL) have already rotated out. The marginal buyer base now is turnaround/value, not income.

### 5. Competition in "Neglected" Products
While 3M spent a decade fighting lawsuits, competitors took share:
- Henkel (Germany) in industrial adhesives
- Honeywell in N95 and PPE
- Nitto Denko (Japan) in optical films
- Private labels in Consumer (Scotch copies at Target, Amazon Basics)

Regaining share takes years and money.

### 6. China Exposure
About 8% of revenue comes from China (it used to be ~10%). Over 10 plants in China that cannot be easily relocated. Tariffs or US-China trade tensions would directly affect.

### 7. R&D Gap May Persist
Brown promised to raise R&D to 5.5% revenue, but increasing engineers in Minnesota is not trivial — tight labor market. If not fulfilled, the innovation pipeline (3M's historical advantage) continues to weaken.

### 8. Free Cash Flow Constrained
About $440M/year in PFAS payments until 2036 + Earplugs payments + capex for transformation = ~$1B annually committed. That restricts flexibility in M&A, more aggressive buybacks, or R&D acceleration.

## Conclusion

MMM at $145.48 allows you to buy:

1. **An iconic 123-year-old industrial** with over 130,000 patents, over 60,000 SKUs, and moats in category-defining products (Scotch, Post-it, Command, N95)
2. **Documented turnaround**: William Brown 24 months in the role, operating margin +230 basis points YoY, $8B in cost-outs identified, portfolio simplification underway
3. **Post-cleanup**: Solventum spun off (2024), PFAS manufacturing exited (2025), settlements structured (no more catastrophic surprises)
4. **Reasonable valuation**: 17x forward when peers are 23-24x — ~30% discount that should close with each quarter of execution
5. **Renewed capital return**: $3.12 div (2.14% yield) + $7.5B buyback authorized = ~4-5% total shareholder return this year via capital
6. **Q1 2026 as catalyst**: first solid quarter post-Brown, market just starting to believe

It's not a retirement-dividend (that broke in 2024). It's a **margin improvement + multiple normalization thesis** in a historic industrial with capped legal issues.

If your thesis is "buy a blue-chip industrial with pedigree where the turnaround is not yet priced-in," 3M post-Solventum is the most obvious option in the sector.

---

*Research date: April 23, 2026 | Next review: Oct 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
MMM.one_liner: 3M makes well-known products like Scotch and Post-it and is refocusing its business under new leadership, showing signs of improvement.
MMM.why_short: 3M is showing signs of recovery after tough years, thanks to new leader William Brown, who has simplified the business and improved profits, potentially setting the company up for growth again.
MMM.risk_short: The biggest risk is 3M still faces costly lawsuits over chemicals, which could be expensive in the future, along with competition and global economic factors that could impact sales.

It operates in 4 segments: Aeronautics (B-21 Raider, drones — 29% revenue), Mission Systems (radars, cybersecurity, C4ISR — 28%), Space Systems (satellites, GEM 63, missiles — 24%), and Defense Systems (munitions, logistics — 18%). It is the main contractor for the B-21 and the next-generation Sentinel ICBM.

# Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) — Complete Research

## Price: $691.43 | P/E: 23.46 | P/E Forward: 24.43 | Div Yield: 1.35% | Market Cap: $98B

---

## What is Northrop Grumman?

Northrop Grumman is the **second largest defense contractor in the US** and the maker of the **B-21 Raider**, the most advanced next-generation stealth bomber in the world. It is also the main contractor for the **Sentinel ICBM**, the replacement for the Minuteman III. With a backlog of **$95.7 billion**, the company has income visibility for years.

## Business Segments (FY2025)

| Segment | Revenue | % Total | Key Programs |
|----------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| **Aeronautics Systems** | $13.0B | 29% | B-21 Raider, Global Hawk, Triton, autonomous drones |
| **Mission Systems** | $12.5B | 28% | Radars, cybersecurity, C4ISR, advanced sensors |
| **Space Systems** | $10.8B | 24% | Satellites, GEM 63, ballistic missiles, Sentinel ICBM |
| **Defense Systems** | $8.0B | 18% | Munitions, IBCS, logistics, training |

## 2025 Results (Full Year)

- **Revenue: $42.0B** (+2% YoY)
- **Q4 Revenue: $11.71B** (+9.6% YoY) — significant acceleration
- **EPS (Adjusted): $29.08**
- **Free Cash Flow: $3.3B** (+26% YoY — third consecutive year of 25%+ growth)
- **Backlog: $95.7B** (record)
- **Book-to-Bill: 1.10x** (receiving more orders than delivering)
- **Net Awards: $46B+** in 2025
- **International sales: +20%** in 2025

## 2026 Guidance

- **Revenue:** $43.5B - $44.0B (~4% growth)
- **EPS Adjusted:** $27.40 - $27.90 (analyst consensus: $28.05)
- **Note:** EPS down vs 2025 due to higher capital investments and B-21 production costs

## The B-21 Raider — The Game Changer

The B-21 Raider is the **largest bomber program in 30 years**:

- **$4.5B expansion contract** agreed in Feb 2026 to increase production capacity by +25%
- **Northrop investing an additional $2-3B** of its own capital to accelerate
- **Current production:** ~8 planes/year, rising to ~10/year
- **Minimum program:** 100 planes (potential for more)
- **First operational plane:** Will arrive at Ellsworth AFB in 2027
- **$5B+ invested** in digital infrastructure and manufacturing
- The program is not yet fully reflected in guidance — **greater upside not captured**

## The ICBM Sentinel — The Known Risk

- **Nunn-McCurdy overrun:** Estimated cost exceeds $141B, surpassing the legal threshold
- **Restructuring ongoing:** Expected to conclude by the end of 2026
- **Test flight delayed:** From 2026 to March 2028
- **Software issues** and silo design complicate timeline
- **Operational capability:** Not before early 2030s
- **Mitigant:** Congress added $2.5B for risk reduction activities

## Dividend and Shareholder Return

- **Annual dividend:** $9.24/share (~1.35% yield)
- **Consecutive years of increase:** 23
- **Last increase:** +12% in May 2025
- **Buybacks 2025:** ~$1.7B in repurchases
- **Current authorization:** $4.2B in pending repurchases
- **Strategy:** Return >100% of FCF to shareholders

## Competitive Advantages (Moat)

1. **B-21 Monopoly:** Sole manufacturer of next-generation stealth bomber
2. **#2 Pentagon Contractor:** Only behind Lockheed Martin
3. **$95.7B Backlog:** ~2.3x annual sales visibility
4. **Classified Programs:** Multiple "black" programs impossible to replicate
5. **Early Cycle Positioning:** B-21 and Sentinel are in investment phase — strong revenue comes in future years
6. **Leadership in Cybersecurity and C4ISR:** Critical capabilities for modern warfare

## The Defense Megatrend

- **NATO rises to 5% of GDP by 2035** — multiplies defense budgets in Europe
- **US defense up ~15% in FY2026** — NOC is a direct beneficiary
- **Active conflicts** (Ukraine, Middle East) accelerate demand
- **International sales growing 20%** — international book-to-bill >1 expected in 2026
- **New threats** (hypersonics, cyberattacks, drones) require exactly what NOC manufactures

## Catalysts

1. **B-21 Raider expansion** — $4.5B in additional capacity, production +25%, first operational plane in 2027
2. **Record backlog $95.7B** — visibility of ~2.3 years of revenue
3. **Accelerating free cash flow** — +26% in 2025, third year of 25%+ growth
4. **International sales +20%** — expanding market due to NATO 5%
5. **US defense budget +15%** — secular tailwind
6. **Classified programs** — upside not visible in public guidance

## Main Risks

1. **Sentinel ICBM in trouble:** $141B+ cost overruns, restructuring, 4-year delays
2. **Priced expensively:** Forward price vs. profit of 24.4x is premium for ~4% sales growth
3. **2026 EPS down vs 2025:** Guidance of $27.65 vs $29.08 achieved
4. **Dependence on defense budget:** Political changes or cuts could affect pipeline
5. **B-21 execution risk:** Massive program in ramp-up

## Competitive Position

| Metric | NOC | LMT | RTX | GD |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **Revenue** | $42B | $75B | $85B | $46B |
| **Backlog** | $95.7B | $194B | $217B | $92B |
| **Dividend Yield** | 1.35% | 2.26% | 2.2% | 2.0% |
| **P/E Forward** | 24.4x | 20.5x | ~22x | ~20x |
| **Defense focus** | 90% | 100% | 65% | 65% |

## Analyst Consensus

| Metric | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Rating | **Buy** (17 analysts) |
| Strong Buy | 5 |
| Buy | 5 |
| Hold | 7 |
| Sell | 0 |
| Average Price Target | **$675.29** |
| High Price Target | $815 |
| Low Price Target | $521 |
| **Upside to Target** | **-2.2%** |

## Conclusion

NOC at $691.43 is a **bet on the future of US air and space defense**. The B-21 Raider is the most important defense program of the next decade, and NOC is the sole manufacturer. With a record backlog of $95.7B, accelerating cash flow, and international sales growing 20%, the company has solid fundamentals. The main risk is the premium valuation (24.4x forward P/E) along with EPS dropping in 2026 due to capacity investments. However, these investments are what will drive future growth. For a diversified defense portfolio (complementing LMT), NOC offers unique exposure to stealth bombers, cybersecurity, and space systems.

---

*Research date: Mar 25, 2026 | Next review: Sep 2026*
*This is not financial advice.*
NOC.one_liner: Northrop Grumman makes planes and missiles for the US military, with a large backlog and fast-growing international sales.

Result: MOP price sustained at $340/t instead of collapsing to $250/t. (8) **Anchor: 8 billion people to feed by 2030**: USDA projects global crop demand +25% by 2030. Potash is input #2 (after N) that cannot be substituted — it's K (potassium), an essential element for photosynthesis. There is no chemical replacement. Nutrien has 6 Saskatchewan mines that produce for 75 years at current rates — an irreplicable geological moat. (9) **Stable crop prices Q1 2026**: corn $4.50/bu, wheat $6.80/bu, soybeans $11.50/bu, palm oil MYR 4,250. Healthy farmer income = sustained fertilizer demand. NOAA forecasts La Niña neutral in 2026 = normal planting conditions. (10) **One-line thesis**: buy the world's #1 potash producer + world's #1 ag retailer at forward P/E 12.5x when (a) Q1 2026 EBITDA +12% + EPS +18%, (b) India $375/t + China contract June = confirmed price floor, (c) Belarus/Russia sanctions structural tailwind, (d) 3.03% dividend + $1B buyback, (e) 8B people to feed in 2030 = permanent demographic demand.

If Belarus sanctions ease + Russia's Uralkali returns to the market or BHP's Jansen project in BC ramps faster than expected, oversupply could push prices below $300/t = NTR EPS -30%. BHP Jansen (4.4Mt initial scale in 2027, up to 8.5Mt by 2030) is the sector's sword of Damocles. (2) **Nitrogen's +22% YoY can reverse quickly**: dependent on China's export policy + natural gas prices. If China relaxes export quotas in H2 2026 + US natural gas prices collapse post-LNG export buildout, urea/UAN -15-25%. (3) **Retail margin is sensitive to farmer cash flow**: Q1 2026 proprietary GM +250bps is great but requires farmers to have cash. If crop prices collapse (corn <$3.50, soybeans <$9.00), farmers cut spending. Nutrien Ag Solutions retail revenue -8-12% in bear cycles (2019-2020 example). (4) **BHP Jansen ramp 2027-2030**: BHP is building Jansen mine in Saskatchewan — phase 1 capacity 4.4Mt in 2027, phase 2 4.1Mt by 2030. Total 8.5Mt = ~10% of the global market entering at once. Canpotex includes BHP too but pure additional capacity = pricing pressure from 2028 onwards. (5) **India / China negotiating power**: India Potash Limited + Chinese state buyers are monopsonists. If they form a buyer group + delay tenders, NTR loses leverage. India's 2025 tender was negotiated at $335 vs an initial offer of $400 — down 16% from the ask. (6) **Belarus/Russia sanctions eventually lift**: if geopolitical thaw (Ukraine ceasefire, partial sanctions relief), Belaruskali + Uralkali partially re-enter the market. ~3-5Mt of potash flooding back would be a -15-25% price hit. (7) **Ken Seitz CEO succession long-tail**: 25+ year potash veteran but 60+ years old. If he retires 2027-2028 without a prepared successor, transition risk. Pedro Farah (previous CFO) left in 2024. (8) **CAD/USD FX exposure**: NTR reports in USD but has significant Canadian operations. A strengthening CAD (BoC tightening, oil tailwind) = higher operating costs. Q1 2026 EBITDA +12% includes a -3% CAD drag. (9) **ESG/regulatory risk**: nitrogen + ammonia plants have a high CO2 footprint. EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment) could penalize nitrogen exports to Europe 2026-2030. Plus farmer pushback on fertilizer use (regenerative ag movement). (10) **Conglomerate discount: NTR trades at forward P/E 12.5x vs Mosaic 13x, Yara 16x**: if Seitz doesn't spin off retail, the discount persists. The retail business deserves a standalone P/E of 18x (recurring + capital-light) but combined with commodity potash, it trades at a lower multiple.

NU.summary_what:
Nu Holdings Ltd (NU) is **the largest 100% digital bank in Latin America** and, by number of customers, the largest fintech platform in the world outside China.

**Core product**: the **purple Nubank credit card** (#1 issuer of new cards in Brazil and Mexico), the **NuConta** digital account (no fees, no branches — receive your salary, transfer, pay), **Nu Crypto** (buy/sell bitcoin from the app), **NuInvest** (investing for beginners — funds, Brazilian CDs, stocks), insurance and personal loans.

**Markets**:
- **Brazil** (~75% of the business, 100M+ customers — more than Itaú, the largest traditional bank)
- **Mexico** (~15% of the adult population, leader in new card issuance)
- **Colombia** (4M+ customers, accelerating credit card expansion)

**Founders and control**: David Vélez (Colombian, ex-Sequoia, Stanford MBA), Cristina Junqueira (Brazilian, ex-Itaú), Edward Wible (American, ex-Boston Consulting). David Vélez remains **CEO — still founder-led**.

**Backers**: **Berkshire Hathaway (Warren Buffett), ~$1B invested since the 2021 IPO** — Buffett rarely touches fintech; NU is a notable exception. **Tencent** is also a major shareholder.

**Listing**: NYSE under ticker NU since December 2021. **Class A ordinary shares** (Cayman Islands holding company; operations in Brazil/Mexico/Colombia).

**Q4 2025/FY2025 (reported Feb 25, 2026)**:
- FY2025 revenue: **$16.3B (+45% YoY)**
- FY2025 net income: **$2.9B (+45% YoY)**
- Q4'25 revenue: **$4.9B (beat the $4.55B consensus)**
- **Record 33% ROE** — the highest of any large bank globally
- **131M customers worldwide** (+17M net in 2025)

**HQ**: São Paulo, Brazil. **~13,000 employees**. **Next earnings**: May 14, 2026 after the close.
NU.summary_why: NU at $14.50 means buying the most profitable bank in the world (33% ROE) growing 45% a year, at a forward P/E of 12.6x — half the valuation the market pays for JPMorgan/BAC (P/E 14-16x with 6-8% growth). Specific reasons: (1) **33% ROE is a global record for any large bank**: JPMorgan 17%, BAC 11%, Citi 7%, HSBC 13%. NU generates $33 of net profit for every $100 of its own capital — more efficient than any Wall Street giant, with a growth rate 5-7x higher. That growth + ROE combination doesn't exist in any other listed bank. (2) **131M customers (+17M net in 2025)** = more than the population of Mexico and almost as many as Japan. In Mexico NU serves 15% of the adult population and is #1 in new card issuance; in Colombia, 4M+ customers with an expanding credit portfolio. The Mexican business alone could be worth $20B+ as a standalone. (3) **U.S. OCC charter conditionally approved January 29, 2026** (after a 121-day review) — NU can operate as a U.S. national bank with insured deposits, lending and digital-asset custody. Plan: hubs in Miami, the SF Bay Area, Virginia and North Carolina. Full capitalization within 12 months, opening within 18. **The U.S. opens a >$3 trillion market vs. LatAm's $400B** — NU becomes a global bank, not just a LATAM bank. (4) **R$45B (~$8.2B) of investment in Brazil in 2026** = nearly 2x the level of two years ago. Management is flooring the accelerator on high-value customer cohorts (NuConta payroll, NuInvest, larger-ticket credit). (5) **Q1 2026 earnings May 14 (after the close, next week)**: consensus revenue $5.06-5.10B, GAAP EPS $0.19, normalized EPS $0.21. NU has beaten revenue consensus for 8 straight quarters. If it beats and guides well, a >5% gap up is likely; if it disappoints, a chance to add at better prices. (6) **Berkshire Hathaway $1B+ since 2021** = validation from Buffett (who rarely invests in fintech) plus Tencent as a shareholder. Very stable insider holdings. (7) **Record efficiency**: cost-to-serve of $0.95/month per active customer vs. $30+ at traditional Brazilian banks. Every new customer is margin-positive from month one. (8) **Forward P/E of 12.6x with 45% growth** = a PEG below 0.3 — extremely cheap. Banks growing at that rate have historically traded at 2-4x that valuation. (9) **Analyst consensus Moderate Buy, average target $18.26** = +25.9% upside vs. $14.50. (10) **24% off the 52-week high of $18.98**: the market punished NU for Brazilian real weakness in Q1 2026, but the business grew 45% in constant dollars — the pullback is FX, not fundamentals.
NU.summary_risk: The risks are specifically measurable: (1) **Brazilian real / FX = ~75% of the business**: when the BRL weakens vs. the USD, earnings reported in dollars shrink even if the business grows in local currency. In 2025 the BRL fell ~20% vs. the USD; NU absorbed the hit in Q1 2026. If the BRL drops another 10-15% (plausible if Brazil hikes interest rates aggressively or if Trump imposes tariffs on Brazil), USD-reported revenue could flatten or fall even with 40% local-currency growth. (2) **Brazilian interest rates (Selic)**: the Selic sits at ~14.25% (May 2026), among the highest in the world. If NU raises customer rates to protect its spread, net interest margin benefits — but defaults rise too. 90+ day NPLs (loans gone unpaid) rose to 6.1% in Q4 2025 — manageable, but the trajectory matters. (3) **Mexico = the most competitive market**: BBVA, Santander, Banamex, MercadoPago, Stori — all fighting for the same unbanked customer. NU leads in new cards but Mexican unit economics aren't profitable yet (a -$15-20M operating loss in Mexico FY2025). Path to MX profitability = 2027-28. (4) **The OCC U.S. charter is conditional**: it requires full capitalization within 12 months plus specific non-public conditions. If the Federal Reserve or FDIC add extra hurdles (enhanced KYC, stricter capital requirements), the U.S. launch slips from 18 months to 24-30. (5) **Q1 2026 earnings May 14 = a binary catalyst**: a beat ($5.10B+ revenue, ROE >32%) likely gaps the stock up; a miss likely means a -10-15% correction (the stock is already -24% off the high). (6) **Founder concentration in David Vélez**: founder-led is a positive but also a single-point risk. Vélez holds control via dual-class shares — if he leaves or his role gets diluted, governance changes. (7) **Crypto exposure post-OCC**: the charter includes digital-asset custody. If crypto collapses (post-2025-26 Bitcoin halving cycle), Nu Crypto could lose profitability/customers. (8) **MercadoPago competition**: across LATAM, MELI's MercadoPago is a direct competitor (also 50M+ financial users, high ROE). If MercadoPago accelerates, NU loses share. (9) **Brazil's government (Lula)**: Brazilian banking regulation could tighten — the Central Bank capped revolving credit-card rates in 2024; another regulatory round would hit margins. (10) **Valuation if growth slows**: if net adds fall from 17M/year to 10M/year, the forward P/E "normalizes" toward the sector average (~10x) → roughly 20% downside.
